name: Home
title: Home
description: Landing page that surfaces the organization’s mission, focus areas, key programs, featured conferences, events, and publications, and provides quick entry points to account-based tools and other sections of the site.

core_features: Load and display organization mission, focus areas, and key programs using getHomepageContent.
Provide a global search form that routes users to publications.html or researchers.html with a query URL parameter based on the selected search target.
Display featured conferences and link each to conference_detail.html with an id URL parameter, with an additional call-to-action to view all conferences.
Display featured events/workshops and link each to event_detail.html with an id URL parameter, with an additional call-to-action to view all events/workshops.
Display recent publications and link each to publication_detail.html with an id URL parameter, highlighting open access status when available.
Show a compact My Account summary (saved schedule, bookmarked conferences, reading list, followed researchers, next upcoming session) using getMyAccountSummary, with navigation to my_account.html.
Provide navigation to membership.html and donate.html via concise call-to-action buttons.
Offer a compact newsletter subscription widget that collects an email and redirects to newsletters.html with the email URL parameter populated.
user_workflows:
Discover upcoming conferences:
1) Page loads and calls getHomepageHighlights.
2) User scrolls to the featured conferences section.
3) Featured conferences are listed from getHomepageHighlights.featuredConferences.
4) User selects a specific conference card; on click, browser navigates to conference_detail.html?id=<Conference.id>.
5) Alternatively, user selects the general conferences call-to-action; on click, browser navigates to conferences.html without additional parameters.

Explore events and workshops:
1) Page loads and calls getHomepageHighlights.
2) User scrolls to the featured events/workshops section.
3) Featured events are listed from getHomepageHighlights.featuredEvents.
4) User selects an event card; on click, browser navigates to event_detail.html?id=<Event.id>.
5) If the user wants a broader list, they click the events call-to-action; on click, browser navigates to events.html.

Find publications or researchers via global search:
1) User enters a keyword in the global search input (homepageSearchQuery internal state).
2) User selects the target type (e.g., publications or researchers) within the search form.
3) User submits the form.
4) If target is publications, browser navigates to publications.html?query=<encoded homepageSearchQuery>.
5) If target is researchers, browser navigates to researchers.html?query=<encoded homepageSearchQuery>.

Review mission, focus areas, and programs:
1) Page loads and calls getHomepageContent.
2) Mission blurb and high-level description are rendered in the header/content area.
3) User scrolls through the list of focus areas derived from getHomepageContent.focusAreas.
4) User optionally clicks a focus area; the page constructs a URL to publications.html?query=<encoded focus area identifier or title> to explore publications related to that focus area.
5) User scrolls through key programs from getHomepageContent.keyPrograms for a concise overview of the organization’s activities.

Use personalized My Account summary:
1) Page loads and calls getMyAccountSummary (if the user context allows; otherwise, the component handles empty or zero-valued results).
2) The summary widget displays counts for upcomingSessionsCount, bookmarkedConferencesCount, readingListCount, and followedResearchersCount.
3) If nextUpcomingSession is present, the widget shows information from nextUpcomingSession.session and nextUpcomingSession.conference.
4) User clicks the My Account button; browser navigates to my_account.html for detailed management of schedule, bookmarks, and reading list.

Navigate to membership and donation sections:
1) User scrolls to the membership/donation navigation strip.
2) User clicks the membership call-to-action; browser navigates to membership.html.
3) Alternatively, user clicks the donation call-to-action; browser navigates to donate.html for creating a Donation record.

Subscribe to newsletters:
1) User enters an email address in the newsletter subscription widget.
2) User submits the form.
3) The widget validates that an email value is present.
4) On success, browser navigates to newsletters.html?email=<encoded email>, enabling the newsletters page to prefill the subscription form for creating or updating NewsletterSubscription entities.
interactions: On initial page load, trigger parallel calls to getHomepageContent, getHomepageHighlights, and getMyAccountSummary; render loading states until responses are available.
Clicking a featured conference card navigates to conference_detail.html with a URL parameter id set to the associated Conference.id from getHomepageHighlights.featuredConferences.
Clicking the conferences call-to-action navigates to conferences.html without additional parameters, allowing that page to manage its own filters and listing.
Clicking a featured event/workshop card navigates to event_detail.html with a URL parameter id set to the associated Event.id from getHomepageHighlights.featuredEvents.
Clicking the events call-to-action navigates to events.html without additional parameters.
Clicking a recent publication title navigates to publication_detail.html with a URL parameter id set to the Publication.id from getHomepageHighlights.recentPublications.
Submitting the global search form with the publications target selected navigates to publications.html?query=<encoded homepageSearchQuery>.
Submitting the global search form with the researchers target selected navigates to researchers.html?query=<encoded homepageSearchQuery>.
Clicking a focus area item triggers navigation to publications.html?query=<encoded focus area value derived from getHomepageContent.focusAreas>.
Clicking the My Account button in the account summary widget navigates to my_account.html.
Clicking the membership button navigates to membership.html.
Clicking the donation button navigates to donate.html.
Submitting the newsletter widget form navigates to newsletters.html?email=<encoded email input value>.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not rely on incoming URL parameters; it loads a fresh state on each visit and uses only the outputs of getHomepageContent, getHomepageHighlights, and getMyAccountSummary. Page-level state consists of: (1) asynchronous loading states and error flags for each interface call, (2) the current value of the homepageSearchQuery and the selected target (publications or researchers) in the global search form, and (3) the current value of the newsletter email input. None of this state is persisted via URL parameters on the Home page itself. URL parameters are used exclusively when navigating away from Home: conference_detail.html, event_detail.html, and publication_detail.html receive an id parameter corresponding to the selected item’s identifier; publications.html and researchers.html receive a query parameter when the global search form is submitted; newsletters.html receives an email parameter from the newsletter widget. These parameters define the state of the destination pages (e.g., prefilled searches or forms) but do not affect the rendering logic of the Home page once navigation occurs.

components.functionality: Displays the primary textual introduction for the Home page, including the main title, subtitle, and a concise mission blurb loaded from getHomepageContent. This provides high-level context about the organization’s mission and activities without additional promotional elements.

Renders a structured list of the organization’s focus areas. Each focus area shows its title and description from getHomepageContent. Selecting a focus area initiates navigation to publications.html with a query parameter derived from that focus area, enabling users to quickly see related publications.
event_handlers: onClickFocusAreaItem: Navigate to publications.html?query=<encoded focus area code or title from getHomepageContent.focusAreas[]>

components.functionality: Displays a list of key programs supported by the organization using getHomepageContent.keyPrograms. Each program entry shows its name and summary to provide a quick overview of organizational activities.

Provides a single input field where users can enter a keyword (homepageSearchQuery) and choose whether to search publications or researchers. On submission, the form redirects the user to the appropriate listing page with the query included as a URL parameter.
event_handlers: onChangeSearchTarget: Update internal search target state between publications and researchers.
onSubmit: If target is publications, navigate to publications.html?query=<encoded homepageSearchQuery>; if target is researchers, navigate to researchers.html?query=<encoded homepageSearchQuery>.

components.functionality: Uses getHomepageHighlights.featuredConferences to render a set of prominent conferences with their associated metadata and highlight text. Each card is clickable and leads to the detailed conference page. A separate call-to-action allows users to navigate to the general conferences listing.
event_handlers: onClickConferenceCard: Navigate to conference_detail.html?id=<Conference.id from the selected featuredConferences item>.

components.functionality: Displays featured events and workshops from getHomepageHighlights.featuredEvents. Each event card shows core information such as title, type, and schedule, plus any highlight text. Clicking an event navigates to its detail page, and a separate call-to-action links to the main events listing.
event_handlers: onClickEventCard: Navigate to event_detail.html?id=<Event.id from the selected featuredEvents item>.

components.functionality: Lists recent publications using getHomepageHighlights.recentPublications. Each item presents key publication metadata and indicates open access status based on the isOpenAccess flag. Clicking a publication title navigates to its detailed view.
event_handlers: onClickPublicationTitle: Navigate to publication_detail.html?id=<Publication.id from the selected recentPublications item>.

components.functionality: Provides a compact overview of the user’s personalized content based on getMyAccountSummary, including counts of upcoming sessions, bookmarked conferences, reading list items, and followed researchers, plus a preview of the next upcoming session when available. Offers a direct navigation link to the full account page.
event_handlers: onInitialLoad: Request getMyAccountSummary and render a loading state until complete.
onClickNextSessionRow: Optionally navigate to a relevant conference_detail.html?id=<Conference.id> if deep linking is desired via the conference reference.

components.functionality: Provides two clear navigation actions for users who want to learn about membership or provide financial support. This component is purely navigational and does not fetch or display dynamic data.

Captures an email address and redirects the user to the newsletters page with that email prefilled via a URL parameter, enabling configuration of NewsletterSubscription entries. Includes basic validation (e.g., non-empty input) before navigation.
data_binding: NewsletterSubscription.email (as a future binding on newsletters.html)
event_handlers: onChangeEmailInput: Update internal email value state.
onSubmitNewsletterForm: If email value is non-empty, navigate to newsletters.html?email=<encoded email>; otherwise, display a validation message within the component.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strict top-to-bottom, single-column flow beneath the existing full-width site header (info bar + navbar). All home page components sit in one centered content column, in this order: page-header-block at the top, followed immediately by the global-search-form, then focus-areas-list, key-programs-list, featured-conferences-list, featured-events-list, recent-publications-list, my-account-summary-widget, support-and-membership-cta-strip, and finally the newsletter-subscribe-widget near the bottom. No sidebars or multi-column page layouts; any internal grids (e.g., for conferences/events) are constrained within this central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strict top-to-bottom, single-column flow beneath the existing full-width site header (info bar + navbar). All home page components sit in one centered content column, in this order: page-header-block at the top, followed immediately by the global-search-form, then focus-areas-list, key-programs-list, featured-conferences-list, featured-events-list, recent-publications-list, my-account-summary-widget, support-and-membership-cta-strip, and finally the newsletter-subscribe-widget near the bottom. No sidebars or multi-column page layouts; any internal grids (e.g., for conferences/events) are constrained within this central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header (info bar + nav) occupies the top ~15–20% of the viewport as in the mockup. The remaining ~80–85% is a white content area with a relatively narrow, centered column (~55–65% of the page width on desktop, or a fixed max-width around 960px). Each major component forms its own distinct block within this column, separated by generous vertical spacing (roughly 80–120px of white space between sections). Only two elements intentionally break the central column pattern: the support-and-membership-cta-strip and the newsletter-subscribe-widget, which use full-width horizontal bands echoing the header bands, but still keep their text centered on the page.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low content density overall: short blocks of text, restrained use of imagery, and plenty of white space around and within each component. Section headings are brief and centered, with line spacing and section padding slightly more generous than typical modern sites. Lists (focus areas, programs, conferences, events, publications) show only a small curated subset on the home page, not exhaustive lists, to preserve the calm, uncluttered feel. Internal layouts favor 1–2 columns at most, with minimal visual embellishment and no dense card stacks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is drawn first to the dark header band at the top (existing site chrome), then naturally drops into the white area where the page-header-block sits, centered and visually dominant. From there, the global search box immediately below becomes the primary interaction point. The flow then becomes a straightforward vertical scan down the central column: first foundational understanding (focus-areas-list, key-programs-list), then timely items (featured-conferences-list, featured-events-list, recent-publications-list), then personalized/account actions (my-account-summary-widget), and finally supporting engagement CTAs in horizontal bands (support-and-membership-cta-strip and newsletter-subscribe-widget). Section headings and generous vertical spacing create a clear stepping-stone rhythm down the page.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing dark site header, the home page opens into a large, bright white field with a single, narrow column of content centered on the screen. At the top of this column, the organization’s name and mission summary sit as a calm, text-centric page header with lots of breathing room above and below, echoing the restrained feel of the mockup’s ‘Jobs’ heading. Directly underneath, a wide but shallow global search bar stretches across most of the content column, clearly visible but not flashy. As you scroll down, you encounter a sequence of well-separated sections: focus areas, key programs, curated lists of conferences, events, and publications, each introduced by a centered heading and modest descriptive text. The visual impression remains one of a large white canvas with discrete, compact islands of text; there are no sidebars and only simple internal grids. Near the mid-lower portion of the page, a full-width, low-height band with a subtle background tint invites users to explore support and membership options, visually mirroring the header’s horizontal banding while maintaining a conservative academic tone. Just above the footer, another understated full-width band hosts the newsletter subscription widget, again keeping fields centered and surrounded by whitespace. Throughout, the user’s eye moves in a simple downward path, always returning to the center of the page, guided by high-contrast headings and deliberate vertical spacing rather than decorative graphics.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned immediately below the site’s black/nav header, the page-header-block begins roughly 10–15% of the viewport height below the bottom of the navigation bar, reinforcing the impression of a large white buffer. It occupies the full width of the central content column (about 55–65% of the page width on desktop, centered horizontally). Within this block, the main page title (e.g., the organization name or 'Welcome') is center-aligned and sits near the top of the block, with a generous top margin from the nav (approximately the height of 2–3 line-heights). A short mission statement or tagline follows beneath, also center-aligned, with comfortable line spacing and ample white space around it. The overall height of this block is modest compared to the white space above and below, so it reads as a compact text cluster floating in a larger white field, very similar in visual weight to the 'Jobs' heading region in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the normal document flow, anchored at the top of the main content area beneath the fixed/standard header. It does not scroll independently or stick; its prominence comes from its position and whitespace.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly below the page-header-block, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 48–64px, the global-search-form spans nearly the full width of the central content column (about 80–90% of that column width, still centered). The search input is a single horizontal bar with a submit button aligned to its right, both centered within the column. The search field’s height is slightly taller than standard text inputs to give it presence, but it remains visually simple—no heavy drop shadows, just a subtle border or understated background. There is generous white space above and below the search form, making it a clear focal point without crowding surrounding elements. A small, center-aligned helper text (e.g., 'Search research, conferences, and publications') can sit immediately above or below the field, but kept to a single line to maintain the low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow. On scroll, it moves with the page; no sticky behavior. It adjusts to a full-width stacked layout on small screens but remains centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The focus-areas-list appears below the global-search-form, after a vertical gap of approximately 80–96px to preserve the sense of separate, calm sections. This component stays within the same centered column width (about 55–65% of page width). At the top of the block, a short, center-aligned heading such as 'Research Focus Areas' introduces the section, followed by a single-line or two-line description also center-aligned. Below, the focus areas are listed in a simple text-forward layout. On desktop, use a maximum of two columns within this central block: either 1–2 focus areas per row with left-aligned labels, each item separated by generous vertical spacing (e.g., 16–24px). Icons are either omitted or kept extremely minimal and small. The overall impression is of a tidy list of key themes, not a dense grid. Bottom padding of about 48–64px separates this list from the next section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky block. Internally, items flow top-to-bottom (and optionally into a second column on larger screens) but remain constrained by the central content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Placed directly below the focus-areas-list with a visible white gap of about 64–80px, the key-programs-list uses the same central column width and alignment. A centered section heading (e.g., 'Key Programs') anchors the block. Underneath, programs are listed vertically as discrete rows; each program line may contain a program name (left-aligned within the column) and an optional one-line descriptor beneath or beside it, but all typography remains simple and text-led. Limit the visible count to a small number (e.g., 3–5 programs) to keep density low, with ample vertical spacing between items (at least 24px). The section feels like a structured but light-weight set of lines within a large white zone, not a compact menu. Bottom margin mirrors the top spacing to maintain rhythm before moving into the more time-sensitive content sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked block within the central flow. No special behavior; adapts to single-column on all viewports while retaining central positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Following the key-programs-list, after a gap of roughly 80–96px, the featured-conferences-list marks the transition into more dynamic, time-bound content. This block still sits entirely within the central column, with a center-aligned heading ('Featured Conferences') at the top and possibly a short supporting line (one sentence max). The conferences themselves are arranged in a very light grid that respects the conservative style: on desktop, use either a single column of horizontally separated items or, at most, a two-column layout with each conference tile being mostly textual (title, date, location) and minimal visual decoration. Each conference entry uses clear line breaks and breathing room, separated from the next by 24–32px of vertical spacing. The grid does not extend to the page edges; it remains comfortably inset within the central column, preserving wide left and right margins. The block ends with generous bottom padding before the events section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block with internal grid behavior. Items reflow to a single column on smaller screens while staying centered within the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Located directly beneath the featured-conferences-list with a vertical gap similar to previous transitions (around 64–80px), the featured-events-list mirrors the conferences layout for consistency. It stays within the central content column and begins with a center-aligned heading ('Upcoming Events' or similar). The events are displayed in a simple, lightly structured list or a conservative two-column grid; each event entry shows title, date/time, and possibly format (online/in-person), all in text form with minimal styling. Entries are separated by ample vertical spacing to avoid crowding. If both sections are visually similar, you can slightly reduce the number of events shown to keep the page from feeling list-heavy. The entire block reads as a modest cluster of event information floating in the white space, not a dense calendar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static. Internally, the grid or list layout gracefully collapses to a single column on narrow screens while maintaining centralized alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After another spacious gap (approximately 80–96px) below the featured-events-list, the recent-publications-list appears as a text-heavy but still low-density section. It occupies the same central column width and starts with a center-aligned heading ('Recent Publications') and an optional brief description (e.g., 'Selected recent articles and reports'). The list itself is vertically stacked, each item comprising a publication title (left-aligned for readability, possibly bold), optional authors on the next line, and a short metadata line (e.g., journal, year). Limit visible items (e.g., 3–6) to maintain a sense of curation. Between each item, add noticeable vertical spacing (24–32px), and between this block and the next component, maintain substantial whitespace (about 80–96px). The design avoids thumbnails or complex card treatments; it is essentially a well-spaced textual bibliography embedded in a wide white area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block of text entries, flowing top to bottom. No sticky behavior; responsive behavior is mainly font-size and line-length adjustments while staying in the center column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-account-summary-widget sits below the recent-publications-list, separated by roughly 96px of vertical space to signal a shift from general content to personalized tools. It remains within the central column but may be visually grouped by a very subtle, light-gray background rectangle or a thin border around the widget content, creating a compact 'card' that still feels conservative. The width of this card is slightly narrower than the content column (e.g., 80–90% of the column width) and centered to emphasize its separateness. Inside, a short, center-aligned heading ('My Account') appears at the top of the card, with key summary lines or links arranged in a simple vertical stack, left-aligned. Padding inside the card is generous (24–32px on all sides), and the card itself sits in a substantial field of white, with clear white margins above and below. This keeps the widget noticeable but not visually heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element, not fixed or sticky. It behaves like a centered card within the vertical flow and collapses naturally to full-width on small screens, maintaining its inset padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Just below the my-account-summary-widget, after a moderate gap of about 64–80px, the support-and-membership-cta-strip breaks the single-column pattern by extending across the full browser width, much like the black header band but with a much lighter, non-distracting background (e.g., very light gray or a subdued brand tint). Vertically, this strip is relatively slim—around 15–20% of the viewport height at most—but has enough height to comfortably house 1–2 short lines of text and one or two call-to-action links or buttons. The content inside the strip is still constrained to the same central max-width as the rest of the page; it is horizontally centered, with the main call-to-action text center-aligned. The text might say 'Support Our Work' with a secondary line 'Learn about membership opportunities', and one or two conservative-style buttons or text links beneath or beside it. Generous padding above and below the text ensures the strip feels like a calm horizontal band, not an aggressive promo banner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width horizontal band in the document flow. It does not stick to the top or bottom; instead, it scrolls with the page. On small screens, its content remains centered and stacked vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Near the bottom of the page, below the support-and-membership-cta-strip with a vertical gap of approximately 64–96px, the newsletter-subscribe-widget provides a final engagement point. It can either sit as a standalone centered block within the white page background or as a very subtle full-width strip with barely distinguishable background shading. In either case, the content is constrained to the central column and centered horizontally. A short, center-aligned heading ('Stay Informed' or 'Newsletter Subscription') appears at the top, followed by a single line of explanatory text. Beneath, one or two form fields (typically email and possibly affiliation) are laid out in a single horizontal row on desktop (spanning roughly 70–80% of the column width) but stack vertically on mobile. The form fields are moderately sized with generous horizontal padding and spacing between them, leaving ample white space to the left and right. The submit button is aligned to the right of the email field (in the same row) or centered below when stacked. Extra whitespace below the widget separates it from the page footer, maintaining the overall airy, uncluttered impression.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the main content flow. The fields respond to viewport size but remain centered; no floating or sticky behavior.

name: Conferences & Calls
title: Conferences & Calls
description: Search, filter, and browse academic conferences organized or supported by the organization, with URL-driven state and navigation to detailed conference pages.

core_features: Load dynamic search options (regions, attendee types, topic suggestions, supported currencies, sort options) via getConferenceSearchOptions and bind them to search and filter controls.
Execute conference searches via searchConferences using keyword query, region, date range, topic, attendee type, fee filters, and sort order, with paging.

Synchronize all search criteria and paging state with URL parameters (query, region, startDate, endDate, topic, attendeeType, maxFee, currency, sort, page, pageSize) so that the page can be shared and reloaded consistently.

Display a paginated list of conferences with key details from each searchConferences result (conference fields, primary topics, locationDisplay, cheapest fee for the selected attendee type) and navigation to conference_detail.html.
Provide interactive filter management (apply, modify, clear individual filters, clear all) that updates both the URL and the search results.
Allow users to change sort order based on sortOptions returned by getConferenceSearchOptions and re-run searchConferences accordingly.
Allow users to navigate to contact.html with topic=conferences for support related to conferences and calls.
Respect pre-filtered access from other pages by reading incoming URL parameters and initializing controls and the first searchConferences call accordingly.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open the page directly and search by keyword
1) User navigates to conferences.html without URL parameters.
2) Page calls getConferenceSearchOptions.
3) Once options load, page initializes default sort and default page/pageSize from system configuration (not hardcoded in this design).
4) URL-state manager reads URL parameters; with no search parameters present, it initializes an empty search state.
5) User types a keyword into the search query input; optional autocomplete suggestions are shown using topicSuggestions from getConferenceSearchOptions.
6) User submits the search form.
7) URL-state manager updates the URL to include query and resets page to 1.
8) Page calls searchConferences with query, current filters (if any), sort, page, and pageSize.
9) Results list displays conferences from the searchConferences response; pagination and result count are updated.

Workflow: Follow a pre-filtered link from index.html (e.g., region and date range)
1) User clicks a link on index.html that points to conferences.html with URL parameters such as region, startDate, endDate, and optionally topic, attendeeType, maxFee, currency, sort, query.
2) On load, page calls getConferenceSearchOptions.
3) URL-state manager parses the incoming parameters and sets the corresponding filter control values (region select, date range, topic input, attendee type, fee filters, currency, sort, query) to match the URL.
4) Once options are loaded and initial state derived from the URL, page calls searchConferences with query (if present), filters (constructed from the URL parameters), sort (from URL or default), page (from URL or default), and pageSize (from URL or default configuration).
5) Results list shows only conferences that match the pre-filtered criteria.
6) User may further adjust filters or query; each change updates the URL and triggers a new searchConferences call.

Workflow: Filter by attendee type and maximum registration fee
1) User lands on conferences.html (either directly or via a link including attendeeType and maxFee parameters).
2) getConferenceSearchOptions provides available attendeeTypes and supportedCurrencies.
3) Filter controls show the attendee type selector and fee inputs, initialized from URL if those parameters are present.
4) User selects an attendee type and enters a maximum fee amount and chooses a currency.
5) User applies filters (via an Apply filters action or equivalent).
6) URL-state manager updates attendeeType, maxFee, and currency URL parameters and resets page to 1.
7) Page calls searchConferences with filters including attendeeType and fee (derived from attendeeType, maxFee, currency), along with query and other filters if set.
8) Results list displays conferences that have at least one ConferenceFee matching the selected attendee type with an amount less than or equal to maxFee in the specified currency (as implemented server-side). The cheapestFeeForSelectedAttendeeType field is displayed for each result.

Workflow: Change sort order and page through results
1) After an initial search, the user sees a list of conference results along with totalCount, current page, and pageSize from searchConferences.
2) User opens the sort control and selects a new sort option whose code matches one of the sortOptions codes from getConferenceSearchOptions.
3) URL-state manager updates the sort parameter in the URL and resets page to 1.
4) Page calls searchConferences with the existing query and filters but with the new sort value.
5) User uses pagination controls (next/previous or a specific page number).
6) URL-state manager updates the page parameter in the URL.
7) Page calls searchConferences again with the same query, filters, and sort but with the new page value.
8) Results list updates to show conferences for the selected page.

Workflow: Open conference details from the result list
1) User reviews the conference results list.
2) User clicks on a conference title or an associated 'View details' link.
3) Browser navigates to conference_detail.html with a URL parameter id set to the id field of the clicked conference (id = conference.id from the searchConferences result).
4) conference_detail.html handles detailed display and bookmarking based on its own assigned interfaces (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Contact the organization about conferences
1) User scrolls to a support or help area on conferences.html.
2) User clicks a link such as 'Contact us about conferences'.
3) Browser navigates to contact.html with a URL parameter topic set to 'conferences'.
4) contact.html uses the topic parameter to preselect or prefill the subject of the contact form.

Workflow: Use browser back/forward navigation with preserved state
1) User modifies filters, search query, sort, and page, causing URL parameters to be updated each time by the URL-state manager.
2) User clicks the browser back button.
3) Browser changes the URL to a previous state; URL-state manager detects URL changes (e.g., using history/popstate events).
4) Page re-derives search state from the new URL and calls searchConferences with the parameters represented in the URL.
5) Controls and results list are updated to match the historical state, without using localStorage.
interactions: Typing in the search input: Every change updates an internal query state; submitting the form triggers a URL update (query and page) and a searchConferences call.

Selecting a region in the filter panel: User chooses a region option sourced from getConferenceSearchOptions.regions; upon applying filters, region is written to the URL and used inside the filters argument for searchConferences.

Adjusting date range: User sets a start date and/or end date; on apply, startDate and endDate parameters are updated in the URL, and a searchConferences call is made with a dateRange filter constructed from those values.

Choosing a topic filter: User types or selects a topic, potentially assisted by topicSuggestions from getConferenceSearchOptions; when applied, the topic parameter is written to the URL and included in filters for searchConferences.

Changing attendee type and fee constraints: User selects an attendee type from getConferenceSearchOptions.attendeeTypes, sets maxFee, and optionally chooses a currency from supportedCurrencies; applying filters updates attendeeType, maxFee, and currency in the URL and uses them in the filters object for searchConferences.

Modifying sort order: User selects a sort option whose code comes from getConferenceSearchOptions.sortOptions; sort is stored in the URL, page is reset to 1, and searchConferences is called with the new sort code.

Paginating results: User clicks next, previous, or a specific page number; the page parameter is updated in the URL, and searchConferences is called with the new page value while retaining query, filters, and sort.

Clearing filters: User removes individual filters via active filter chips or selects a clear-all action; relevant parameters (region, startDate, endDate, topic, attendeeType, maxFee, currency) are removed from the URL; searchConferences is re-called with the updated filters.
Opening conference details: Clicking a conference title or 'View details' triggers navigation to conference_detail.html with id equal to the conference.id from the searchConferences result item.
Contacting support: Clicking a contact link navigates to contact.html with topic=conferences, without affecting the search state on conferences.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by URL parameters and live interface responses, without using localStorage. On initial load, the page calls getConferenceSearchOptions to retrieve regions, attendeeTypes, topicSuggestions, supportedCurrencies, and sortOptions. In parallel or immediately afterward, a URL-state manager parses the current URL for query, region, startDate, endDate, topic, attendeeType, maxFee, currency, sort, page, and pageSize parameters. These URL parameters determine the initial values for search and filter controls. If some parameters are missing, internal defaults are applied (for example, default sort code and default page/pageSize from configuration external to this design), but those defaults are not hardcoded here. Once options and URL-derived state are available, the page constructs a filters object for searchConferences (containing values derived from region, date range, topic, attendeeType, and fee-related fields) and calls searchConferences with query (string or empty), filters (object), sort (string from URL or default), page (number from URL or default), and pageSize (number from URL or default). Whenever the user changes query, filters, or sort, the URL-state manager updates the corresponding URL parameters and resets page to 1, then triggers a new searchConferences call using the updated state. Pagination updates only the page (and optionally pageSize) URL parameters and re-calls searchConferences with the same query, filters, and sort. Any browser navigation that changes the URL (back/forward, manual edits, or pre-filtered links from other pages) is handled by re-parsing the URL, recomputing the derived search state, updating controls, and re-invoking searchConferences. Thus the URL is the single source of truth for the search state, and all filters, sort, and pagination are reproducible via URL alone.

components.functionality: Fetches search configuration options (regions, attendee types, topic suggestions, supported currencies, sort options) when the page loads and makes them available to other components.

Parses URL parameters into a normalized search state (query, filters, sort, page, pageSize), updates the URL when controls change, and triggers searchConferences calls whenever the state changes or the URL changes (e.g., via back/forward).

Provides a text input for keyword-based conference search. Initializes the input from the query URL parameter and, upon submission, updates the URL and triggers a new search via url-state-manager and searchConferences. Optionally displays autocomplete or suggestion behavior using topicSuggestions from getConferenceSearchOptions.

Manages filters for region, date range, topic, attendee type, max fee, and currency. Initializes each control from the corresponding URL parameters (region, startDate, endDate, topic, attendeeType, maxFee, currency). On applying filters, it passes updated criteria to the url-state-manager, which updates URL parameters and triggers searchConferences.

Displays the currently active filters (region, date range, topic, attendee type, fee, currency) based on the URL-derived search state. Allows the user to remove individual filters or clear all filters. Removal or clearing triggers URL updates and corresponding searchConferences calls via the url-state-manager.

Provides a dropdown or selection control for sort options and controls for pagination (current page display, total results, navigation between pages). Initializes sort from the sort URL parameter and page/pageSize from their URL parameters. On change, it updates the URL and triggers searchConferences through the url-state-manager.

Displays a list of conferences returned from the latest searchConferences call. For each result item, it shows key conference information (using the conference entity fields such as title, status, startDate, endDate, region, locationDisplay), primaryTopics, and the cheapestFeeForSelectedAttendeeType if present. Each item provides interactions to open the conference detail page.

Handles navigation when a user selects a conference in the results list. It receives the conference.id from the clicked item and navigates to conference_detail.html with the id URL parameter set to that value.

Indicates the loading state while searchConferences is in progress and displays error messages if the interface call fails. Optionally provides a retry action that re-invokes searchConferences with the current search state from the URL.

When searchConferences returns a totalCount of zero for the current query and filters, displays a functional message and suggests actions such as modifying filters or clearing them. Uses the current URL-state to determine which filters are active and can provide a clear-all action via active-filters-summary.
Displays a functional link for users who want to contact the organization regarding conferences. Clicking the link navigates to contact.html with the topic parameter set to conferences.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: All interactive elements are arranged in a single, centered vertical column beneath the existing full-width header bands. The page starts with a centered 'Conferences & Calls' heading, followed by a short explanatory line (implicit within the search area), then a prominent search bar. Filters and active filter indicators sit directly below the search bar as stacked blocks, followed by sort/pagination controls and then the conference results list. Status messages (loading, errors, no results) replace the results area when needed. A centered contact panel closes the page at the bottom, mirroring the jobs page’s final email CTA.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All interactive elements are arranged in a single, centered vertical column beneath the existing full-width header bands. The page starts with a centered 'Conferences & Calls' heading, followed by a short explanatory line (implicit within the search area), then a prominent search bar. Filters and active filter indicators sit directly below the search bar as stacked blocks, followed by sort/pagination controls and then the conference results list. Status messages (loading, errors, no results) replace the results area when needed. A centered contact panel closes the page at the bottom, mirroring the jobs page’s final email CTA.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The existing dark header (info bar + navbar) occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The main content column below is relatively narrow, spanning about 50–60% of the viewport width and centered, with generous left and right margins. The top of the content area has a substantial vertical buffer before the page title, and similarly large vertical spacing separates each major block (search, filters, results, contact). The results list consumes most of the vertical space when populated, but each element within it remains compact and text-centric, with breathing room between items.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays deliberately low. Each functional cluster (search, filters, sort/pagination, results, contact) is visually distinct with ample whitespace above and below. The filter panel is vertically stacked rather than side-by-side to avoid a busy, dashboard-like feel, and it may be collapsed or visually understated when not in use. Text remains the primary visual element, and interactive controls are simple and unobtrusive, avoiding heavy visual chrome or dense control groups.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the dark header draws the eye, the first visual stop is the centered page title. The eye then naturally moves downward to the large, centered search field as the primary interactive element. Below that, secondary attention goes to the filter summary and optional filter panel, then along a horizontal line for sort on the left and pagination on the right. From there, reading continues in a straightforward vertical flow through the conference results list. When present, loading/no-results/error messages appear centrally in the results zone, and the red-highlighted contact email link at the bottom becomes the final, strong call-to-action that echoes the jobs page’s closing pattern.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark full-width header bands, the main content begins as a calm, white canvas with a relatively narrow, centered column that occupies about half the viewport width. After a comfortable vertical buffer, a center-aligned 'Conferences & Calls' heading appears as the first anchor point, followed closely by a succinct line of explanatory text and a prominent, wide search bar centered on the page. Beneath this, the filters are presented in a single-column, text-centric manner that aligns flush with the search bar’s width, with active filters summarized in a thin horizontal strip and detailed filter options in a low-key, optionally collapsible panel. Further down, a horizontally arranged sort-and-pagination bar spans the content column, leading the eye into a vertical stack of simple, text-based conference entries that fill most of the scrollable area but are interspersed with generous vertical spacing. Whenever data is loading, empty, or errored, a centered message occupies this results zone, maintaining the same calm, uncluttered feel. Finally, after a visible gap separating it from the functional content, a center-aligned contact strip with red-highlighted email links sits at the bottom of the column, providing a clear, final point of interaction similar to the jobs page CTA and leaving large margins of white space around it for a composed, academic-corporate impression.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and sits conceptually at the very top of the main content area, just below the header bands, orchestrating the initial population of search options and filters. It occupies no visible height or width on the page and does not alter the visual layout directly; instead, the user simply experiences a short moment before the search bar, filters, and results appear. If tied to visible loading feedback, it triggers the status indicator within the results area rather than rendering its own UI.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Background controller with no rendered DOM footprint; it runs before and alongside visible components and does not reserve or consume any layout space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is also a non-visual component that conceptually spans the entire centered content column, governing how search, filter, and pagination states map to the URL. It does not show any visible element, border, or panel. Any changes it manages are reflected by repositioning or updating existing visible components (such as which filters are active or which page of results is shown) without occupying its own space in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible state controller that overlays the whole content column logically but renders nothing; it does not affect spacing or alignment of visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned near the top of the white content area, the search-query-bar sits directly beneath the center-aligned 'Conferences & Calls' heading (and any brief explanatory line), with generous vertical spacing above and below to echo the mockup’s airy feel. It is centered horizontally and spans roughly 60–70% of the main content column’s width (about 30–40% of the total viewport width on desktop), presenting as a single prominent input field with an understated label or placeholder. The input is a simple horizontal rectangle with minimal styling, aligning with the conservative academic tone, and it anchors the beginning of the interactive flow before any filters or results are shown.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level element in the vertical stack; it does not scroll independently or stick, and it always remains just below the page title within the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-panel sits immediately below the search-query-bar, separated by a noticeable vertical gap (approximately the height of one to two lines of body text) to maintain low density. Instead of a lateral sidebar, it occupies the full width of the centered content column, matching the search bar’s horizontal footprint. The panel appears as a simple vertical stack of filter controls, each filter group occupying its own horizontal row with left-aligned labels and controls, all within the same narrow column. To keep the page from feeling busy, the filter-panel may be visually understated or collapsible, with a small, center- or right-aligned 'Show filters' / 'Hide filters' text link at its top. When expanded, it extends downward but still leaves generous white space around and between filter groups, avoiding any multi-column or dense grid arrangement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the main vertical flow; when collapsed it reduces its vertical footprint to a thin trigger row, and when expanded it pushes subsequent content (active filters summary, sort bar, results) further down without overlapping them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filters-summary appears directly below the filter-panel (or directly below the search bar if the filter panel is collapsed and no detailed controls are visible), occupying a low, horizontal band across the full width of the centered content column. It is relatively slim—about the height of one or two lines of text—and uses small, text-based badges or inline phrases to list active filters. Content is center-aligned or subtly left-aligned within the column, but the entire band remains visually calm with plenty of white space above and below. If no filters are active, this area either disappears entirely or shrinks to a minimal, lightly styled line such as 'No filters applied,' ensuring the overall layout stays airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky strip in the vertical stack; it appears and disappears without shifting the overall column alignment, only changing the vertical spacing before the sort-and-pagination bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-and-pagination-bar sits below the active-filters-summary, separated by a modest vertical gap, and spans the same width as the rest of the content column. It forms a thin horizontal control band: the sort control is left-aligned within the column (e.g., a subtle dropdown or text link), while page navigation controls are right-aligned on the same line, creating a simple left-to-right tension across the bar. The bar is not overly tall—roughly the height of one line of text plus padding—and uses understated typography and minimal borders, fitting the conservative style. White space above and below ensures this control strip feels distinct and not cramped against the results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal bar in the main column; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick to the top or bottom of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The conference-results-list occupies the main body of the page below the sort-and-pagination bar, taking up most of the vertical space available as the user scrolls. It is a single, centered vertical stack of text-based result items, constrained to the same narrow column (about 50–60% of the viewport) to maintain consistency. Each result item is left-aligned within the column, with a clear visual hierarchy of title (slightly larger or bolder), followed by date, location, and other key details in smaller text. Items are separated by generous vertical spacing and, optionally, by a thin, subtle horizontal line beneath each item, giving a calm rhythm similar to lines of text on a printed program. There are no side-by-side cards; the list respects the linear, top-to-bottom reading pattern from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list that grows as results increase; it scrolls with the page and adjusts its height dynamically while always remaining centered within the fixed-width content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component enhances the behavior of each item within the conference-results-list without adding any overt visual chrome. Each result entry is treated as a single, unified clickable block, but visually it remains a simple text cluster with perhaps a subtle hover background or underline on the title. There are no additional icons or navigation widgets added; the text remains the primary focus. All clickable behavior is contained within the boundaries already defined for each list item, so the perceived layout—spacing, item height, and alignment—does not change when navigation is applied.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral layer attached to each result item; it does not consume additional space or alter the static layout, only expanding the clickable area and navigation handling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the results are loading or an error occurs, this component temporarily occupies the same central area where the conference-results-list would appear. In loading mode, it shows a single, center-aligned indicator (such as a small spinner or the word 'Loading…') within the narrow content column, with ample white space above and below so the message feels light and unobtrusive. In an error state, it presents a concise, text-based error message centered in the column, possibly with a subtle red accent in the text but no large banners or heavy panels. In both cases, the message block is roughly the height of a few lines of text and sits directly beneath the sort-and-pagination bar, pushing the contact panel further down as needed, while the actual list of results is hidden.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual replacement for the results list; it appears and disappears within the same vertical slot as the list, without shifting other components horizontally or changing the overall column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results-state also occupies the central results zone when a search or filter combination yields no conferences. It appears as a calm, center-aligned text block in the narrow content column, typically consisting of a short headline such as 'No conferences found' followed by one or two lines of guidance (e.g., 'Try adjusting your filters' or a 'Clear all filters' link). The block is surrounded by generous white space, roughly the height of three to five lines of body text, so it resembles a small island of text in a wide white canvas, echoing the minimalism of the jobs page content. It sits directly below the sort-and-pagination bar, with the contact panel still clearly visible further down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual content that replaces the results list content area when applicable; it occupies the same vertical position and width as the list, without introducing new columns or sticky elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-link-panel is placed at the very bottom of the main content column, acting as the closing element of the page, analogous to the red email addresses in the jobs mockup. It is center-aligned and spans only as wide as its text content within the narrow column. The panel consists of one or two short lines of text, with email addresses or contact links highlighted in a red or similarly high-contrast color to stand out as the final call-to-action. There is a generous vertical margin separating it from the last piece of functional content above (results list or no-results/error message), and additional white space below before the end of the page, reinforcing the sense of a calm, intentional ending.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer-like block within the main content column; it scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the page and does not stick or overlap other components.

name: Conference Detail
title: Conference Detail
description: Displays full details for a specific conference and provides an interactive program/schedule where users can filter sessions and build a personal agenda.

core_features: Load conference details by ID using getConferenceDetail, including basic information, tracks, registration fees, bookmark state, and key deadlines.
Display an overview section with conference title, dates, location/format, topics, status, and external website URL when provided.
Allow users to bookmark or unbookmark the conference using bookmarkConference and unbookmarkConference, with live UI feedback based on isBookmarked.
Show a fees section listing registration options (attendee type, currency, amount, validity dates) using ConferenceFee data from getConferenceDetail.
Show a key deadlines section based on keyDeadlines from getConferenceDetail.
Provide a Program/Schedule tab that loads the conference program structure (days and tracks, plus defaultFilters) using getConferenceProgram.
Within the Program/Schedule tab, allow users to switch between conference days via a day selector that controls which sessions are requested.
Provide filter controls for sessions (keyword, track, start time from, end time to) aligned with defaultFilters from getConferenceProgram.
Load and display sessions for the selected day and filter combination using getConferenceSessions, including track information and isInPersonalSchedule flags.
Allow users to add sessions to their personal schedule using addSessionToPersonalSchedule and remove them using removeSessionFromPersonalSchedule.
Highlight a specific session in the schedule when the sessionId URL parameter is present, and optionally auto-scroll to it.
Provide a quick access link from the Program/Schedule tab to the user’s full schedule in My Account (my_account.html?section=my_schedule).
Enable navigation back to the conferences list (conferences.html) and to researcher profiles (researcher_profile.html?id=...).
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – View conference overview and details: 1) User navigates to conference_detail.html with a required id URL parameter (e.g., conference_detail.html?id={conferenceId}). 2) Page controller reads id from URL and calls getConferenceDetail(conferenceId). 3) When the response returns, the page displays: title, subtitle (if present), status, date range, region, location/format information, topics, external website URL, tracks summary, fees table, and key deadlines. 4) User can read all information without further interaction or switch to the Program/Schedule tab if available.

Workflow 2 – Bookmark/unbookmark a conference: 1) After getConferenceDetail returns, the UI renders a bookmark toggle based on isBookmarked. 2) If the user clicks the bookmark button when isBookmarked is false, the page calls bookmarkConference(conferenceId, notes) (notes may be supplied from an optional notes field or as an empty string). 3) On a successful response, the UI updates the bookmark state to true and may show the returned message. 4) If the user clicks the button when isBookmarked is true, the page calls unbookmarkConference(conferenceId). 5) On success, the UI updates the bookmark state to false and may show the returned message.

Workflow 3 – Explore the program and switch days: 1) User opens the Program/Schedule tab. 2) Page controller ensures getConferenceProgram(conferenceId) has been called; when it returns, the UI displays a day selector using days[]. 3) The initially selected day is determined by the day URL parameter if present and valid; otherwise, the first day in days[] is selected. 4) The selected day index, combined with defaultFilters from getConferenceProgram and any URL filter parameters, is used to construct a filters object. 5) The page calls getConferenceSessions(conferenceId, filters) and displays the resulting sessions list for the selected day. 6) When the user selects a different day, the selected day index is updated in both UI state and the day URL parameter, and getConferenceSessions is called again with the updated filters.

Workflow 4 – Filter sessions by keyword, track, and time: 1) In the Program/Schedule tab, the filter bar initializes using defaultFilters from getConferenceProgram and any URL parameters representing current filters (e.g., keyword, trackId, startTimeFrom, endTimeTo). 2) User types a keyword (for example, to find climate-related sessions) or chooses a track from the list of tracks in getConferenceProgram, and/or adjusts start/end time range. 3) On filter change, the UI updates the URL query parameters for the active tab (e.g., keyword, trackId, startTimeFrom, endTimeTo, and day) without reloading the page. 4) The page constructs a new filters object combining conferenceId, the selected day index, and the current filter values, then calls getConferenceSessions(conferenceId, filters). 5) The sessions list updates to show only sessions matching the filters. 6) If the user clears filters, the URL filter parameters are removed and getConferenceSessions is called again using defaultFilters.

Workflow 5 – Add or remove a session from personal schedule: 1) In the sessions list, each item is rendered with its current personal schedule state from isInPersonalSchedule. 2) If a session is not in the personal schedule, an action such as 'Add to my schedule' is available. When the user clicks it, the page calls addSessionToPersonalSchedule(sessionId, notes) (notes may be empty or taken from an optional field). 3) On successful response, the UI updates the state for that session to isInPersonalSchedule = true, and any personal schedule summary component is refreshed if needed. 4) If a session is already in the personal schedule, an action such as 'Remove from my schedule' is available. When clicked, the page calls removeSessionFromPersonalSchedule(sessionId). 5) On success, the UI updates that session to isInPersonalSchedule = false and refreshes any dependent UI parts.

Workflow 6 – Deep link to a specific day and session: 1) User arrives at conference_detail.html with id, optional day, and optional sessionId URL parameters (e.g., conference_detail.html?id={conferenceId}&day={dayIndex}&sessionId={sessionId}). 2) Page controller calls getConferenceDetail and getConferenceProgram based on id. 3) When Program data is available, the selected day is set to the day URL parameter if valid; otherwise it falls back to the first available day. 4) The page constructs filters including the selected day index and calls getConferenceSessions(conferenceId, filters). 5) When sessions are loaded, the session whose id matches sessionId (if present in the data) is marked as highlighted. 6) The UI may scroll to the highlighted session and visually emphasize it. 7) If the user changes the selection to a different session (for example, clicking 'highlight' from within this page), the sessionId URL parameter is updated to match.

Workflow 7 – Navigate to My Schedule and other pages: 1) In the Program/Schedule tab, a 'View my schedule' link points to my_account.html with section=my_schedule. When clicked, the browser navigates to my_account.html?section=my_schedule. 2) A 'Back to all conferences' link navigates to conferences.html when clicked. 3) Within each session item, speaker names are displayed where researcherId information is available. Clicking a speaker name navigates to researcher_profile.html?id={speaker.researcherId}.
interactions: Page load: On initial load, read URL parameters id, day, and sessionId. If id is missing or invalid, the page displays an appropriate error or redirection; otherwise it calls getConferenceDetail(conferenceId) and getConferenceProgram(conferenceId). After program data is available and the active day is determined, it calls getConferenceSessions(conferenceId, filters) for the current day and filter state.

Tab switching: If multiple tabs are implemented (e.g., Overview vs Program/Schedule), clicking a tab updates the visible content area. Tab state can optionally be mirrored in the URL via a tab parameter (e.g., tab=program), allowing direct deep links. Switching to the Program/Schedule tab triggers any necessary initial program/session API calls if not already performed.

Bookmark toggle: Clicking the bookmark control triggers either bookmarkConference or unbookmarkConference based on the current isBookmarked state. While awaiting a response, the button can be temporarily disabled or show a loading indication. On success, UI state and icon/label are updated; on error, the state is reverted and an error message is shown.

Day selection in program: Selecting a day updates a local activeDayIndex state and synchronizes the day URL parameter. It then recomputes filters and calls getConferenceSessions. The currently selected day is visibly indicated in the UI.

Filter interactions: Typing into a keyword field, selecting a track from a dropdown, or picking a start/end time updates internal filter state and corresponding URL parameters. Debounced or immediate changes cause a new call to getConferenceSessions with the latest filters. A reset/clear action removes filter parameters from the URL and reverts to defaults from getConferenceProgram.defaultFilters.

Session add/remove interactions: For each session row/card, clicking 'Add to my schedule' calls addSessionToPersonalSchedule(sessionId, notes) and, on success, sets isInPersonalSchedule to true for that session. Clicking 'Remove from my schedule' calls removeSessionFromPersonalSchedule(sessionId), and on success sets isInPersonalSchedule to false. UI states such as button label and styling update accordingly.

Session highlight via URL: When a sessionId URL parameter is present, after getConferenceSessions returns, the page checks the sessions array for a matching id. If found, that session is given a highlighted visual state. If the user navigates to another session via in-page controls, the sessionId URL parameter is updated so that the same session remains highlighted on reload or sharing the URL.

Navigation interactions: Clicking 'Back to all conferences' navigates the browser to conferences.html. Clicking 'View my schedule' navigates to my_account.html?section=my_schedule. Clicking a speaker name navigates to researcher_profile.html?id={speaker.researcherId}.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state of the page is managed via URL parameters and API responses, without relying on localStorage. The required id parameter determines which conference is loaded. On every load or history navigation event, the page reads id; if valid, it calls getConferenceDetail(id) and getConferenceProgram(id). The optional day parameter (1-based) represents the currently selected conference day in the Program/Schedule tab. After days[] is returned by getConferenceProgram, the page validates day against available dayIndex values. If day is valid, it sets the activeDayIndex to day; otherwise it sets activeDayIndex to the first available day and updates the URL to reflect this default if needed. The optional sessionId URL parameter identifies a session to highlight. After getConferenceSessions is called and sessions are returned, the page searches for the provided sessionId; if a matching session is found, it is marked as highlighted, and the UI may scroll to it. Optional additional URL parameters can be used to represent filters in the Program/Schedule tab, such as keyword, trackId, startTimeFrom, and endTimeTo. When present, these values override corresponding fields in getConferenceProgram.defaultFilters for building the filters object used in getConferenceSessions. When the user modifies filters, the application updates these URL parameters (adding, changing, or removing them) and then triggers a new getConferenceSessions call so that the URL always reflects the current visible state. Optionally, a tab parameter may be used to persist whether the Overview or Program/Schedule tab is active; on load, this parameter is read to determine which tab to show first. All state transitions (day changes, filter changes, tab switches, session highlights) are either initiated by URL changes or cause URL updates, enabling deep linking and reproducible views without client-side storage.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and top-level state management for the Conference Detail page. It reads id, day, and sessionId from the URL, calls getConferenceDetail and getConferenceProgram, derives the activeDayIndex and filter state, and then calls getConferenceSessions with the constructed filters. It also listens for in-page state changes (day, filters, highlight session, tab changes) and updates URL parameters accordingly.

Displays the main conference identification information and high-level metadata, including title, subtitle (if present), date range derived from startDate and endDate, status, region, city/country, venue, and format flags (isOnline, isHybrid). It may also show topics and a link to the external conference website (websiteUrl) when provided. It also includes a compact summary of tracks if available.

Provides a control to bookmark or unbookmark the current conference. The initial state is derived from isBookmarked returned by getConferenceDetail. Optionally, it can display a notes input field used when creating or updating a bookmark.

Shows a structured overview of the conference using data from getConferenceDetail: description, topics list, status, region, location details, isInternational flag, and any additional metadata useful to attendees. This section is primarily read-only and is usually visible in an 'Overview' tab or section.

Displays all available registration fees for the conference, grouped or listed by attendeeType, with corresponding currency, amount, optional description, and validity periods (validFrom and validTo, including early-bird indication via isEarlyBird). It is read-only and derived from the fees[] array in getConferenceDetail.

Displays key deadlines associated with the conference, using the keyDeadlines array from getConferenceDetail. Each item includes a label and a date. It may sort them chronologically and highlight deadlines that are past or upcoming based on the current date.

Organizes the page into logical sections such as 'Overview' and 'Program/Schedule'. The active tab determines which main content panel is visible. Tab selection can optionally sync with a tab URL parameter to allow direct linking to a specific tab.

Encapsulates all functionality related to viewing and interacting with the conference program. It hosts the day selector, filter bar, sessions list, and personal schedule summary. It receives program structure from getConferenceProgram and passes relevant data and callbacks to its child components. It is typically displayed when the Program/Schedule tab is active.

Displays the list of conference days using the days[] array from getConferenceProgram, showing labels and dates. It allows users to select which day’s sessions to view. The selected day controls the dayIndex filter for getConferenceSessions and updates the day URL parameter.

Provides controls to filter sessions, including a text input for keyword, a dropdown/select for trackId using tracks[] from getConferenceProgram, and time range inputs using startTimeFrom and endTimeTo. It initializes its fields using defaultFilters from getConferenceProgram combined with any filter-related URL parameters, and emits filter changes to the controller to trigger getConferenceSessions.

Renders the list of sessions returned by getConferenceSessions for the currently selected day and filter set. Each list entry displays session title, time range (startDateTime and endDateTime), room, sessionType, related track (including name and track color if available), and any climate-related indications (for example, derived from isClimateRelated or keywords). It also passes session-level actions (add/remove schedule, navigate to speakers) to child components.

Provides controls for a single session: a toggle button to add or remove the session from the user’s personal schedule based on isInPersonalSchedule, and clickable speaker names that navigate to researcher_profile.html using the speaker’s researcherId when available.

Manages highlighting of a specific session based on the sessionId URL parameter. After sessions are loaded, it locates the matching sessionId within the sessions array and applies a highlighted state to that item. It can also scroll the viewport to bring the highlighted session into view. When the user selects a different session to highlight, it updates the sessionId URL parameter.

Displays a concise summary of how many sessions for this conference are currently in the user’s personal schedule (derived from isInPersonalSchedule flags in the sessions list or from a subset of PersonalScheduleItem data if available) and provides a link to view the full schedule in My Account.

Provides page-level navigation actions, including a 'Back to all conferences' link to conferences.html and a contextual 'View my schedule' link (usually duplicated from personal-schedule-summary) to my_account.html?section=my_schedule. It may also include anchor links to jump between overview and program sections on the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: All conference content sits in a single, fixed-width central column beneath the existing full-width site header (info bar + navigation bar). The layout is strictly linear and top-to-bottom: a conference summary header at the top of the content column, followed by a bookmark control aligned to it, then a simple text-based tab row. Under the tabs, content is stacked vertically in one column: for the Overview tab, the overview text, fees table, and deadlines list; for the Program tab, the schedule controls and session list. There are no sidebars or multi-column content blocks; any multi-element groups are arranged vertically within this narrow column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All conference content sits in a single, fixed-width central column beneath the existing full-width site header (info bar + navigation bar). The layout is strictly linear and top-to-bottom: a conference summary header at the top of the content column, followed by a bookmark control aligned to it, then a simple text-based tab row. Under the tabs, content is stacked vertically in one column: for the Overview tab, the overview text, fees table, and deadlines list; for the Program tab, the schedule controls and session list. There are no sidebars or multi-column content blocks; any multi-element groups are arranged vertically within this narrow column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (not part of these components) occupies roughly 15–20% of the viewport height on desktop. Below it, the main content column spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, with wide left/right margins that create a strong white frame. Within this column, the conference summary header occupies only the top 10–15% of the visible content area, leaving a large swath of white space around it, echoing the mockup’s minimal text-on-large-canvas feel. Each subsequent section (overview, fees, deadlines, program) uses only as much vertical space as needed, separated by generous vertical gaps (roughly 2–3 line-heights between intra-section elements and 4–5 line-heights between major sections). The program schedule can extend significantly downwards as the user scrolls, but each session entry still has comfortable spacing and is not densely packed.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low and text-centric throughout. Sections contain concise text with ample line spacing and clear separation between lines and paragraphs. Lists (e.g., deadlines) keep each item on its own line with generous vertical spacing, mirroring the numbered list on the Jobs mockup. The fees table remains visually light, with plenty of white space around and within it. Even in the program schedule, each session line has extra vertical padding, making the full list feel calm and scannable rather than compact and crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first meets the strong, dark header bar across the top of the page, scanning left-to-right across logo, navigation, and utilities. The stark transition from dark header to white content draws attention downward to the centered conference summary header (title, dates). From there, the eye moves slightly down to the bookmark toggle, then to the horizontally centered tab row that signals the main content areas. Within the Overview tab, the flow is straight down: overview text, then the fees table, then the deadlines list. Within the Program tab, the flow is again vertical: day selector at the top of the program block, the filter bar directly beneath, then a long vertical list of sessions, each ending with clearly separated action links. At the very bottom of the page, center-aligned navigation links offer a final, modest call to move to other conferences.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width black header band, a wide expanse of white opens, with a relatively narrow content column centered on the page—about two-thirds the width of the viewport. After a comfortable vertical gap from the header, the conference title and key details appear as a centered summary header, occupying only a small portion of the visible screen and surrounded by generous empty space, mimicking the calm focal block seen in the Jobs mockup.
