A subtle bookmark toggle sits in line with or just beneath this title area, aligned toward the right side of the same content column but visually lightweight. Directly below, a text-based tab row stretches across the column, centered horizontally and following the simple, pre-material aesthetic—no heavy boxes, just underlined active text. Depending on the selected tab, the main content appears below as a vertical stack: in the Overview tab, a paragraph-style overview, then a lightly bordered fees table, then a spaced-out deadlines list with each date on its own line. In the Program tab, the program area begins with a centered row of day selectors, then a slim filter bar, and then a tall, scrolling list of sessions, each line separated by generous breathing room. At the bottom of the whole content column, understated, center-aligned previous/next or back-to-list links echo the mockup’s final red call-to-action, providing a quiet but clear navigation end point. The overall impression is a restrained academic page with large amounts of white space and a single, easy vertical reading path.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the structural wrapper for all conference-specific content below the global header. It defines a fixed-width content column centered in the viewport, occupying roughly 60–70% of the page width on desktop while leaving wide, symmetrical left and right margins of white space. Vertically, it begins just below the main black navigation band, with a generous top margin (around 8–10% of the viewport height) before the first visible content (the conference-summary-header), preserving the mockup’s feeling of a large white field with a compact centered block. Inside this controller, all other components are stacked in a single linear column from top to bottom, with consistent vertical spacing rules (small gaps within related elements; larger gaps between major sections) and no floating side elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-height container for the page’s main content; it scrolls with the page and ensures all nested components align to the same centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the top of the content column managed by the controller, the conference summary header is the first visual element in the white area beneath the dark site header. It is centered horizontally within the content column and occupies about 60% of the viewport width, with the text set in a simple, conservative style. The main conference title appears as a bold line centered across the column, with any date and location details on one or two lines beneath it, also center-aligned. Vertically, this block takes up only the upper 10–15% of the scrollable content region at initial load, leaving a notable band of white both above and below, echoing the centered ‘Jobs’ heading in the mockup. There is no background box; the text sits directly on the white canvas, making the header feel like a compact island of content in a wide empty field.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the main content column; scrolls out of view as the user progresses down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark toggle is visually associated with the conference summary header and sits within the same horizontal band. It appears as a small icon plus label (e.g., “Bookmark conference”) aligned to the right edge of the content column, horizontally level with the lower part of the conference title block, or immediately beneath the title but still right-aligned. It occupies only a narrow slice of width (around 10–15% of the column) and preserves ample white space between itself and the title text. This maintains the clean, text-centric look: the control is understated, with no heavy button styling, and is spaced so that it feels like a discreet utility rather than a competing focal element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline control within the summary header area; scrolls normally with the content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overview panel sits below the conference summary/header area and the tab row (when the Overview tab is active). It begins after a generous vertical gap (approximately 3–4 line-heights) beneath the tabs, preserving the low-density feel. The panel spans the full width of the centered content column, but its text is either center-aligned (to match the Jobs mockup’s center alignment) or softly left-aligned while still visually centered within the column; in either case, there is no background box or side embellishment. Paragraphs are short, with roomy line spacing; each paragraph is separated by a full line-height of vertical space, giving the sense of a calm, academic description. The section has a simple heading (e.g., “Overview”) in slightly larger text at the top, aligned consistently with the body text, with space above and below to visually separate it from the tab row and the following fees table.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text block that appears only when the relevant tab (e.g., Overview) is active; scrolls with the page and does not introduce additional scrolling containers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fees table is placed directly below the overview panel, separated by a clear vertical gap (around 2–3 line-heights) to keep the content light and scannable. It is centered within the same fixed-width content column but uses slightly less width than the full column—about 70–80% of the column width—so that there is visible white space on both sides of the table. The table itself is simple and minimal, with thin horizontal rules and modest cell padding, reflecting the conservative academic style rather than a modern, card-like look. Column headers are in a slightly heavier weight but not styled as strong colored bands. Rows have enough vertical padding to feel open; there is no zebra striping or heavy borders, maintaining a light, text-first impression. A section label such as “Registration Fees” sits above the table, aligned consistently with the table itself and separated by a small vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level table; appears in the vertical flow under the overview and scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The deadlines list is positioned below the fees table, with a generous vertical gap (approx. 3 line-heights) so that the table and list feel like distinct sections. It spans the same content column width as the overview panel, with the text centrally positioned. Each key deadline (e.g., paper submission, registration cutoff) appears on its own line or in its own short row, with noticeable vertical space between items, closely mirroring the numbered items in the Jobs mockup. The list may use simple numbering or bullet points, but they are subtle and text-oriented rather than graphic. A brief heading like “Important Deadlines” is placed above the first list item, aligned with the list text. The overall section occupies only a small vertical portion of the page relative to the available white space, reinforcing the low content density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list; appears under the fees table in the Overview tab’s content stack and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tabbed panel is located directly beneath the conference summary header (and visually associated bookmark toggle), with a moderate vertical gap (about 2 line-heights) separating the header block from the tabs. The tabs form a single horizontal row across the width of the content column, centered visually. Each tab label is simple text (e.g., “Overview”, “Program”, “My Schedule”), with no heavy background; the active tab is distinguished by a modest underline or a slight weight difference, in keeping with the pre-material, text-centric style. The tab row itself is relatively thin compared to modern, chunky tab designs—occupying only one line of text plus a small amount of padding. Generous whitespace above and below the row ensures it does not feel cramped. When a tab is selected, its corresponding content (Overview or Program) appears directly underneath in the same single-column flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal control within the content column; it does not stick to the top of the viewport on scroll, and switching tabs swaps out the content stack below without altering the global page layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program schedule panel occupies the central content column when the Program tab is active. It begins directly below the tabs, with a vertical gap comparable to the one above the overview panel (approximately 3–4 line-heights). Within this panel, all program-related elements—the day selector, session filter bar, session list, session actions, and personal schedule summary (if shown here)—are arranged vertically in a single stack. The panel itself does not introduce a separate boxed background; it simply defines a logical section of content on the same white canvas. The top of the panel is slightly emphasized with a simple text heading such as “Conference Program” aligned consistently with the rest of the text. As the user scrolls, the panel can extend far down the page while maintaining generous spacing between internal elements, avoiding a crowded schedule view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical section that appears only when the Program tab is active; scrolls with the page without separate scrollbars or floating headers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day selector is positioned at the top of the program-schedule-panel, just below any optional program heading, with a modest gap separating them. It consists of a horizontal row of day labels (e.g., “Day 1”, “Day 2”, or explicit dates), centered within the content column. Each day label is rendered as simple text or a lightly outlined pill with very minimal styling, in keeping with the conservative aesthetic; active and inactive states are differentiated via text weight or underline, not bold colored fills. The entire day selector row is relatively short in height, occupying roughly one to one-and-a-half lines of text plus small padding. There is comfortable white space above and below this row before the filter bar, ensuring the controls feel airy and not cramped together.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal control at the top of the program section; it does not remain sticky as the user scrolls the session list, and changes in selection update the sessions displayed below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is placed directly beneath the day selector within the program-schedule-panel, separated by a small but clear vertical space (around 1.5–2 line-heights). It spans the full width of the centered content column, but the controls (e.g., dropdowns for track, search field, checkboxes for formats) are arranged in a single row or two soft rows, aligned left within the column while still respecting the overall central framing of the page. The appearances of the inputs are plain and understated—simple bordered fields with light labels, no filled color bars—matching the slightly older, academic style. The bar itself does not have a colored background; it is just a set of text and form controls on white. There is sufficient white space beneath the filter controls before the session list begins, so the list reads clearly as a separate block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control strip within the program panel; it scrolls with the sessions and does not float independently. Filter changes update which sessions appear below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session list sits immediately below the filter bar, with a clear vertical separation (about 2 line-heights) so that the start of the list is visually obvious. It occupies the full width of the content column, and each session entry is displayed as a horizontally full-width row with text aligned to the left margin of the column. To maintain low density, each session row has generous vertical padding, and there is additional vertical space (approximately one line-height) between rows, so sessions never feel stacked tightly together. Typical session details (time, title, speakers, room) are presented in simple text lines, possibly with the time on the first line and title on the next, relying on font weight and spacing rather than colored badges. The list can extend far down the page as the primary scrolling content while keeping a consistent, calm rhythm of evenly spaced entries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A vertically scrolling list within the main page scroll (no nested scroll area); as the user scrolls down, more session rows become visible in a continuous column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session item actions are embedded within each session row in the session-list, typically aligned to the right side of the row while the core session details sit on the left. They occupy a narrow horizontal band to the far right of the content column, containing text-based controls such as “Add to my schedule,” “Details,” or an icon plus label. These actions are visually lightweight—small type, simple underlines or subtle iconography—so they don’t overpower the descriptive text. Vertically, the actions align with the main session information, usually on the same baseline as the session title or time, and there is enough horizontal spacing between the text and the actions that neither feels cramped. This preserves the simple, text-centric style while offering clear functionality.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline action group within each session row; it moves with its session item and has no independent scrolling or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual helper component that manages highlighting of sessions (for example, when a user hovers, focuses, or adds them to their personal schedule). Its impact on layout is indirect: when a session is highlighted, the change is subtle and keeps the conservative aesthetic—perhaps a light background tint within the session row or a slight weight change on the title, without altering the overall padding or row height. There are no popovers or overlays that break the single-column flow; any highlight remains confined within the existing session row boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral; it does not occupy physical space and only affects visual styling of existing session-list items without shifting layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The personal schedule summary appears as a compact panel within the main content column, typically at the lower portion of the Program tab content or just beneath the program-schedule-panel depending on the design choice. To maintain the Jobs-like sense of a final, focused call-to-action, it is recommended to place it below the session list, after a large vertical gap (around 3–4 line-heights), so that it reads as a concluding summary of the user’s selections. The panel spans the full width of the content column but remains visually light: no heavy box, just a short heading (e.g., “Your Selected Sessions”) and one to three lines of text summarizing counts and maybe a small link to “View full personal schedule.” Text alignment follows the page’s dominant alignment (centered or soft left) and remains surrounded by ample white space, making the summary stand out quietly as the end of the program content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the vertical flow; scrolls into view after the user passes through the session list. It does not float or dock to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The conference navigation links form the last visible component in the main content column, placed below all other sections (Overview or Program content and any personal schedule summary) with a generous top margin ( approximately 4–5 line-heights) so they feel clearly separated as the final navigational element. The links—such as “Back to all conferences,” “Previous conference,” and “Next conference”—are center-aligned within the content column and expressed as simple text links, possibly with a subtle color accent similar to the red email addresses in the Jobs mockup. They occupy only a single line or two of text, leaving substantial white space above and below so the end of the page feels open rather than abruptly cut off. This placement and styling make them a soft but clear conclusion to the page’s visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, footer-like navigation control at the bottom of the content column; scrolls normally and is the final element in the page’s vertical flow.

name: Researchers Directory
title: Researchers Directory
description: Search and filter researchers by keywords, geography, expertise, and metrics, and navigate to individual researcher profiles.

core_features: Keyword-based researcher search using the searchResearchers interface, with optional pre-populated query from URL parameters.
Filter panel powered by getResearcherSearchOptions to populate countries, expertise keywords, primary research areas, and h-index range boundaries.
Dynamic filters for country, expertise keyword(s), primary research area(s), and h-index min/max, mapped to the filters parameter of searchResearchers.
Search results list displaying researcher snippets (name, affiliation, country, h-index, selected expertise keywords), using Researcher objects returned by searchResearchers.
Highlighting of matched expertise terms in each result using the highlightedExpertise values from searchResearchers.
Pagination of results using the page and pageSize parameters of searchResearchers, with totalCount used to determine the number of pages.
Sorting of results via the sort parameter of searchResearchers (e.g., by relevance, h-index ascending/descending, name), controlled by UI sort options.
Navigation to detailed researcher_profile.html pages when users click on a researcher name using researcher.id in the URL.
Support link 'Need help finding collaborators?' that navigates to contact.html with topic=membership as a URL parameter.
Full synchronization of search filters, query, sort, and pagination with URL parameters, so the current state can be shared or reloaded.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open the directory with or without pre-filled parameters
1) User navigates to researchers.html directly from the main navigation, or via a contextual link that may include query, country, hIndexMin, hIndexMax, and/or expertise URL parameters.
2) On page initialization, the state controller component calls getResearcherSearchOptions to retrieve available countries, expertiseKeywords, primaryResearchAreas, and hIndexRange.
3) After options are loaded, the controller reads the current URL parameters (including query, country, hIndexMin, hIndexMax, expertise, page, and sort if present).
4) The controller derives an initial search configuration from these parameters and calls searchResearchers with query and a filters object constructed from the parsed URL parameters.
5) The search form and filter panel components are populated with the current query and filters derived from the URL, so the UI reflects the state used for the initial search.
6) The results list, pagination controls, and sort UI are rendered from the searchResearchers response.

Workflow 2: Perform a new keyword search
1) User types keywords into the search input (e.g., a combination of topics, methods, or application areas).
2) User submits the search form (via a button click or pressing Enter).
3) The search form component triggers an event to the state controller with the updated query while preserving the current filters (country, hIndexMin, hIndexMax, expertise, primaryResearchAreas) unless the user has explicitly cleared them.
4) The controller resets the current page to 1, updates the URL parameters to reflect the new query and reset page, then calls searchResearchers with the updated query and existing filters.
5) When the response returns, the results list and pagination components update. The new state is reflected in the URL so the search can be bookmarked or shared.

Workflow 3: Refine search via filters
1) User opens or interacts with the filter panel component and selects one or more filter values (e.g., chooses a country from the countries options, adjusts h-index min/max within the hIndexRange limits, selects expertise keywords or primary research areas from the options returned by getResearcherSearchOptions).
2) On each filter change (or when the user applies filters using an 'Apply filters' action if configured), the filter panel emits an event with the updated filters object.
3) The state controller resets the page to 1, merges these filters with the current query and sort settings, updates URL parameters (country, hIndexMin, hIndexMax, expertise, plus any additional filter-related parameters such as primaryArea if used), and calls searchResearchers.
4) The results list refreshes based on the filtered results, and the filter panel reflects the chosen filter values. The URL changes ensure back/forward browser navigation restores the same filter combination.

Workflow 4: Change sorting or pagination
1) User changes the sort option (e.g., selects a different sort criterion) via the sort control component.
2) The sort control emits an event specifying the selected sort configuration.
3) The state controller sets page back to 1, updates the sort-related URL parameter, and calls searchResearchers with the same query and filters but with the new sort parameter.
4) The updated sorted results are rendered in the results list.
5) When the user clicks on a pagination control to move to another page, the pagination component emits a page change event.
6) The state controller updates the page parameter in the URL and calls searchResearchers with the updated page value, keeping query, filters, and sort intact.
7) The results list updates to show the selected page.

Workflow 5: Open a researcher profile
1) User clicks on a researcher’s name (or a dedicated 'View profile' action) within the results list.
2) The results list component reads the researcher.id from the clicked item and constructs a navigation URL researcher_profile.html?id={researcher.id}.
3) The browser navigates to researcher_profile.html with the id parameter, leaving the directory state preserved in the previous URL for back navigation.
4) If the user returns using the browser back button, the directory-state-manager re-parses the URL and re-runs the same search, restoring the search state.

Workflow 6: Get help finding collaborators
1) User is unsure how to refine search or wants personalized guidance, and clicks the 'Need help finding collaborators?' link or button.
2) The support link component triggers navigation to contact.html?topic=membership.
3) The contact page uses the topic parameter to display contact options relevant to collaboration or membership support.

Workflow 7: Clear filters or reset directory
1) User chooses to clear all filters (and optionally the query) via a 'Clear filters' or 'Reset search' action in the filter panel or search form.
2) The corresponding component emits an event indicating which parts of the state to clear (filters only, or both filters and query).
3) The state controller updates the URL parameters accordingly (e.g., removing country, hIndexMin, hIndexMax, expertise, and other filter-related params, optionally clearing query, and resetting page and sort to default).
4) The controller calls searchResearchers with the resulting simplified state.
5) The UI updates to reflect that filters (and possibly query) have been cleared.
interactions: Typing in the keyword search field and submitting the form triggers an onSubmit event, which updates the query URL parameter, resets the page to 1, and calls searchResearchers.

Selecting or changing a country filter in the filter panel triggers onFilterChange, updating the filters object (e.g., filters.country) and corresponding URL parameters, followed by a searchResearchers call.

Adjusting h-index minimum and maximum values via numeric inputs or a range control triggers onFilterChange; the state controller validates that values are within the hIndexRange boundaries from getResearcherSearchOptions before updating hIndexMin/hIndexMax URL parameters and calling searchResearchers.

Selecting expertise keywords or primary research areas in the filter panel (e.g., through multi-selects or chips populated from getResearcherSearchOptions) triggers onFilterChange and updates expertise-related URL parameters, then calls searchResearchers.
Changing the sort option in the sort control triggers onSortChange, which updates the sort URL parameter and calls searchResearchers (with page reset to 1).
Clicking a pagination control (next/previous page or a specific page number) triggers onPageChange, which updates the page URL parameter and calls searchResearchers with the new page.
Clicking on a researcher’s name in the results list triggers onClickResearcherName, which navigates to researcher_profile.html?id={researcher.id}.
Clicking the 'Need help finding collaborators?' link triggers onClickHelpLink, which navigates to contact.html?topic=membership.

Clicking a 'Clear filters' action triggers onClearFilters, which removes filter-related parameters from the URL, resets internal filter state, and calls searchResearchers with the remaining query and default sort/page.

Browser back/forward navigation triggers onUrlChange (e.g., via popstate), which causes the state controller to re-parse URL parameters and re-issue a searchResearchers call so that the UI reflects the historical state.

If the user interacts with highlighted expertise chips within a result (if implemented as interactive), onClickExpertiseChip can add that expertise term to the filters, update URL parameters (expertise), and re-run searchResearchers.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies entirely on URL parameters and API responses (getResearcherSearchOptions and searchResearchers) for state. Initial load: upon page initialization, the controller calls getResearcherSearchOptions with no parameters to obtain the full set of supported filters (countries, expertiseKeywords, primaryResearchAreas, and the overall hIndexRange). Once options are available, the controller parses URL parameters: query, country, hIndexMin, hIndexMax, expertise, and any additional search-related parameters used by the implementation (such as page, sort, or primaryArea). All parameters are interpreted as the source of truth for the UI state. If hIndexMin or hIndexMax are outside the range returned by hIndexRange, they are clamped or ignored according to validation rules before being passed to searchResearchers. The controller constructs the searchResearchers call as follows: it passes query directly from the URL query parameter (or an empty string if absent); it builds a filters object that includes country, hIndexMin, hIndexMax, expertise and optionally primaryResearchAreas, mapped from one or more URL parameters; it passes sort parsed from a URL parameter representing the selected sort option; and it passes page and pageSize (page from URL, with a default if missing; pageSize from application configuration or a default within the UI logic). Whenever the user changes query, filters, sort, or pagination through the UI, the controller updates the corresponding URL parameters using pushState/replaceState (or equivalent), then calls searchResearchers again, ensuring the UI stays in sync with the URL. Browser back/forward navigation is handled by re-parsing the URL parameters and re-running searchResearchers, so the page state is fully reconstructable from the URL alone without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Central controller that initializes filter options, parses URL parameters into a unified search state, invokes getResearcherSearchOptions and searchResearchers, and synchronizes UI components and URL parameters.

Provides a text input for keyword-based researcher search and an action to submit or clear the query. Reflects and updates the query parameter in the URL and triggers new searches via the state manager.

Displays and manages filter controls for country, h-index range, expertise keywords, and primary research areas. Uses options from getResearcherSearchOptions and current URL parameters to show selected filters, and notifies the state manager of changes.
data_binding: URL parameters: country, hIndexMin, hIndexMax, expertise and any additional filter-related parameters

components.functionality: Shows a textual summary of the current search state and results, such as the total number of matches and the active query and filters, based entirely on URL parameters and searchResearchers results.
data_binding: URL parameters: query, country, hIndexMin, hIndexMax, expertise and other filters

components.functionality: Allows users to select a sort order for researchers (e.g., relevance, h-index ascending/descending, name). Updates the sort URL parameter and triggers re-search via the state manager.

Renders the list of researchers returned by searchResearchers, showing key profile snippets and highlightedExpertise. Supports navigation to individual researcher profiles when the user interacts with an item.
Displays pagination links based on totalCount and pageSize from searchResearchers, and the current page. Triggers page changes that update the page URL parameter and re-run the search.
Provides a shortcut for users to request help finding collaborators by linking to the contact page with an appropriate topic parameter.
Displays contextual information when searchResearchers returns no results, optionally prompting the user to adjust filters, broaden the query, or use the collaborator-support link.
Shows loading feedback while getResearcherSearchOptions or searchResearchers is in progress, and hides itself when responses are received.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing full-width black header (info bar + main navbar), the Researchers Directory page uses a single, centered content column with no sidebars, mirroring the Jobs page. The content flows strictly top-to-bottom: page title and short descriptor, then the search form, then a stacked filter panel, followed by a results summary bar with a right-aligned sort control on the same horizontal line. Beneath that sits the vertical list of researcher results, then pagination controls, and finally a small collaborator-support link as the closing call-to-action. Conditional UI (loading indicator and empty state message) appears in the central results region, replacing or overlaying the results list while preserving the basic vertical structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing full-width black header (info bar + main navbar), the Researchers Directory page uses a single, centered content column with no sidebars, mirroring the Jobs page. The content flows strictly top-to-bottom: page title and short descriptor, then the search form, then a stacked filter panel, followed by a results summary bar with a right-aligned sort control on the same horizontal line. Beneath that sits the vertical list of researcher results, then pagination controls, and finally a small collaborator-support link as the closing call-to-action. Conditional UI (loading indicator and empty state message) appears in the central results region, replacing or overlaying the results list while preserving the basic vertical structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (thin info bar + thicker navbar) occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height, just like in the mockup. Below this, a tall band of white space leads into the centered page title and introductory copy, taking up another ~10–15% of the viewport before any interactive controls begin. The main content column is relatively narrow on desktop—about 55–65% of the viewport width—centered horizontally, creating wide left and right margins. Each major block (search form, filter panel, summary/sort row, results list, pagination, support link) is separated vertically by generous whitespace (roughly 2–3 line heights), so even functional controls feel lightly placed within a large white canvas.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays deliberately low. The search form is a single prominent input with one or two concise controls, not a dense advanced search. Filters are vertically stacked within a simple block, with each filter group in its own line or short cluster, avoiding multi-column filter layouts. The results summary and sort are compact, single-line controls. Researcher result items are text-centric blocks with a few key lines (name, affiliation, location, a small set of metrics) and ample spacing between items. Pagination is a simple, uncluttered line of links. Overall, there are no cramped UI clusters; each element has breathing room consistent with the Jobs page’s sparse, calm composition.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first encounters the dark global header band and navigational structure, then drops down to the stark contrast of the white content area. Within this white space, the centered 'Researchers Directory' heading stands as the primary entry point, similar to the 'Jobs' heading. The eye then moves directly downward to the keyword search form, which acts as the main interactive focal point. Below that, the filter panel offers refinement options in a secondary, more subdued block. Once a search is running, the user’s gaze follows the horizontal results summary (left) and sort control (right), echoing the header’s left-center-right balance. From there, attention flows down the vertical list of researcher entries, ending naturally on the centered pagination controls and finally the collaborator-support link, which, like the red email addresses in the mockup, functions as a last, subtle call-to-action at the bottom center.

layout.overall_description: The page sits beneath the existing full-width black header and navigation, which dominate the top 15–20% of the screen. Immediately below, a broad expanse of white creates a calm buffer before the content begins. In the center of this white field, a narrow content column—about 60% of the viewport width—is aligned to the center, leaving wide, quiet margins on both sides. At the top of this column, the 'Researchers Directory' title appears as a centered heading, with a short, center-aligned descriptive line beneath it. Below a generous vertical gap, a wide, single-line keyword search input anchors the functional experience, stretching almost the full width of the column and centered visually. Another comfortable gap leads into a vertically stacked filter panel that occupies the same column width and feels like a simple text-driven block rather than a heavy sidebar. Further down, a single horizontal row hosts the results summary text on the left and the sort control aligned to the right, all within the central column. The main results list flows downward from here, each researcher entry occupying the full column width with plenty of white space above and below each item. After the list, centered pagination controls sit with ample breathing room before a final small line of text—the collaborator support link—appearing centered near the bottom as the last detail on the page. Conditional states (loading or empty results) simply replace the central results area with a centered message or indicator, preserving the same narrow, vertical, text-focused composition. The overall impression is a restrained, corporate-academic directory: mostly white, gently structured, and highly readable, with a strong top-to-bottom reading path and no visual clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The directory-state-manager is non-visual and does not render as a visible element on the page. Conceptually, it sits behind the main content column, orchestrating which visible components show at any given time. It controls whether the loading indicator or empty-state message replaces the results list area, and how filters and search criteria affect the results summary and list. It occupies no explicit space in the layout, ensuring that the clean, text-centric composition remains visually uninterrupted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical/structural; no spatial footprint in the UI. Its behavior is limited to showing/hiding or updating other components without affecting the physical layout structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The researcher-search-form sits near the top of the central content column, directly beneath the centered page title and brief description. There is a generous vertical gap (roughly 48–64px on desktop) between the description and the top edge of the search form, mirroring the airy spacing seen above the 'Jobs' content in the mockup. The form is horizontally centered within the main column and spans roughly 90–100% of the column’s width (which itself is about 60% of the viewport), making the search input the dominant interactive element. The primary keyword input is a single, long text field running almost the full width of the form, with any supporting controls (e.g., a submit button or a simple keyword help text) aligned in a simple, linear fashion—either right-aligned within the same line or stacked directly beneath, but never forming a dense multi-row cluster. There is comfortable padding above and below the input (around 16–24px) so it feels like an isolated, emphasized control in a large white space. On smaller screens, the form still appears centered but expands to use nearly the full viewport width, maintaining the single-column, top-to-bottom flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow. It scrolls off the top of the viewport as the user moves down the page; it does not behave as a sticky or floating element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The researcher-filter-panel is placed immediately below the search form, separated from it by a generous vertical gap (around 32–40px) to maintain the low-density, segmented feel. It occupies the full width of the central content column and is horizontally centered along with it. Rather than appearing as a side column, the filters are presented in a single stacked block, aligned to the left within the column but visually balanced by the large symmetrical white margins on both sides. Each filter group (e.g., geography, expertise, metrics) is given its own line or short cluster, arranged top-to-bottom so that fields rarely sit side by side; if two fields are paired horizontally, they still retain ample horizontal spacing and avoid feeling grid-like or cramped. The panel may be enclosed with a very subtle boundary (e.g., a light gray top border or a faint background tint) but keeps decoration minimal, consistent with the conservative style. There is comfortable internal spacing—each filter row separated by roughly 16–24px—and a similar margin (about 32px) below the last filter row before the next component begins. On smaller screens, all filters gracefully stack full-width, maintaining a simple vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the main content flow. Optionally, the entire panel can be collapsible behind a single, left-aligned 'Refine results' text header, but when expanded it simply occupies vertical space without sticking or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-summary-bar appears below the filter panel, separated by a modest vertical gap (about 24–32px). It shares a row with the sort control, both residing within the same central content column. The summary text (e.g., '124 researchers found') is left-aligned at the start of this row, roughly 16–24px from the column’s left edge, reinforcing a clean reading line. The bar itself is visually subtle—essentially just text on the white background with perhaps a slight typographic emphasis (e.g., slightly bolder or a hairline separator above) to differentiate it from body content. It spans the full width of the content column, but the actual text occupies only the left portion, leaving the right side visually open for the sort control. Vertical height is compact, about one line of text with minimal padding (8–12px), to keep the focus on the results that follow while still clearly communicating the directory state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored just above the results list. When the page scrolls, it moves with the rest of the content and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control shares the same horizontal row as the results-summary-bar, aligned within the central content column. While the summary text is left-aligned, the sort control is aligned to the right edge of the column, mirroring the header’s logo-left / nav-center / icons-right balance in a simplified manner. This creates a gentle left-right tension that guides the eye across the row before dropping down to the results. The sort control itself is compact—likely a single dropdown or a small set of understated text links—and does not dominate the line visually. It sits vertically centered relative to the summary text, with small internal padding (around 4–8px) and no heavy borders. The space between the summary text and the sort control remains mostly empty, preserving the airy feel. On smaller screens, the sort control can stack below the summary, remaining right-aligned or center-aligned while keeping sufficient vertical separation (about 8–12px) to avoid crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the summary row. It is not sticky and adjusts naturally in responsive layouts, either remaining in the same row or stacking vertically beneath the summary bar on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The researcher-results-list sits directly below the results summary/sort row, with a vertical gap of around 24–32px to clearly separate meta information from the actual content. It occupies the full width of the central content column and remains horizontally centered in the page. Each researcher entry extends from the left to the right edge of this column, appearing as a text-centric block with generous vertical padding (16–24px above and below each item) and a consistent separation (24–32px) between items. Entries do not appear as heavily styled cards; instead, they resemble structured text sections with a clear hierarchy: name (slightly larger or bolder), followed by affiliation, location, and a small line or two of key metrics or expertise tags. The information is primarily left-aligned, maintaining a strong vertical reading line from top to bottom. There are no multi-column grids; if any metadata aligns horizontally (e.g., metrics side-by-side), it remains minimal and well-spaced so the block still reads as a single cohesive unit. The overall list can extend vertically as needed; the white background and wide side margins ensure that even a long list remains visually calm. On mobile, entries simply scale to full width and maintain the same linear stacking pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the main page flow. As the list grows, the user scrolls down; no infinite-scroll mechanics are implied in this layout description.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls component is positioned beneath the researcher-results-list, separated from the last result item by a generous vertical gap (around 40–56px) so it feels like a distinct, concluding section rather than part of the last entry. It is horizontally centered within the main content column, with the pagination links or numbers arranged in a single, compact line. The style is understated: simple text links or numbers with modest spacing (8–12px) between them and minimal decoration, aligning with the conservative academic tone. The overall width of the pagination line is relatively small compared to the column width, leaving noticeable white space on both sides and reinforcing the centered, low-density look. There is additional white space (32–40px) below the pagination before any subsequent elements, ensuring the page’s bottom never feels crowded even if there are many pages.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the results section. It scrolls normally and does not remain fixed; it appears once per results list, at the end of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collaborator-support-link sits at the very bottom of the main content column, below the pagination controls, separated by a comfortable vertical gap (around 32–48px) so it reads as a distinct, final call-to-action. It is center-aligned within the column and typically presented as a short, single line of text (e.g., 'Need help finding collaborators? Contact our support team'), with the actionable portion (e.g., an email address or link) highlighted in an accent color analogous to the red email addresses in the Jobs mockup. The link line is not enclosed in any box or button; it appears as simple text on the white background, keeping the minimal, text-centric look. Additional whitespace (24–32px) is left below this link before the end of the page content, allowing it to breathe and to visually mirror the way the Jobs page ends on a centered, slightly emphasized contact detail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky footer-like element of this page section. It appears once at the bottom of the main content column and scrolls off-screen with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message occupies the same central region as the researcher-results-list when no results are available. It appears directly below the results summary/sort row, centered within the main content column both horizontally and, where possible, visually weighted toward the vertical center of the remaining viewport (by adding extra top and bottom padding around the message). The message text is center-aligned, echoing the Jobs page style where each numbered activity line sits in the middle of a large white space. There is a generous vertical margin (48–64px) above and below the message block, making it feel like a stand-alone central cluster of text within a broad white canvas. The copy may include one or two short lines explaining that no researchers were found and offering suggestions (e.g., 'Try broadening your filters'), with any links or action phrases subtly highlighted. No separate frame or illustration is used; the empty state remains simple and textual, consistent with the conservative, academic aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the researcher-results-list area. When active, the list is hidden and the empty-state-message occupies its vertical space. It scrolls as normal content and does not overlay the summary/sort row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears in the results region when a search or filter action is in progress. It is positioned directly below the results summary/sort row, aligned with the center of the main content column. The indicator is visually minimal—such as a small spinner next to the word 'Loading…' or a simple animated dot sequence—presented on the plain white background without additional chrome. Vertical padding (about 40–56px above and below the indicator) ensures it appears as a small, focused detail floating in the middle of a generous white space, similar to how the content on the Jobs page sits in a large open area. When results are short, the indicator may sit roughly around the vertical center of the viewport as the page first loads; as the user scrolls, it behaves like any other element. Once data is loaded, the loading-indicator disappears and is replaced by either the researcher-results-list or the empty-state-message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily occupies the space where the results list or empty state would be, without overlaying other fixed content. It is inline with the document flow and appears/disappears based on state changes managed by the directory-state-manager.

name: Researcher Profile
title: Researcher Profile
description: Displays detailed information about a specific researcher, including biography, expertise, metrics, selected work, and follow/unfollow controls tied to the user’s Followed Researchers list.

core_features: Load and display a full researcher profile (biography, affiliation details, research areas, expertise keywords, contact links, and metrics) based on the URL parameter `id` using `getResearcherProfile`.

Show selected publications associated with the researcher (from `selectedPublications` in `getResearcherProfile`) with navigation to individual publication detail pages and to a full publications search filtered by the researcher’s name.

Show conferences associated with the researcher (from `associatedConferences` in `getResearcherProfile`) with navigation to individual conference detail pages and to the main conferences search filtered by one of the researcher’s primary research areas.

Provide follow/unfollow controls that call `followResearcher` and `unfollowResearcher`, update the profile’s follow state (`isFollowed`), and offer navigation to the Followed Researchers section in My Account.
Surface contextual status and error messages for profile loading and follow/unfollow actions, without using any static or hardcoded data values.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – View a researcher profile:
1) User arrives on `researcher_profile.html` with a URL such as `researcher_profile.html?id={researcherId}` from researchers, conference detail, publication detail, or My Account.
2) On page initialization, the page reads the `id` URL parameter and passes it as `researcherId` to `getResearcherProfile`.
3) While waiting for the response, the page shows a loading state for all profile sections.
4) When `getResearcherProfile` returns, the page populates the profile header (name, affiliations, contact links), expertise and research areas, metrics (h-index, citation count), selected publications, associated conferences, and follow state (`isFollowed`).
5) If `getResearcherProfile` fails or returns invalid data, the page shows an error message and suppresses dependent interactions (e.g., follow/unfollow, navigation links) until a valid profile is loaded.

Workflow 2 – Follow a researcher:
1) On a loaded profile, if `isFollowed` is false, the Follow control shows a primary Follow action and an optional notes input.
2) User may enter optional notes to remember why they are following this researcher.
3) User activates the Follow action.
4) The Follow component calls `followResearcher` with `researcherId` taken from the `id` URL parameter and `notes` from the input.
5) While the request is pending, the Follow action is disabled and a progress state is shown.
6) On success, the component updates local follow state to `true`, optionally uses the returned `FollowedResearcher` object for confirmation details, and shows a success message.
7) The Follow panel now displays an Unfollow action and reveals a link to `my_account.html?section=followed_researchers` so the user can review all followed researchers.

Workflow 3 – Unfollow a researcher:
1) On a loaded profile where `isFollowed` is true, the Follow control shows an Unfollow action.
2) User activates the Unfollow action.
3) The Follow component calls `unfollowResearcher` with `researcherId` taken from the `id` URL parameter.
4) While the request is pending, the Unfollow action is disabled and a progress state is shown.
5) On success (when the response `success` is true), the component updates local follow state to `false` and shows a confirmation message.
6) The Follow panel returns to the Follow state (with optional notes input), and any My Account navigation link remains available for users who want to see their current list of followed researchers.

Workflow 4 – View all publications by this researcher:
1) On a loaded profile, the Selected Publications section lists items from `selectedPublications` and exposes a control labeled functionally as “View all publications by this researcher.”
2) When activated, this control reads `researcher.fullName` from the loaded profile.
3) The page navigates to `publications.html?query={encoded researcher.fullName}` without any hardcoded query string values.
4) The publications page can then perform its own search using the provided query parameter.

Workflow 5 – Find related conferences:
1) On a loaded profile, the Conferences section lists items from `associatedConferences` and exposes a control labeled functionally as “Find related conferences.”
2) When activated, the control reads the first value in `researcher.primaryResearchAreas` (if available).
3) The page navigates to `conferences.html?query={encoded researcher.primaryResearchAreas[0]}`.
4) The conferences page can use this query parameter to filter or search for relevant conferences.

Workflow 6 – Go to Followed Researchers in My Account:
1) After following a researcher (or when viewing a profile that is already followed), the Follow panel displays a navigation control labeled functionally as “View all followed researchers.”
2) When activated, the page navigates to `my_account.html?section=followed_researchers`.
3) My Account can then use the `section` URL parameter to display the Followed Researchers view.

Workflow 7 – Switch between researchers via URL:
1) User (or another page) changes the URL to `researcher_profile.html?id={anotherResearcherId}` via navigation.
2) The page initialization or URL change handler detects the new `id` value.
3) Existing profile data is cleared or set to a loading state.
4) The page calls `getResearcherProfile` with the new `researcherId`.
5) When data is returned, all profile sections re-bind to the new researcher data, including the follow state, publications, and conferences, without any hardcoded content.
interactions: On page load or when the URL `id` parameter changes, the data loader component calls `getResearcherProfile` with `researcherId` equal to the current `id` parameter.
If there is no `id` parameter or it is invalid, the page shows a functional error state and does not attempt to call `getResearcherProfile` until a valid `id` is provided.
Clicking the Follow button triggers validation of the notes input (if any) and then calls `followResearcher(researcherId, notes)`; during the call the button is disabled.
Clicking the Unfollow button calls `unfollowResearcher(researcherId)` and disables follow/unfollow actions until the response is received.
On successful follow, the follow control updates to an Unfollow state, a success message is shown, and a link to `my_account.html?section=followed_researchers` becomes or remains visible.
On successful unfollow, the follow control updates to a Follow state, and a confirmation message is shown.

If `followResearcher` or `unfollowResearcher` returns an error-like response or indicates failure, the follow control displays an error message and leaves the previous follow/unfollow state unchanged.

Clicking an item in the Selected Publications list navigates to a publication detail page using a URL such as `publication_detail.html?id={publication.id}` (the `id` is taken from the `Publication` object, not hardcoded).
Clicking the functional control 'View all publications by this researcher' navigates to `publications.html?query={encoded researcher.fullName}` based on the loaded `Researcher` data.

Clicking an item in the Associated Conferences list navigates to a conference detail page using a URL such as `conference_detail.html?id={conference.id}` (the `id` is taken from the `Conference` object).
Clicking the functional control 'Find related conferences' navigates to `conferences.html?query={encoded researcher.primaryResearchAreas[0]}` if a primary research area is available.
Clicking the functional control 'View all followed researchers' navigates to `my_account.html?section=followed_researchers`.
Clicking any contact link in the header (email or website) opens the corresponding mail or external URL as provided by the `Researcher` entity; no values are hardcoded.

Clicking internal section navigation links (e.g., to biography, expertise, publications, conferences) updates the URL fragment (hash) or scrolls to the relevant section, without changing the `id` parameter or making additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The core page state is driven by the URL parameter `id` and by responses from the assigned interfaces. On initialization, the page reads `id` from the query string of `researcher_profile.html`. If `id` is present and non-empty, it is used as `researcherId` in a call to `getResearcherProfile`. The response populates a composite state that includes: (1) `researcher` (Researcher entity); (2) `selectedPublications` (array of Publication entities); (3) `associatedConferences` (array of Conference entities); and (4) `isFollowed` (boolean). This state determines what each component renders. If `id` is missing or invalid, the page sets a 'no-profile' error state and does not attempt to call `getResearcherProfile`. When the `id` URL parameter changes (for example, via internal navigation or browser history), the page detects the change, resets visual components to a loading state, and calls `getResearcherProfile` again with the new `researcherId`. The follow/unfollow state is primarily derived from the latest `isFollowed` value returned either by `getResearcherProfile` or from the results of `followResearcher` / `unfollowResearcher`. After a successful `followResearcher` call, the page updates the internal follow state to true and may optionally reflect details from the returned `FollowedResearcher` object in confirmation messages; after a successful `unfollowResearcher` call, the follow state is set to false. The URL itself is not modified when follow state changes; it remains keyed solely on the `id` parameter so that reloading the page will always re-derive the latest follow state from `getResearcherProfile`. Additional navigation links construct new URLs with their own query parameters (e.g., `publications.html?query={researcher.fullName}`, `conferences.html?query={researcher.primaryResearchAreas[0]}`, `my_account.html?section=followed_researchers`) but do not affect the current page’s state until the user actually navigates. No state is stored in localStorage or any similar client-side storage; all persistent state is derived dynamically from interface responses and URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the `id` URL parameter, calling `getResearcherProfile` with that value as `researcherId`, and distributing the resulting data (researcher, selectedPublications, associatedConferences, isFollowed) and load/error states to child components.

Displays the researcher’s key identifying information, including full name, affiliation breakdown (department, institution, country), general country, profile image (if available), and optional contact links (email, website) sourced from the Researcher entity.
Shows the researcher’s biography text from the Researcher entity and handles empty-bio states if no biography is provided by the API.

Renders primary research areas and expertise keywords as structured tags using `primaryResearchAreas` and `expertiseKeywords` from the Researcher entity, handling cases where one or both arrays are empty.
Displays the researcher’s metrics, including h-index (required) and citation count (if available), based on the Researcher entity returned from `getResearcherProfile`.

Lists selected publications associated with the researcher using the `selectedPublications` array from `getResearcherProfile`. Each item can show title, year, and access information from the Publication entity and link to a separate publication detail page using the publication’s `id`. Provides a control to navigate to a full publications listing filtered by the researcher’s full name.

Lists conferences associated with the researcher using the `associatedConferences` array from `getResearcherProfile`. Each item can show key conference attributes from the Conference entity and link to a conference detail page using the conference’s `id`. Provides a control to navigate to the main conferences listing filtered by one of the researcher’s primary research areas.

Manages the follow/unfollow behavior for the current researcher. It reads the `isFollowed` value from `getResearcherProfile` to determine the initial state, provides an optional notes input, and invokes `followResearcher` or `unfollowResearcher` with the current `researcherId` derived from the URL. It updates follow state based on interface responses and exposes a navigation link to view all followed researchers.

Provides internal navigation links to scroll to or focus specific sections of the profile such as biography, expertise, publications, and conferences. Optionally updates the URL fragment without changing query parameters or triggering interface calls.

Displays contextual status messages for loading states, errors from `getResearcherProfile`, and success/error messages from `followResearcher` and `unfollowResearcher` in a unified area. It does not contain any hardcoded data content; all messages are derived from interface responses or generic functional text.

Controls the overall visibility of loading indicators and high-level error messages across the profile page, based on the status of the `getResearcherProfile` request and the presence of a valid `id` URL parameter.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header bands (info bar + main navigation), mirroring the mockup. All researcher profile components are stacked vertically in a linear, top-to-bottom sequence. There are no sidebars or multi-column layouts; any small horizontal structuring (e.g., metrics side by side) happens inside this main column. The researcher name functions as the focal heading, occupying the same visual role as the 'Jobs' title in the mockup, with subsequent sections flowing downwards.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header bands (info bar + main navigation), mirroring the mockup. All researcher profile components are stacked vertically in a linear, top-to-bottom sequence. There are no sidebars or multi-column layouts; any small horizontal structuring (e.g., metrics side by side) happens inside this main column. The researcher name functions as the focal heading, occupying the same visual role as the 'Jobs' title in the mockup, with subsequent sections flowing downwards.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header area (top info bar plus navigation) occupies the upper 15–20% of the viewport. Directly below, a generous white space buffer (roughly another 10–15% of viewport height) precedes the main researcher heading, preserving the mockup's sense of a large white canvas. The content column is relatively narrow, spanning about 50–60% of the viewport on desktop, capped at a comfortable max width (around 780–840px) and centered horizontally. Each section (bio, expertise, metrics, publications, conferences) is separated by large vertical margins, so the stacked blocks do not visually collide. The bottom of the page tapers off with whitespace after the final list, echoing the calm, open feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low by intentionally limiting the visible data in each block and relying on generous spacing and line breaks. Text sections avoid dense paragraph blocks; headings, labels, and key numbers are separated by whitespace similar to the numbered list in the mockup. Lists (publications and conferences) show a curated subset with ample vertical spacing between items, rather than compressed tables. Critical controls (follow, navigation links) are compact but not crammed; each control group sits in its own clearly separated horizontal band within the column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-to-vertical pattern inherited from the mockup: the eye first scans the dark global header from left (logo) through center (navigation) to right (icons/search). It then drops sharply into the white content area, landing on the centered researcher header as the primary anchor. From there, the flow is straightforward and vertical: status messages (if present) near the top of the column, then the researcher name and core identity info, followed by follow controls and the simple in-page navigation. Next, the eye moves down through metrics, bio, expertise, and finally into selected publications and associated conferences. Any red-accented elements (e.g., email/contact text in the bio) become the natural final call-to-action points at the bottom of the reading path.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the existing dark, full-width header, the screen opens into a broad white field. A relatively narrow, centered content column occupies about half the page width, surrounded by wide left and right margins that emphasize the text as the focal element. After a generous band of empty white space, a clean, centered researcher profile header appears, much like the 'Jobs' title in the original mockup but enriched with the researcher's name and affiliation. Just above or below this header, a slim horizontal status band may appear if there is a follow or loading message. Under the header, a compact strip contains the follow/unfollow controls and a simple text-based in-page navigation, both centered and separated by ample vertical spacing. Scrolling down, the content continues in discrete, well-separated sections that each fill only part of the vertical viewport: a metrics band with key academic numbers, a bio text block, a centered cloud of expertise tags, and two cleanly titled lists for selected publications and associated conferences. Each section is separated by generous white space so the overall impression is of a calm, minimal, text-centric page where everything aligns to a single central axis, echoing the conservative corporate-academic aesthetic of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears within the white content area directly below the global header, occupying the same centered column that the profile content uses. When active, it is vertically centered in the visible portion of the content area (roughly mid-screen below the header), with the spinner or loading indicator centered horizontally within the column. The loader's width is modest (around 25–30% of the column width), with plenty of whitespace above, below, and to the sides so it feels like a single, unobtrusive element floating in the white space. No other profile components are visible when this loader is in its primary loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a centered block inside the main content column, optionally overlaying or replacing the column's usual content while data is loading; static position relative to scroll (no fixed overlay beyond the content region).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The researcher header is the first substantive content element within the main content column. It sits roughly one content-column height below the bottom of the dark global header band, after a generous top margin of empty white space. It spans the full width of the centered column (about 50–60% of the viewport), but the key text is center-aligned to mirror the 'Jobs' heading in the mockup. The researcher's name appears as a prominent single line near the top of this block, with a slightly larger font size than other headings. Directly below, in one or two lines, appear the affiliation, title, and optionally location, using a smaller, regular-weight type. If an avatar or profile image is used at all, it should be small and placed above or just to the left of the name but still visually integrated so the overall block reads as vertically centered text rather than a left-heavy profile card. The header block occupies about 15–20% of the vertical portion of the content area above the fold, with comfortable padding above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main vertical flow, anchored at the top of the content column and moving with page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bio section appears below the metrics and in-page navigation band, occupying a clear, self-contained segment of the main column. It begins with a simple section heading such as 'Biography' that is center-aligned and spaced a full line-height below the component above it. The heading spans the column width but sits in a narrow strip of its own, with about one line-height of whitespace above and below. The body text of the bio is left-aligned for readability but constrained to the same narrow column, giving the impression of a long, centered text block framed by generous side margins. Paragraphs are short, and each paragraph is separated by visible vertical space roughly equivalent to a line-height and a half, echoing the mockup's generous spacing between numbered items. The whole bio section occupies about 20–25% of the page height on initial view if the bio is medium length, but the abundant spacing maintains the low-density feel even when the text is substantial.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text block in the main vertical flow; its height grows with content but it always maintains the same column width and centered alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The expertise panel sits directly below the bio section, separated by a vertical gap roughly equivalent to the height of two lines of text. It begins with a small, center-aligned heading such as 'Expertise' or 'Research Areas'. Under this heading, the tags are displayed as simple, unobtrusive pill-shaped or underlined textual elements arranged in one or more rows, but always centered horizontally within the column. The tag block spans no more than 70–80% of the column width, leaving small buffers on either side, and the tags themselves are spaced with generous horizontal and vertical gaps so they do not create visual clutter. The entire expertise block is moderately shallow, occupying around 10–15% of the visible content area if the number of tags is typical, with significant white space above and below so it reads as a light, informational band rather than a dense cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that flows with the page; tags wrap onto new rows as needed within the fixed-width column while remaining center-aligned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metrics panel is positioned just below the researcher header and follow controls, and above the bio section, acting as a concise summary band. It spans the full width of the main content column but uses a simple internal grid of 2–4 equally spaced metrics (e.g., h-index, total citations) aligned along a single horizontal line. Each metric is presented as a large numeric value with a smaller label beneath, both center-aligned within their own narrow column. The entire metrics band is only as tall as needed for two lines of text plus padding, and it is surrounded by generous whitespace above and below—similar to the separation between lines in the numbered list on the mockup—so it feels like a clean, calm strip of numbers rather than a dashboard. On very narrow screens, the metrics can stack vertically, maintaining the same generous spacing between each value.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static strip within the main column; internally it uses a simple horizontal layout that gracefully wraps to multiple rows on smaller viewports, but the block itself remains centered and non-sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The selected publications list appears below the expertise panel, separated by a vertical gap roughly equivalent to the height of a medium-sized heading plus one line of space. A section title such as 'Selected Publications' is center-aligned at the top of this block, similar in visual weight to the 'Jobs' heading but slightly smaller than the main researcher name. The list items are arranged as a vertical, left-aligned list within the centered column, each publication occupying its own block of 2–3 lines with title and basic citation details. There is generous vertical spacing—approximately one to one-and-a-half line-heights—between each publication, creating a calm, low-density list. The list itself shows only a curated subset (for example, 5–10 items) to avoid overwhelming the column. If there is a 'View all publications' link, it appears as a simple text link centered beneath the list with extra space above it, acting as a subtle call-to-action rather than a prominent button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list that grows with content but stays within the same centered column; scrollable with the rest of the page with no special sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The associated conferences list follows the selected publications section, separated by a vertical gap comparable to the space between other major sections (about the height of a short paragraph). It starts with a center-aligned heading such as 'Associated Conferences' or 'Related Events'. Below the heading, individual conferences are listed vertically in the same narrow, centered column. Each conference entry is left-aligned and kept visually light: a title line, optional year or role, and perhaps one brief descriptor line. Like the publications list, conferences are spaced apart vertically to preserve low density, and the total number shown is limited (e.g., 3–8 entries). The entire section is shorter than the publications list in typical cases, occupying a modest portion of the page height toward the bottom. Any link to explore more conferences sits centered below the list, separated by extra whitespace to echo the mockup’s final call-to-action positioning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the main flow; no sticky behavior, scrolling off-screen as the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The follow controls are located immediately below the researcher header block, within the same centered column. They are arranged in a compact horizontal strip that does not exceed the column width and is itself center-aligned. Typical elements include a primary follow/unfollow button and possibly a secondary text link such as 'Add to Followed Researchers' or 'View Followed List'. The primary control may be more visually emphasized but still restrained—matching the conservative aesthetic—without large colorful buttons. There is generous vertical spacing: a gap of roughly one line-height between the bottom of the researcher header text and the controls, and another similar gap before any metrics or navigation components below, so the controls feel like a discrete decision point rather than being crowded by text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static action strip within the main column, positioned in the normal document flow. It does not stick to the top on scroll; it scrolls away with the header content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in-page navigation sits close to the top of the content column, just below the follow controls and above the metrics or bio, acting as a subtle map of the profile sections. It appears as a thin, horizontal row of text links (e.g., 'Overview', 'Publications', 'Conferences') centered within the column. Each link is separated by ample horizontal spacing or simple dividers, ensuring the row feels airy and uncluttered. The nav row occupies only a small vertical slice—about the height of a single line of text plus padding—but has clear top and bottom margins so it is visually isolated as its own band. On narrow screens it can wrap to two lines, still centered. The visual style remains understated; it reads as a typographic element more than a tabbed widget, aligning with the academic, text-first style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky navigation row within the main column. Clicking links scrolls the page to sections, but the navigation itself scrolls naturally with content and does not fix to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Status messages appear as a slim banner within the main content column near the very top of the researcher profile content. When present, this banner is placed either directly above the researcher header or immediately below it, depending on the message type, but always within the same centered column. The banner spans the full column width and has a slightly shaded or bordered background to distinguish it from the white canvas, yet it remains low-profile—its height is only slightly more than a single line of text. Text is center-aligned for consistency with other top-of-page elements. There is clear vertical spacing above and below the banner so it does not touch the header text or the following blocks, maintaining the open, low-density feel. When no status is active, this area collapses, leaving only white space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically within the normal flow of the column. It does not overlay content or stick to the viewport; instead, it pushes adjacent content down when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state controller governs the overall presentation of loading and error states across the profile page. Visually, when an error state is active, it replaces the usual profile content within the main centered column with a prominent error banner similar in width to the status messages but taller and with more padding, sitting in the same location where the loader or header would normally appear. The error message is center-aligned and surrounded by ample whitespace to echo the calm design language. In loading states, it triggers the researcher-profile-loader component to appear in the middle of the white content area, keeping the dark header untouched. Once data is available, it hides both loader and error banners, allowing the standard stacked layout (header, controls, sections) to occupy the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a controller that conditionally shows or hides the loader and error banners within the main column. Visually, the elements it controls are static within the normal scroll flow, never fixed to the viewport; the controller itself has no visible footprint when not in a special state.

name: Publications & Papers
title: Publications & Papers
description: Search, filter, and browse publications, reports, and academic papers, with options to refine by year, access type, and journal impact factor, sort results, open detailed records, and manage a personal reading list.
