At the very bottom of the content column, a small, centered support link sits alone, surrounded by white space, echoing the restrained, academic tone of the original mockup. The overall impression is a spacious, text-driven page that guides the user from search to results in a straightforward vertical flow, without visual noise or complex layouts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates invisibly at the very top of the main content region, just below the global header. It does not render any UI but conceptually anchors the search experience, initializing state before the visible title and search form appear. It occupies no explicit height or width in the layout; surrounding components collapse around it so the white content area appears uninterrupted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; inline with the top of the main content stack and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This loader is also non-visual and sits logically just above the search form in the DOM, preparing filter options before the form is displayed. Like the initializer, it does not consume visible space; the white canvas flows continuously from the bottom edge of the dark header down to the page title and search form without any visible indication of this component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; inline with content flow, with no effect on spacing or alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event search form is the first clearly interactive element in the main content column. It is positioned slightly below a centered 'Events & Workshops' page heading (assumed to be part of this section), with roughly one to one-and-a-half line heights of white space between the heading and the form. The form itself is horizontally centered and spans about 60–65% of the overall page width on desktop, leaving substantial left and right margins. Its primary search input stretches most of this width as a single, prominent line of text, with any additional inputs (date range, type, location) stacked beneath on separate lines rather than side-by-side, maintaining a low-density, academic feel. There is generous vertical padding within the form area and at least one full line height of white space above and below the form before the next elements appear. The entire form block feels like a compact, centered island of interaction surrounded by white space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column; scrolls with the page and does not stick to the top, preserving the simple, non-dynamic feel of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied filters bar sits directly beneath the search form, aligned to the same centered content column. There is a modest gap—about half a line height—to clearly separate it from the form. The bar spans the same width as the form (around 60–65% of the page width), and its content is left-aligned within that column while the block itself remains centered on the page. If filter chips or tags are used, they appear in a single line that wraps cleanly as needed, with ample horizontal spacing between chips and generous vertical padding above and below the text. The bar is visually light: no heavy colored background, perhaps only a subtle light-gray underline or none at all, ensuring it doesn’t overpower the form or results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned as a simple block in the vertical flow without sticky behavior; it moves out of view as the user scrolls down through the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results status (e.g., '23 events found') is placed just beneath the applied filters bar, within the same centered column and left-aligned to its text baseline. It sits with a comfortable vertical gap (roughly half a line height) above and below, making it easy to spot without crowding other elements. On wider screens, it can share a horizontal row with the sort controls, occupying the left side of this row, but still respecting the narrow content column. The typography is understated—regular weight, modest size—so it informs the user without becoming a focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; inline with surrounding components, forming a horizontal pairing with sort controls on larger screens and stacking above them on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort controls sit at the same vertical level as the results status on desktop, aligned to the right edge of the centered content column. Together they form a single horizontal row: status on the left, sort on the right, with ample white space between. Within the column, the sort control group is modest in size, using a simple text label and a dropdown or basic links. There is generous top and bottom padding for this entire row (about one line height above and below), preserving the airy feel. On narrower screens, the sort controls naturally wrap beneath the status text and center-align within the column, maintaining the single-column hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; aligned to the right within the content column on larger screens, but otherwise part of the standard scrolling flow without fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This loader appears in place of or over the event results list area when data is being fetched. Visually, it is centered both horizontally within the content column and vertically within the portion of the viewport where the list would normally begin. It might be represented by a simple text message ('Loading events…') or a subtle spinner, with no heavy overlay or darkening of the background. The loader occupies roughly the same width as the results list (about 60–70% of the page width) but uses minimal height, surrounded by generous white space so the page remains calm even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally within the results list region; it scrolls with the page and does not cover the header or search area, avoiding modal or fixed-overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event results list starts just below the results status and sort controls row, separated by about one line height of white space. It is a single vertical column of event items, centered on the page and spanning roughly 60–70% of the page width, echoing the search form width. There are no side-by-side event cards; each event occupies the full width of this column. The list extends downward as needed, with each item separated by generous vertical gaps—at least equal to the height of one line of body text—so the list reads as a calm sequence rather than a dense grid. There may be only minimal visual separators (e.g., a very light horizontal rule or just whitespace) between items to maintain the academic, text-first aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable vertical list within the central column, with no sticky or floating behavior; it grows in height as more results are displayed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each event result item is a flat, text-centric row within the results list. It spans the full width of the list column (around 60–70% of the page width, centered) and consists primarily of text: a bold event title at the top, with date, location, and brief description stacked beneath on separate lines. There is ample internal vertical padding—about 16–24px above the title and below the last line—to create a comfortable reading block. Items are separated from each other by generous vertical whitespace and optionally a very subtle divider line. There are no heavy borders or card shadows; the design relies on alignment and spacing to distinguish items. Any key action, such as a 'View details' text link, is aligned left with the content and styled simply, blending with the conservative aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level element within the list; it expands in height based on content and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit below the last event result item, with a sizable vertical gap above—roughly the height of one or two event items—to clearly separate navigation from content. The controls are horizontally centered within the same content column and occupy a relatively small vertical space. Buttons or page numbers are laid out in a simple, linear row with moderate spacing between them, avoiding dense clusters or elaborate pagination widgets. Surrounding white space above and below ensures the pagination feels unobtrusive and easy to scan, rather than visually heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; fixed in the vertical flow at the bottom of the results list. As the user navigates between pages, the control remains in this position relative to the list, without sticking to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events support link appears at the very bottom of the main content column, acting as a quiet final call-to-action. It is centered horizontally and separated from the pagination controls by generous vertical whitespace—at least one full viewport fold on shorter lists or a solid block of white space equivalent to two or three line heights. The link text is small to medium in size, matching body text styling but may use a subtle accent color (e.g., the same red used for contact emails on the Jobs page) to stand out against the otherwise minimalist layout. It sits alone in this lower region, surrounded by white space, reinforcing the calm and understated character of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; positioned at the end of the content flow and scrolls offscreen like any other text, with no sticky or floating behavior.

name: Event / Workshop Detail
title: Event / Workshop Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected event or workshop and provides a flow to register or pre-register (including guest registration) for a specific time slot with a chosen payment option.

core_features: Fetch and display detailed event information based on the URL parameter `id` using the `getEventDetail` interface.
Show all available time slots for the event and allow the user to select one for registration.
Indicate registration configuration (guest allowed, pre-registration only, available payment options) based on `registrationInfo` from `getEventDetail`.
Provide a registration flow that supports guest and account-based registration types, depending on configuration and user choice.
Capture user information (name, email, affiliation) and required registration metadata (time slot, registrationType, paymentOption, isPreRegistration) and submit it via `submitEventRegistration`.
Display submission feedback (success or error) using the response from `submitEventRegistration`.
Offer navigation back to the main events list and to the contact page for registration assistance.
user_workflows: View event details: (1) User opens URL `event_detail.html?id={eventId}` from events listing or homepage; (2) Page reads `id` parameter and calls `getEventDetail` with it; (3) On success, page displays event title, description, date/time information, location, topics, and base pricing info derived from `event`; (4) Page also lists all `timeSlots` and shows registration configuration from `registrationInfo`.

Select a time slot for registration: (1) User scrolls to the registration section; (2) If multiple time slots exist, user selects one from a list; (3) The selected `timeSlotId` is stored in page state and used to update any contextual hints (e.g., displaying the selected slot’s date and time near the form); (4) If a `slot` URL parameter is present and matches one of the available time slots, that slot is pre-selected.

Start guest or account-based registration: (1) In the registration panel, user chooses a registration type. If `registrationInfo.canRegisterAsGuest` is true, a 'Guest / Register without account' option is enabled; (2) If the platform indicates the user is signed in (handled outside this page’s interfaces), an 'Account' registration option can also be offered; (3) The chosen `registrationType` is stored in page state and will be sent to `submitEventRegistration`.

Choose payment preference: (1) Registration section lists only those payment options present in `registrationInfo.availablePaymentOptions`; (2) User selects a payment option (e.g., one of the codes defined in `EventRegistration.paymentOption`); (3) If `registrationInfo.isPreRegistrationOnly` is true, the UI clarifies this and may disable or hide payment options that conflict with a pre-registration-only flow; (4) Selected `paymentOption` is stored in page state.

Submit registration: (1) User fills in full name, email, and optionally affiliation in the registration form; (2) User ensures a time slot and payment option are selected; (3) User clicks 'Submit registration' (or equivalent); (4) The page validates required fields and, if valid, calls `submitEventRegistration` with `eventId` (from URL), selected `timeSlotId` (if any), `registrationType`, `fullName`, `email`, `affiliation`, chosen `paymentOption`, and `isPreRegistration` (derived from `registrationInfo.isPreRegistrationOnly` or user choice if both are possible); (5) On success, the page displays the returned confirmation message and basic status from the `registration` object; (6) On error, the page displays an appropriate error message without losing the form input.

Contact for help or go back: (1) If the user needs help, they click 'Contact us about this workshop'; (2) The page navigates to `contact.html?topic=conferences`; (3) Alternatively, user can click 'Back to all workshops & events' to navigate to `events.html` without additional parameters.
interactions: On initial load, read the `id` URL parameter; if present, trigger a data-load action using `getEventDetail(eventId=id)`; if missing or the call fails, display an appropriate error state instead of the event details and registration form.
Click on 'Back to all workshops & events' triggers navigation to `events.html`.
Click on 'Contact us about this workshop' triggers navigation to `contact.html?topic=conferences`.
Changing the selected time slot in the time slot selector updates the currently chosen `timeSlotId` in page state and updates any contextual details about the selection.

Changing the registration type (guest/account) updates the `registrationType` in page state and may adjust which personal information fields are editable or pre-filled, depending on account context (if available).
Changing the selected payment option updates `paymentOption` in page state and may show or hide any explanatory text related to that option.
Typing into name, email, or affiliation fields updates the corresponding values in page state bound to the registration form.

Submitting the registration form validates user input (e.g., presence and format of email, presence of name, time slot, and payment option where required); if validation passes, the form is disabled and `submitEventRegistration` is called; the result then updates a confirmation or error message component.

If the selected payment option implies any follow-up action outside this page (e.g., online payment), that follow-up can be initiated after the `submitEventRegistration` call succeeds, using information returned in the `registration` object and external logic (not defined in this spec).
page_functionality.state_logic: The core state of this page is controlled by URL parameters and transient in-page state. The required URL parameter `id` holds the event identifier and is used directly as `eventId` when calling `getEventDetail` and `submitEventRegistration`. If the `id` parameter is missing or invalid, the page shows an error state and does not initialize the registration flow. An optional `slot` URL parameter may be used to pre-select a time slot in the registration section; if present, the page attempts to match this parameter with one of the `timeSlots` returned by `getEventDetail` and sets the initial `timeSlotId` to that value; if no match is found, no slot is pre-selected. Additional view-related state (such as which panel is expanded: details vs. registration vs. confirmation) is maintained in memory within the page and can optionally be synced to a `view` URL parameter (e.g., `view=register` to open the registration section by default), but no persistent storage such as localStorage or sessionStorage is used. All business-critical submissions always rely on the `id` parameter for `eventId` rather than on any stored client-side values.

components.functionality: Reads the `id` URL parameter, calls `getEventDetail` with it, manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded `event`, `timeSlots`, and `registrationInfo` to child components.
Displays high-level event information such as title and event type derived from the loaded `event` data, along with key metadata like date range and organizer.

Shows structured event details (date and duration derived from `startDate`, `endDate`, `durationType`; location and format from `locationType`, `city`, `country`; topics from `topics`; base fee from `baseFeeAmount` and `baseFeeCurrency`).

Lists all available `timeSlots` with their labels and start/end times as the event's agenda or schedule reference; highlights the currently selected time slot if one is chosen in the registration section.

Provides an interactive control within the registration area for choosing one of the available time slots (or confirming the only time slot, if there is just one). Maintains and exposes the selected `timeSlotId` to the registration form.

Summarizes registration options and constraints for the event, such as whether guest registration is allowed (`canRegisterAsGuest`), which payment options are available (`availablePaymentOptions`), and whether the event supports only pre-registration (`isPreRegistrationOnly`).

Allows the user to choose between 'guest' and 'account' registration types, respecting the `canRegisterAsGuest` flag and any higher-level indication of account availability; updates the `registrationType` state used by the registration form.

Displays the list of available payment options derived from `registrationInfo.availablePaymentOptions` and allows the user to select one; the selected option is bound to the registration form’s `paymentOption` field.

Captures user registration data (fullName, email, affiliation) along with the currently selected time slot, registration type, payment option, and pre-registration flag, and submits this data via `submitEventRegistration`.
Shows feedback after a registration attempt, including the `message` returned from `submitEventRegistration` and key fields from the resulting `registration` object such as status.
Provides navigation links for 'Back to all workshops & events' and 'Contact us about this workshop', triggering navigation to `events.html` and `contact.html?topic=conferences` respectively.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: All event components sit in a single, vertically stacked main content column centered beneath the existing full-width dark header bands (phone bar + navigation). The column follows a strict top-to-bottom order with no sidebars: event title and meta at the top, then agenda and time slot selection, followed by registration configuration, registration controls (type + payment), the registration form, status/result messaging, and finally support links at the bottom. Alignment favors centered headings and summary copy, with form labels and choices aligned in a simple stacked manner inside the same central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All event components sit in a single, vertically stacked main content column centered beneath the existing full-width dark header bands (phone bar + navigation). The column follows a strict top-to-bottom order with no sidebars: event title and meta at the top, then agenda and time slot selection, followed by registration configuration, registration controls (type + payment), the registration form, status/result messaging, and finally support links at the bottom. Alignment favors centered headings and summary copy, with form labels and choices aligned in a simple stacked manner inside the same central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Below it, a wide expanse of white space leads to the event title and details in a narrow, fixed-width content column (e.g., max-width ~720px) centered on the page. Each component block takes up a relatively small portion of the vertical space and is separated by generous white space (approximately 32–64px between major sections), keeping the content cluster visually compact within a large white canvas. Side margins are substantial on large screens, reinforcing a narrow central reading column and a calm, uncluttered impression.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse. Text and controls are laid out with ample vertical breathing room, avoiding multi-column forms or dense tables. Lists (such as agenda timeslots) are presented as simple vertical stacks with large gaps between items. Form fields are arranged in a single column, one field per row, to maintain low density and reduce visual noise. Decorative elements are minimal; emphasis comes from spacing, simple typographic hierarchy, and occasional color accents (e.g., red for key contact or action text).
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the eye first locks onto the dark global header and navigation, it is drawn down by the stark contrast into the white content area and immediately to the centered event title. From there, the visual flow is a straightforward vertical reading path: title → meta information → agenda/timeslots → time-slot selection → registration configuration note → registration type and payment options → registration form → result banner → support links. Center-aligned headings and short text blocks act as visual anchors, while red-accented contact or confirmation text at the bottom provides a clear final call-to-action or reassurance, echoing the mockup's flow ending on red email addresses.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s dark, full-width header bands, the page opens into a broad white field. Centered within this field is a relatively narrow content column that never stretches edge-to-edge, creating wide left and right margins. The event title appears first, slightly lower than you’d expect, with generous empty space above it that emphasizes the transition from the dark header to the white body. Directly under the title, compact lines of meta information sit centered, followed by a vertically spaced list of agenda times and a simple time-slot selector—each element occupying a clean stripe of the central column with noticeable white gaps between. As you scroll down, the registration configuration message, toggles for registration type, payment choices, and then the registration form itself appear one after another, each block separated by consistent vertical spacing so nothing feels crowded. The form fields are stacked in a single column, with a centered submit button, preserving the text-centric, conservative feel. If a registration result appears, it shows up as a simple banner in the same column, not as a pop-up, and the flow ends with small, center-aligned support links and red-highlighted contact emails near the bottom. Overall, the layout mirrors a calm academic document on a large sheet of white paper: a tight cluster of essential information in the center framed by generous, empty margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the event-detail-loader occupies the central content column area directly beneath the global dark header, temporarily replacing the event content. It spans roughly 60–70% of the page width (matching the main column max-width, centered) and is vertically centered within the visible white area, so it appears about mid-screen on desktop. The loader’s indicator and text are centered both horizontally and vertically within this block, with large margins to the left and right and clear white space above and below, preserving the low-density, calm feel even while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay/placeholder for the main content column; while active, it sits fixed within the scrolling context (not fixed to the viewport), occupying the same space where the event-summary-header and subsequent components will appear once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-summary-header is the first visible component in the white content area below the global header. It appears as a prominent, center-aligned title block positioned with a generous top margin of roughly 96–128px from the bottom edge of the navigation band, mimicking the mockup’s ‘Jobs’ heading placement. The title text spans about 70–80% of the main content column width, with no surrounding box or border, and is centered horizontally within the page. A modest amount of white space (around 24–32px) separates the title from the event-meta-info that follows, clearly marking it as the primary focal point of the page body.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element flowing with the document; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-meta-info block sits directly beneath the event-summary-header within the same centered column, separated by a comfortable vertical gap. It occupies the same width as the title (approximately 60–70% of page width, capped at a fixed max-width) and is also centered horizontally. Information such as date, time, location, and organizer appears as 2–5 lines of text, each line left-aligned within the narrow column but visually centered on the page. Line spacing is generous, similar to a formatted abstract, with about 8–12px between lines and 24–32px of white space separating this block from the agenda list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block within the vertical flow; no special spatial behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-agenda-timeslots list appears directly under the event-meta-info, following the same narrow, centered column. It uses the mockup’s numbered-list feel: each agenda item or timeslot is displayed on its own line (or compact 2–3 line cluster) with a simple leading number or time marker, centered as a whole within the column. Items do not sit tightly together; instead, each agenda entry is separated by generous vertical spacing (about 24–40px between items), creating a low-density vertical rhythm. The entire agenda block occupies roughly 25–35% of the visible content height, but with ample white space above and below, so it feels like a list floating in the middle of the white canvas. A slightly larger gap (around 32–48px) below the final agenda item visually separates it from the time-slot-selector that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block list; scrolls naturally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-slot-selector sits immediately below the agenda list, still within the same central column and centered horizontally. It occupies about 50–60% of the page width on desktop, never extending to the edges. The selector might be a dropdown or a simple vertical list of radio options; either way, its label is placed above the control, with both elements stacked in a single column. The label is center-aligned, while the control itself can be either centered or slightly left-aligned within the column, but always contained within the narrow central band. There is a comfortable top margin (24–32px) from the agenda and a similar margin below (24–32px) before the registration-configuration-banner, so the selector feels like its own small, self-contained step in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block component that flows with the page; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-configuration-banner appears directly under the time-slot-selector as a single, slim information band within the central column. It spans the full width of the main content column (around 60–70% of the page width), centered horizontally, with its text center-aligned for emphasis. The banner height is modest—roughly the height of 1–2 text lines plus padding—making it visually similar to a sentence set apart by white space rather than a heavy card. There is a clear buffer of white space (around 16–24px) above and below the banner, keeping it visually distinct from both the selector above and the toggles below, while maintaining the calm, uncluttered feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline banner; appears/disappears in place and shifts surrounding content but does not overlay or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-type-toggle sits beneath the registration-configuration-banner, centered in the same narrow column. It occupies approximately 40–60% of the page width, aligned centrally. The label or heading for this toggle is center-aligned and placed above the control, with 8–16px spacing between. The toggle options (for example, "Register myself" vs "Register a guest") appear as horizontally arranged buttons or pills, but the group itself remains centered, with ample white space around it so it feels like a simple binary choice rather than a dense control bar. A vertical margin of roughly 24–32px below separates it from the payment-option-selector, visually grouping both as part of a single registration-configuration step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level selector; no special spatial behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-option-selector is positioned directly below the registration-type-toggle within the central column, maintaining the same narrow width (about 60–70% of page width, centered). The section begins with a center-aligned label or heading, then presents payment options as a simple vertical stack of radio buttons or a dropdown, keeping to the text-centric style. If radios are used, each option is left-aligned within the column but the entire group remains centered on the page. Vertical spacing between options is moderate (12–16px) to avoid crowding. A larger vertical gap of 32–40px below this selector separates it clearly from the more extensive event-registration-form, signaling a shift from configuration to data entry.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block selector; scrolls with content, no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-registration-form forms the largest continuous block in the central column and is placed directly beneath the payment-option-selector. It adheres to the conservative, low-density aesthetic: all fields are stacked in a single column, spanning roughly 60–70% of the page width (matching the column) and centered. Each field has its label above the input, with generous vertical padding (12–20px) between fields, avoiding side-by-side controls where possible. The form’s top is separated from the payment options by around 32–40px, and there is ample whitespace below the final field before the submit button (16–24px). The submit button is centered horizontally at the bottom of the form block and may use the accent color (e.g., red for text or border) to echo the mockup’s red email addresses. The entire form area, including the button, typically takes up 35–50% of the viewport height on desktop, but is visually anchored within a larger white space above and below so it doesn’t feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard form section within the document flow; no sticky behavior, but its height adjusts fluidly based on the number of fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-result-banner appears within the main content column as a simple, text-centric feedback strip. Upon submission, it is positioned immediately above the event-registration-form (or directly below the payment-option-selector if more logically aligned with the configuration step) so that it is encountered as soon as the user scrolls back to the form area. It spans the full width of the central column and is horizontally centered. The message text is center-aligned and may use a subtle colored text or accent (such as red for errors or muted green for success), reminiscent of the mockup’s red email highlight, but without heavy borders or icons. Vertical margins of about 16–24px above and below the banner keep it visually separated from adjacent components while preserving the overall clean look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline message banner that appears and disappears in place, pushing content down or collapsing without overlaying other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-support-links component is the closing block of the page’s main content column, echoing the final center-aligned paragraph with red email addresses in the mockup. It sits below the event-registration-form (and any active registration-result-banner) with a generous top margin of roughly 48–72px, giving a clear sense that it is a concluding section. The links—such as support email, help pages, or policies—are presented as one or two short lines of text, centered within the narrow column and spanning about 60–70% of the page width. Emails or key contact items are highlighted in red to match the mockup’s visual cue. A large bottom margin (80–120px) separates these links from the site footer area, allowing them to sit in a small pocket of white space at the bottom of the layout, functioning as the final visual and interactive element in the user’s vertical reading path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level link group at the end of the document flow; remains static and scrollable with the page.

name: Newsletter Subscriptions
title: Newsletter Subscriptions
description: Manage newsletter email subscriptions by selecting topics, setting per-topic frequency and email format, and saving or updating preferences using an email address.

core_features: Load available newsletter topics, frequency options, and format options using getNewsletterTopics, and expose them for selection without hardcoded topic or option values.
Capture an email address either from the URL parameter or via an input form, and use it to look up existing newsletter subscriptions with getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail.

Display a per-topic preferences matrix that lets users opt in or out of each available topic and choose a frequency and format for each selected topic based on the options returned by getNewsletterTopics.
Create or update newsletter subscriptions for the entered email by sending the aggregated per-topic preferences to updateNewsletterSubscriptions and reflecting the updated subscriptions on the page.
Offer navigation back to the homepage (index.html) after saving preferences via a dedicated control.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load with optional email parameter
1. User opens newsletters.html, optionally with an email URL parameter.
2. The page controller reads the email parameter, if present, and sets it as the current email value in the email input.
3. The page calls getNewsletterTopics to retrieve the list of topics, available frequencies, and formats, and stores them in page state.
4. If an email parameter was present and is non-empty, the page automatically calls getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail with that email.
5. The returned subscriptions are merged with the topic list to build an editable per-topic preferences state (subscribed flag, frequency, format) for display in the topic preferences table.

Workflow: Manually enter or change email to manage preferences
1. User focuses the email input and types a valid email address or edits an existing one.
2. User clicks a 'Load preferences' action or an equivalent control tied to the email-capture form.
3. The page validates that the email is non-empty and syntactically valid; if invalid, an inline error is shown and no API call is made.
4. On success, the page calls getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail with the entered email and shows a loading state for the topic preferences area.
5. When subscriptions are returned, the page updates the per-topic editable state so that topics with active subscriptions are marked as selected and prefilled with their existing frequency and format; other topics are initialized with default frequency and format derived from NewsletterTopic.defaultFrequency and the lists from getNewsletterTopics.
6. The topic preferences table is rendered or refreshed with this state for further editing.

Workflow: Configure per-topic subscription preferences
1. With topics loaded and an email present, the user reviews the topic preferences table listing all available topics.
2. For each topic, the user can toggle a control (such as a checkbox or switch) that marks the topic as subscribed (selected) or unsubscribed (not selected).
3. When a topic is marked as subscribed, the row’s frequency selector becomes active and presents frequency options from getNewsletterTopics.frequencyOptions; the user picks one option, and the selection is stored in the editable state for that topic.
4. Similarly, when a topic is marked as subscribed, the row’s format selector becomes active and presents format options from getNewsletterTopics.formatOptions; the user picks one option, and the selection is stored in the editable state for that topic.
5. When a topic is marked as unsubscribed, its frequency and format values remain in state but are not considered active; these values can still be reused if the topic is re-subscribed before saving.
6. Changes are tracked client-side as unsaved until the user explicitly saves.

Workflow: Save or update subscriptions
1. After configuring preferences, the user clicks a 'Save preferences' action in the actions bar.
2. The page validates that an email is present and valid and that topics have been loaded; if validation fails, error messages are shown and the save is aborted.
3. The page constructs a subscriptions payload from the editable state: for each topic, it creates a subscription update object containing the topic code, selected frequency, selected format, and a flag representing whether the topic is active for this email. Topics that were previously subscribed but are now deselected are represented with isActive=false so they can be deactivated.
4. The page calls updateNewsletterSubscriptions with the current email and the constructed subscriptions payload and shows a saving state.
5. On success, the page updates local subscription state using the returned updatedSubscriptions, re-merges them with the topics to reflect the authoritative saved values, and displays a success message using the returned message.
6. The URL is optionally updated to include or replace the email parameter with the saved email value so the state can be restored on page reload without localStorage.
7. A 'Return to homepage' navigation control becomes available (or remains enabled) for the user to go back to index.html.

Workflow: Reset or reload preferences for the same email
1. If the user makes changes but wants to discard them before saving, they click a 'Reset' or 'Reload' control in the actions bar.
2. The page uses the current email value to call getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail again.
3. On success, it rebuilds the editable per-topic state from the response and topic list, discarding any unsaved local changes.
4. The topic preferences table is refreshed to match the stored server preferences.

Workflow: Navigate back to homepage after managing subscriptions
1. After saving preferences (or at any time if permitted by design), the user clicks the 'Return to homepage' control.
2. The page performs a navigation to index.html without additional parameters, as specified by the outgoing navigation definition.
interactions: Email input interaction: User types in the email field; on change, the page updates the in-memory email state and clears any error messages that are tied to an earlier email validation.

Load preferences action: User clicks a button associated with the email-capture form (or submits the form via the Enter key). Event handler validates the email and, if valid, calls getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail and updates the topic preferences state when data returns.

Per-topic subscription toggle: For each topic row, user toggles a 'subscribe' control. When turned on, frequency and format selectors for that topic are enabled. When turned off, the topic is marked as inactive while retaining its last chosen frequency and format in the editable state.

Per-topic frequency selection: For a subscribed topic, user opens a frequency selector populated with getNewsletterTopics.frequencyOptions and selects a value. The chosen value is stored in the editable state for that topic and later used when constructing the subscriptions payload.

Per-topic format selection: For a subscribed topic, user opens a format selector populated with getNewsletterTopics.formatOptions and selects a value. The chosen value is stored in the editable state for that topic and later used when constructing the subscriptions payload.

Save preferences submission: User clicks a 'Save preferences' control. The page validates the email and topic state, constructs the subscriptions payload, and calls updateNewsletterSubscriptions. On success, it shows a confirmation message and syncs local state with updatedSubscriptions; on error, it shows an appropriate error message.

Reset preferences: User clicks a 'Reset' or 'Reload from server' control. The page discards unsaved changes and calls getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail again with the current email to repopulate the topic preferences state.

Status messages: Throughout loading, saving, or error states, a status component updates to reflect the current state (e.g., loading topics, loading subscriptions, save successful, or error when a call fails).
Homepage navigation: User clicks a 'Return to homepage' link or button which triggers navigation to index.html without URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page state is driven by the URL parameter 'email' and the results of the assigned interfaces. On initial load, the page controller inspects the current URL for an 'email' parameter. If present and non-empty, the value initializes the email input field and is immediately used to call getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail after getNewsletterTopics returns. If there is no 'email' parameter, the email input starts empty, and existing subscriptions are not fetched until the user provides an email and triggers the load or save action.

The topics, frequency options, and format options are always loaded via getNewsletterTopics on page initialization; no topic names, frequencies, or formats are hardcoded. The topics array from getNewsletterTopics, combined with the subscriptions array from getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail for the current email, is transformed into an internal per-topic editable state structure. This state tracks, for each topic, whether the user currently intends to subscribe (active flag), the selected frequency, the selected format, and whether there was an existing subscription for that topic. This editable state is not persisted to localStorage; it exists only in memory while the page is open.

When the user changes the email in the input field, the internal email state updates but does not automatically alter the URL or subscriptions state until the user explicitly triggers loading or saving. Upon successful save with updateNewsletterSubscriptions, the page may update the URL to reflect the current email by setting or replacing the 'email' parameter, so subsequent reloads or direct links rehydrate the same preferences using getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail. Navigation back to index.html uses a simple location change to index.html without adding URL parameters, as defined in the outgoing navigation specification.

All changes to subscription preferences are considered unsaved until updateNewsletterSubscriptions completes successfully. Resetting preferences for a given email uses the current email state and reiterates getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail; no historic state is stored beyond what is returned by the APIs and what is encoded in the URL parameter.

components.functionality: Orchestrates page initialization, reads the 'email' URL parameter, calls getNewsletterTopics, conditionally calls getNewsletterSubscriptionsForEmail when an email is available, maintains in-memory state for topics and editable per-topic preferences, and coordinates calls to updateNewsletterSubscriptions.

Captures the email address used to identify the subscription set. Initializes from the 'email' URL parameter when available, validates user input, and triggers loading of existing subscriptions or saving of preferences using the current email.

Displays all available newsletter topics returned by getNewsletterTopics and a per-topic row of controls for subscription status, frequency, and format. Merges topic data with current editable subscription state to show whether the user is subscribed to each topic and which frequency and format are selected. Updates internal state whenever the user toggles a subscription or changes frequency or format.

Provides actions to save the current preferences, reset to the server state for the current email, and, after saving or when appropriate, navigate back to the homepage. Orchestrates validation and triggers the appropriate API calls through the page controller.

Shows feedback about current operations and results, such as loading topics, loading subscriptions, save in progress, save success, or API error messages. Consumes messages and status flags emitted by the page controller and displays them contextually near the email form and topic preferences table.

Keeps the URL 'email' parameter in sync with the currently active email used for subscriptions management. On initial load, it reads the parameter. After a successful save with updateNewsletterSubscriptions, it updates the URL to reflect the current email without reloading the page, allowing bookmarking and revisiting the same preferences later.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly linear, top-to-bottom layout with a single, centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header (info bar + nav). The header remains unchanged and occupies the top 15–20% of the viewport. Directly below the header, a large white area introduces the page with a centered "Newsletter Subscriptions" heading controlled by the page controller. Under this heading, components stack in this order: a short explanatory line, the email capture form, the topic preferences table, the subscription actions bar, and finally the subscription status panel near the bottom of the content column. No sidebars, no multi-column content within the main flow; everything reads as one central vertical narrative.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly linear, top-to-bottom layout with a single, centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header (info bar + nav). The header remains unchanged and occupies the top 15–20% of the viewport. Directly below the header, a large white area introduces the page with a centered "Newsletter Subscriptions" heading controlled by the page controller. Under this heading, components stack in this order: a short explanatory line, the email capture form, the topic preferences table, the subscription actions bar, and finally the subscription status panel near the bottom of the content column. No sidebars, no multi-column content within the main flow; everything reads as one central vertical narrative.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The full-width header (info bar + navigation) occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Below it, the white content area takes up the remaining 80–85%. Within this content area, the actual interactive content (title + form + table + actions + messages) is vertically compact, concentrated in the central 40–50% of the page height on a typical desktop, leaving generous white space both above and below the main block. Horizontally, the content column is relatively narrow, spanning about 50–60% of the viewport width and centered, with wide left and right margins that reinforce the text-centric, academic feel. Each major component block (email form, table, action bar, status panel) uses the same column width and alignment to read as one continuous, centered strip.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain very low density, echoing the mockup. Each main component is visually separated from the next with generous vertical spacing (roughly 32–56px between blocks on desktop). Inside each component, avoid packing elements too tightly: labels, inputs, and table rows have comfortable line heights and vertical padding. There is no attempt to fill all available space; large areas of white space are intentionally left unused around and between the components to create a calm, uncluttered experience. Explanatory text is brief and center-aligned near the top, with the more data-heavy table still respecting generous row height and spacing so the page never feels dense.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the dark, full-width header and logo on the left, then scans across navigation and any social/search elements, exactly as in the mockup. The sharp contrast between the dark header and the white content area naturally pulls the eye down to the centered "Newsletter Subscriptions" heading. From there, the visual flow is strictly vertical: heading → one-line description → email capture form → topic preferences table → centered actions bar → status panel. The actions bar and status panel at the bottom serve as the final focal area, akin to the red email addresses in the mockup: they are the last elements encountered and thus the primary call-to-action and feedback zone for the page.

layout.overall_description:
Beneath the site’s existing full-width dark header band, the page opens into a broad white canvas. In the upper-middle of this canvas, a narrow, centered column holds the entire newsletter subscription experience. At the top of this column, a centered "Newsletter Subscriptions" heading sits with ample white space above and below, mirroring the minimal, calm feel of the original Jobs mockup. Directly underneath, a short, center-aligned line explains that users can manage topics, frequency, and email format, keeping the introduction compact and text-first.

Below this heading area, the email capture form appears as the first interactive block, centered in the same column width with generous vertical padding. It is followed by the newsletter topic preferences table, which aligns perfectly with the form’s width and sits a comfortable distance below, retaining a structured yet airy feel despite containing rows of data. Under the table, a centered actions bar with primary buttons occupies a relatively small horizontal band, visually detached by white space so it stands out as the key call-to-action. Finally, near the bottom of this central column, the subscription status panel appears when needed as a clear, text-based message area, echoing the mockup’s prominent red email addresses as the closing visual note. The rest of the page—especially the space to the left and right of the column and the white area below the last component—remains mostly empty, reinforcing a conservative, academic tone and focusing attention squarely on the central stack of components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines and anchors the main content column below the site header. Immediately under the dark navigation band, there is a tall block of white space; within the upper half of this white region, the page controller centers the page title "Newsletter Subscriptions" horizontally on the page. The title sits within a fixed-width column spanning roughly 50–60% of the viewport, centered with wide left and right margins. About one line (or a very short paragraph) of introductory text appears directly below the title, also center-aligned, with a modest gap (around 16–24px) between title and text, and a larger gap (around 32–40px) before the next component (the email capture form). The page controller ensures that all subsequent components (form, table, actions bar, and status panel) are stacked vertically in this same centered column, maintaining consistent width and alignment so the entire content block reads as a single, narrow, vertically oriented strip floating within a larger white canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the main content column; it does not scroll independently or stick, but it defines the column width and central alignment for all child components within the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The email capture form is positioned directly below the introductory text managed by the page controller, with a generous vertical gap (approximately 32–40px) to keep the layout airy. It occupies the full width of the centered content column (around 50–60% of the viewport), aligned perfectly with the title above. The form contents are arranged in a simple, vertical stack: an email label sits above a single email input field, with both elements aligned to the center of the column in overall placement but using left-aligned text for the label and field for clarity. There is ample vertical padding within the form block—roughly 24–32px between label, input, and any helper text—so it feels uncluttered despite being interactive. The entire form block has a comfortable amount of white space both above and below, clearly separating it from the heading area above and the preferences table below. There are no decorative borders or cards around this form; it simply appears as well-spaced text and inputs resting on the white background, consistent with the conservative aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow; it expands vertically as needed (e.g., for validation messages) but always remains centered in the main column and pushes subsequent components downward in the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter topic preferences table sits immediately beneath the email capture form, separated by a clear band of white space (around 40–48px) to respect the low-density layout. It shares the same centered column width as the form and heading (50–60% of viewport width), with its left and right edges aligned precisely to the elements above. Within this column, the table itself spans nearly the full width, with a small internal left and right padding so text does not touch the edges. The table header row (topic name, frequency, email format, and subscription toggle, for example) uses slightly bolder text but remains visually simple—no heavy shading, just light rule lines if any. Each row has generous vertical padding (roughly 12–16px above and below text) so the table feels readable and not cramped even if there are many topics. Columns are laid out left-to-right, but the entire table block is visually read as part of the single central column. If the number of topics grows large, the table lengthens vertically and the page scrolls; there is no horizontal scrolling on desktop as columns are sized to fit within the narrow content column. The table is the largest visual mass on the page but is still surrounded by ample whitespace above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing beneath the email form. It grows vertically with the number of topics. On smaller viewports it may allow vertical scrolling while maintaining its alignment to the central column, but it is not sticky or fixed relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscription actions bar appears directly below the table, with a generous top margin (around 32–40px) so it clearly reads as a separate, concluding section. It is centered horizontally within the same content column and is visually compact: a single horizontal row of one to three buttons (e.g., "Save preferences", "Reset", or "Unsubscribe"), all centered relative to the column. The buttons themselves do not span the entire column width; instead, they occupy roughly 40–50% of the column width, leaving comfortable blank space on both sides. Vertical padding above and below the buttons within the bar is modest (about 16–24px), enough to make the bar feel substantial yet not heavy. This bar serves as the visual equivalent of the mockup’s final red email CTA: it stands out as the primary actionable element near the bottom of the content stack. The design is still conservative—flat, simple buttons without dramatic shadows—so it harmonizes with the academic aesthetic while subtly drawing attention.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and part of the normal vertical flow. It sits just below the table and above the status panel. When the page scrolls, it scrolls away with the rest of the content; it is not sticky to any edge of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscription status panel is placed directly below the subscription actions bar, separated by a smaller vertical gap than between previous sections (roughly 16–24px) so it feels visually associated with the action buttons. It shares the same centered column width and alignment as all other components. When visible, the panel appears as a single block of text (e.g., a success or error message), center-aligned and limited to the column width so it remains easy to read. To echo the mockup’s use of red email addresses as a final highlight, the status message uses a slightly stronger text color (e.g., red for errors, green or a strong neutral for success) and can optionally sit on a very subtle, lightly tinted background stripe that spans the column width, giving it prominence without introducing heavy decoration. Vertical padding within the panel (around 12–20px top and bottom) ensures it is clearly defined as a distinct band of feedback at the bottom of the page content. When there is no status to show, this component effectively collapses, and the white space in its place contributes to the clean page ending.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and conditional. It occupies space in the normal flow only when there is a message to display; otherwise, it collapses to zero height. It does not fix or float, and as messages grow longer, it simply expands downward within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component has no visible representation on the page. It exists purely as an underlying behavior that synchronizes the current subscription selections and email address with URL parameters or similar state, enabling deep-linking and restoration of preferences. Visually, it introduces no new element, spacing, or boundary, and it does not alter the alignment or size of any other component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial. It occupies no physical space in the layout and does not affect flow, alignment, or scroll behavior; it runs in the background, logically associated with the page controller and form interactions.

name: Donate / Support Us
title: Donate / Support Us
description: This page lets users configure a donation (type, amount, target program, anonymity, and communication preferences), provide basic donor information, review all details, and then create a donation draft without entering any payment information.

core_features: Load available donation programs and supported currencies using getDonationPrograms and expose them to the donation form.
Allow users to choose a donation type (e.g., one_time or recurring) and enter a custom donation amount in a selected supported currency.
Allow users to select which donation program/fund they wish to support from the dynamically loaded list.
Provide options to mark the donation as anonymous and to choose a communicationPreference value that controls how the organization may contact the donor.
Collect essential donor information (donorName and donorEmail) and an optional donorAddress to support receipts or postal communication.
Guide the user through a two-step flow: an editable donation form followed by a review step that displays a read-only summary of all donation details.
On confirmation from the review step, call createDonationDraft to create a Donation record in draft status and display the resulting confirmation message and summary.
Offer navigation to return to the homepage after either confirming the donation draft or cancelling, and a contextual link to the contact page for donation-related questions.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: Donation from navigation without preselection: 1) User opens donate.html from the header or footer navigation without URL parameters. 2) Page initialization triggers getDonationPrograms to load programs and supportedCurrencies. 3) Once loaded, the donation form shows an empty program selection and no prefilled amount. 4) User chooses a donationType, selects a program from the loaded list, selects a currency from supportedCurrencies, and enters an amount. 5) User sets isAnonymous and communicationPreference, then enters donorName and donorEmail, optionally donorAddress. 6) User clicks the primary form submit action (e.g., 'Review donation'), which validates the input and, if valid, saves the values in client-side state and switches the UI into the review step. 7) The review step displays all donation and donor details in read-only form. 8) User clicks 'Confirm donation intention', which calls createDonationDraft with the values from the review state. 9) On success, the review component displays the returned Donation summary and the server-provided message, and shows a 'Return to homepage' link. 10) User clicks 'Return to homepage', navigating to index.html.

Workflow 2: Donation with preselected program and amount: 1) User clicks a contextual 'Support this program' link on index.html, about.html, or membership.html that opens donate.html with programId and optionally amount URL parameters. 2) On load, the page reads programId and amount from the URL but waits for getDonationPrograms to resolve. 3) After getDonationPrograms succeeds, the form attempts to preselect the program whose id matches the programId parameter; if no match is found, the program field remains unselected. 4) The form sets the amount field to the numeric value in the amount parameter if it is valid; if not valid, the amount field remains empty. 5) User adjusts any prefilled values as desired and completes all required fields. 6) User submits the form to move to the review step; the current selections (which may differ from the URL defaults) are stored in client state. 7) User reviews the details and either (a) edits them, returning to the form where all previously entered values are restored, or (b) confirms, which calls createDonationDraft. 8) On successful draft creation, the confirmation message and final donation details are shown, and the user can return to the homepage.

Workflow 3: Cancel or seek help: 1) At any point on the form step, user can click a 'Cancel' action that navigates directly to index.html without calling createDonationDraft. 2) On the review step before confirmation, user can also choose to cancel; this immediately navigates to index.html without calling createDonationDraft. 3) If the user has questions at any point, they click the 'Questions about donations?' link. 4) The page navigates to contact.html with the URL parameter topic=donations so that the contact page can preselect the appropriate topic.
interactions: On page load, call getDonationPrograms to populate the list of donation programs and supportedCurrencies. While loading, the form is disabled or indicates loading; on error, an error state is displayed with an option to retry the load.
When getDonationPrograms succeeds, bind the returned programs to the program selection input and the supportedCurrencies array to the currency selection input.

On initial render after getDonationPrograms, read programId and amount URL parameters: preselect a program matching programId if it exists in the loaded programs, and prefill the amount field with the numeric amount if it is valid. These URL-provided values are only used to initialize the form and do not override user changes thereafter.

When the user changes the selected program, update the internal form state to reflect the new programId. Optionally, the current programId may be mirrored in the URL query string to preserve state on refresh, without affecting any server data.

When the user changes the amount field, update internal form state with the current numeric value and clear or display validation messages if the amount is not a valid number or is not greater than zero.
When the user selects a currency, enforce that only values provided in supportedCurrencies can be chosen; changing the selection updates internal form state.
When the user toggles the anonymity option, update isAnonymous in form state, and adjust review text accordingly (e.g., whether donorName will be displayed as part of public acknowledgments).
When the user selects a communicationPreference option, update the form state so that createDonationDraft later receives a valid enum value from the allowed set.

As the user types donorName, donorEmail, and optional donorAddress, keep the internal form state synchronized and run basic client-side validation (e.g., required fields non-empty and email format) before allowing progression to the review step.

On clicking the primary form submit action to proceed to review, validate all required fields; if validation fails, prevent navigation to the review step and surface field-level error messages. If validation passes, store all current form values in a dedicated review state object and switch the UI to show the review panel component.
In the review panel, display all collected values in a read-only summary, formatted for clarity but without altering the underlying data. No API call has been made yet at this point.

When the user clicks 'Edit details' on the review panel, switch back to the form view and repopulate all form fields from the review state, allowing the user to change any value without losing progress.

When the user clicks 'Confirm donation intention' on the review panel, call createDonationDraft with donationType, amount, currency, programId, isAnonymous, communicationPreference, donorName, donorEmail, and donorAddress values taken from the review state. Disable the confirm action while the request is in progress to avoid duplicate submissions.

If createDonationDraft succeeds, store the returned Donation object and the message in the review panel's state. Update the UI to show the confirmed donation summary, using the Donation fields (including id, status, and timestamps) if appropriate for display, alongside the dynamic confirmation message. Show a link or button that navigates to index.html.
If createDonationDraft fails, display an error message in the review panel and allow the user to retry confirmation or return to the edit form without losing their filled-in data.
When the user clicks the 'Return to homepage' action after a successful draft creation, navigate to index.html without passing additional parameters.
When the user clicks a 'Cancel' action from either the form step or the review step (before confirmation), navigate to index.html immediately without calling createDonationDraft.
When the user clicks the 'Questions about donations?' support link, navigate to contact.html with the URL parameter topic=donations so that the contact page can prefill the topic field.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is controlled through a combination of URL parameters and in-memory UI state, without using localStorage. On initial load, the page parses the current URL's query string for two parameters: programId (string) and amount (number). Immediately after getDonationPrograms successfully returns, the donation form attempts to match programId against the list of DonationProgram entities. If a program with a matching id exists and is active, the form's program selection is initialized to that program; otherwise, the program selection remains uninitialized and the user must choose manually. For the amount parameter, the page attempts to parse it into a numeric value; if parsing succeeds and the value passes basic validation (e.g., it is greater than zero), the amount input is initialized with that value; if not, the input remains empty. These URL-derived values are used only as initial defaults. After the user interacts with the form, the internal form state becomes the source of truth. The page may optionally keep the currently selected programId and the current amount reflected in the URL (e.g., via history.replaceState) to allow sharing or bookmarking, but this mirroring does not affect server-side data and does not trigger any additional API calls. The navigation step (form vs. review vs. post-confirmation) is managed in in-memory state via a step indicator (for example, 'form', 'review', or 'confirmed') and is not encoded in the URL, because there is no interface provided to reload a previously created Donation by id. After the user confirms and createDonationDraft returns successfully, the resulting Donation object and message are stored in a 'confirmed donation' state used solely for display; subsequent navigation away from the page (e.g., to index.html) will clear this state. No state is persisted in localStorage or other client-side storage mechanisms; only the URL parameters programId and amount are used, and only as described above for initial prepopulation and optional non-authoritative mirroring.

components.functionality: On page initialization, this component calls getDonationPrograms to retrieve the list of available DonationProgram entities and the supportedCurrencies array. It manages loading and error states and exposes the resulting data to the donation form and review components.

This component renders the main donation input form, bound to the programs and supportedCurrencies provided by donation-programs-loader. It collects donationType, amount, currency, programId, isAnonymous, communicationPreference, donorName, donorEmail, and optional donorAddress. It validates input and, on successful submission, stores the values in review state and triggers the transition to the review step without calling any API.

This component displays a read-only summary of all donation details captured by donation-form. Initially it uses only the in-memory review state (no Donation record). When the user confirms, it calls createDonationDraft with the review state's values, then displays the returned Donation object and message in a confirmation view. It also allows returning to the form for edits prior to confirmation, and cancelling back to the homepage.

This component centralizes client-side validation for the donation-form. It checks that required fields (donationType, amount, currency, programId, isAnonymous, communicationPreference, donorName, donorEmail) are present, that amount is a valid number greater than zero, that currency is one of supportedCurrencies, that programId corresponds to a loaded DonationProgram, and that donorEmail has a valid format. It provides validation results and error messages back to the form to control whether the user can proceed to the review step.

This component renders the 'Questions about donations?' link. On click, it navigates to contact.html with the URL parameter topic=donations, enabling the contact page to preselect the donation-related topic.

This component centralizes navigation back to the homepage (index.html). It is used by both the form step (for a direct cancel action) and the review/confirmation step (after a draft is successfully created or when the user cancels before confirmation). It does not pass additional URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly linear, top-to-bottom, single-column layout beneath the existing global header. After the site-wide phone/info bar and navigation band, the main content area begins with a centered page title ('Donate / Support Us'), followed by a short explanatory text (if present), then the donation form, then the donation review panel, and finally a compact block of center-aligned navigation/support links at the very bottom. All interactive components are stacked vertically within a narrow, centered content column; there are no sidebars or multi-column layouts in the main content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly linear, top-to-bottom, single-column layout beneath the existing global header. After the site-wide phone/info bar and navigation band, the main content area begins with a centered page title ('Donate / Support Us'), followed by a short explanatory text (if present), then the donation form, then the donation review panel, and finally a compact block of center-aligned navigation/support links at the very bottom. All interactive components are stacked vertically within a narrow, centered content column; there are no sidebars or multi-column layouts in the main content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (phone bar + navigation) uses the top ~15–20% of the viewport, as in the mockup. The remaining ~80–85% is a large white content canvas with a relatively narrow, centered text column occupying roughly 50–60% of the viewport width on desktop (capped at about 640–720px). Above the main title there is a notable band of white space, and below the title there is generous vertical spacing before the form begins. Each major block (title, form, review panel, bottom links) is separated by ample vertical margins so that the eye sees a few clearly defined zones surrounded by white space.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. Form fields are grouped into a small number of vertical sections with clear headings and liberal spacing between groups and between individual rows. There are no densely packed multi-column field rows; instead, everything lives in a single, easy-to-scan column. The review panel is compact and to the point, summarizing information in a simple text layout rather than a busy visual card system. Bottom navigation/support links sit on their own lines with plenty of breathing room, reinforcing the calm, uncluttered feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows the mockup’s pattern: the eye first lands on the dark site header and logo, then moves across the navigation and social/search icons. The strong contrast between the dark header band and the white body then pulls the eye down to the centered 'Donate / Support Us' heading. From there, the user scrolls straight down through the donation form fields in a simple vertical reading flow. Next, attention moves to the donation review panel directly below the form as a natural 'summary' step. Finally, the gaze ends at the bottom center of the page on the red-accented donation support link (mirroring the red email emphasis in the Jobs page) and the more neutral homepage link beneath it.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the full-width dark header and navigation band, the Donate / Support Us page opens into a wide, white content area where everything of importance is gathered in a narrow, centered column. After a comfortable vertical gap, a bold but conservative, center-aligned page title ('Donate / Support Us') appears, immediately telling the user where they are. Below the title, another cushion of white space leads into a centered, single-column donation form that runs for only a modest vertical stretch compared to the overall screen height, emphasizing simplicity over density. The form’s labels and inputs are left-aligned inside the central column, but the block as a whole is visually centered, with generous side margins. Directly under the form, separated by a noticeable vertical gap, a review panel occupies the same width, reading like a clean, text-heavy summary box. Under that, another pocket of whitespace introduces a small, center-aligned footer-like block: first a red-highlighted support link inviting users to get help with their donation, then a quieter homepage navigation link below it. The total impression matches the original mockup: a conservative, academic-corporate page with a lot of breathable white space, few distractions, and a simple top-to-bottom flow that gently leads users from information, to configuration, to review, and finally to support/exit links.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation-programs-loader operates behind the scenes and does not occupy any visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the main content column, just before the donation form content is rendered. From a layout perspective, the loader’s activity is reflected only indirectly: until programs are loaded, the central white canvas shows either a subtle, center-aligned loading text or spinner placeholder within the same narrow content column where the form will appear. Once data is ready, that placeholder vanishes and the donation-form takes its place in the exact center column area, preserving the clean and spacious feel of the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, does not create permanent spatial elements. Any temporary loading indicator is centered within the main content column and vertically offset slightly below the page title, but disappears once loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation-form is the primary focal block in the white content area. It is centered horizontally beneath the page title, occupying roughly 50–60% of the viewport width on desktop, with a maximum width of about 640–720px, and equal wide left and right margins. There is a generous vertical gap (approximately 64–96px) between the page title and the top of the form, reinforcing the calm, spacious layout. Within the form, all fields are arranged in a single vertical column: section headings (e.g., 'Donation Details', 'Your Information', 'Communication Preferences') appear as simple, left-aligned text inside this centered block, each separated from the next section by ample vertical spacing. Individual label–input pairs sit on their own lines; labels are left-aligned, and inputs extend to nearly the full width of the form column, with comfortable padding above and below each row. The primary action (e.g., 'Create Donation Draft' button) sits at the bottom of the form, also left-aligned inside the column but visually centered within the page because of the narrow column.
