There is a clear vertical margin (roughly 48–72px) below the form before the review panel begins, keeping each major block visually distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the single-column flow; it does not float or stick. It expands vertically as needed based on the number of form fields while always remaining horizontally centered with fixed maximum width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation-review-panel sits directly below the donation-form, aligned to the same central axis. It matches the form’s width, sharing the same narrow, centered column (50–60% of viewport width, max ~640–720px), so that the page reads as one continuous, centered column of content. A sizeable vertical gap (about 48–72px) separates the bottom of the form and the top of the review panel, making the review feel like a distinct, subsequent step. Visually, the panel appears as a simple, text-centric summary area: either as plain text headings and bullet-style lines or within a subtle, thin, light-gray bordered box, but without heavy decoration. The content inside is left-aligned and arranged in short lines or small groups (e.g., 'Donation Amount: ...', 'Program: ...', 'Anonymity: ...'). The panel’s height is moderate—enough to list all key details without feeling dense—and there is generous whitespace at the top and bottom within the panel to maintain the low-density aesthetic. Below the panel, another comfortable vertical margin (around 64px) precedes the block containing the support and homepage links.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element stacked in the vertical flow directly after the form. It grows vertically with content but remains centered, matching the form’s width, with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation-validation-controller is non-visual and does not appear as a distinct element in the layout. It is conceptually linked to the donation-form and donation-review-panel, governing their states, but it occupies no independent space in the content column. Any validation messages it triggers appear inline within the form itself—directly beneath the relevant field labels or at the top of the form as brief text lines—maintaining the single-column, text-centric style. These messages use the same narrow column width and spacing conventions as the rest of the form, preserving the overall clean visual rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible controller that influences the form and review components. It does not affect page structure by itself; any spatial impact is expressed through inline messages or status text within the form’s existing layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation-support-link is placed at the bottom of the main content column, directly beneath the donation-review-panel and centered horizontally. After a generous vertical margin (approximately 64–80px) below the review panel, this link appears as a single, center-aligned line of text, similar in prominence to the red email call-to-action at the end of the Jobs page. The link text is set in the standard body type but highlighted in red or a similarly strong accent color, making it the final, eye-catching element in the vertical reading flow. It spans only its intrinsic text width, not the full column width, and sits surrounded by white space above and below, giving it a clear, isolated presence. The homepage-navigation-link is positioned just beneath this support link, so the support option is seen first as the primary bottom-of-page action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline text link centered within the single-column layout. It does not stick or float; it remains at the bottom of the scrollable content, separated clearly from the review panel and from the homepage link below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-navigation-link appears immediately below the donation-support-link, aligned to the same horizontal center within the narrow content column. A modest vertical gap (roughly 16–24px) separates the two lines, creating a small, self-contained bottom navigation cluster. The homepage link is styled more neutrally than the red support link—using the default link color or a subdued tone—so that it reads as a secondary escape route back to the main site rather than a primary call for help. It occupies only its own text width and is surrounded by ample white space at the bottom of the page, echoing the open, minimal feel of the mockup. When the user reaches the end of the content, this link forms the last, gentle visual punctuation mark on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, center-aligned inline link positioned directly after the support link. It does not have any sticky behavior and simply sits at the natural end of the page’s vertical flow.

name: Membership Overview
title: Membership Overview
description: Page for viewing and comparing available membership plans, understanding included benefits and pricing, and proceeding to the appropriate membership application.

core_features: Fetch and display all active membership plans using the getMembershipPlans interface, including name, description, annual price, currency, and benefits.
Compare membership plans side-by-side, with clear visibility of whether each plan includes access to member-only webinars and discounted conference fees.
Allow users to specify required benefits (e.g., access to member-only webinars, discounted conference fees) and automatically identify the lowest-priced plan that meets those criteria.
Highlight a specific membership plan on initial load based on the optional highlightPlan URL parameter mapped to the MembershipPlan.planType field.
Provide per-plan actions ('Apply' or 'Join') that navigate to membership_apply.html with the selected MembershipPlan.id as a URL parameter.
Provide alternative navigation for users who do not want to become members: a link to donate.html and a membership-specific support link to contact.html with topic=membership.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: General plan browsing
1. User navigates to membership.html (optionally with highlightPlan in the URL).
2. On page initialization, the page calls getMembershipPlans with no parameters.
3. The data loader component receives the response object containing an array of MembershipPlan entities and a default currency value.
4. The comparison table component renders one entry per active plan, displaying each plan's name, description, annualPriceAmount with annualPriceCurrency, and the benefits list.
5. For each plan, boolean fields includesMemberWebinars and includesDiscountedConferenceFees are displayed as benefit indicators (e.g., via labels or icons).
6. User can scan the table to compare plans, scroll if needed, and click into per-plan expand/collapse actions to see full benefit lists.
7. When the user finds a plan they want, they click the plan’s 'Apply' or 'Join' button.
8. The click handler navigates the browser to membership_apply.html with a query parameter planId set to the MembershipPlan.id of the selected plan.

Workflow 2: Start on a specific plan from another page
1. User clicks a 'Membership' link on index.html that appends a highlightPlan parameter (for example, membership.html?highlightPlan=student).
2. On initialization, the page reads the highlightPlan URL parameter value.
3. The page calls getMembershipPlans.
4. After plans are loaded, a controller component searches the plans array for plans whose planType matches the highlightPlan value.
5. If a matching plan exists, the page marks that plan as the primary highlighted plan in the comparison table (for example, by default expanding its details or visually emphasizing it) and, if appropriate, automatically scrolls it into view.
6. If no plan matches highlightPlan, the page falls back to a neutral state with no special highlight.
7. User can still adjust benefit requirements, view other plans, and proceed with any plan’s 'Apply' or 'Join' button.

Workflow 3: Find the most economical plan with required benefits
1. User arrives on membership.html (with or without highlightPlan).
2. The page calls getMembershipPlans and renders the plans.
3. User interacts with a benefit requirement control where they can specify that a plan must include access to member-only webinars, discounted conference fees, or both. The control updates local component state and also updates the page URL to include or update benefit-related parameters (for example, membership.html?requireWebinars=true&requireDiscountedConferenceFees=true&highlightPlan=professional), so that the state can be restored on reload or sharing.
4. The recommendation logic filters the plans array to only those where includesMemberWebinars and includesDiscountedConferenceFees meet the user’s stated requirements.
5. Among the filtered plans, the component selects the plan with the lowest annualPriceAmount (in the currency returned by getMembershipPlans) as the recommended plan.
6. The recommended plan is highlighted in the comparison table and optionally surfaced in a small summary panel (e.g., 'Recommended plan' area) that links directly to the corresponding 'Apply' or 'Join' action.
7. If no plan meets the required benefits, the component shows a message stating that no plan matches the current criteria and suggests relaxing benefit requirements.
8. User can then click the recommended plan’s 'Apply' or 'Join' button, which navigates to membership_apply.html?planId=<selected_plan_id>.

Workflow 4: Support without membership
1. User reviews membership options but decides not to join.
2. User clicks a clearly labeled link such as 'Support us without becoming a member.'
3. The click handler triggers navigation to donate.html with no additional parameters.
4. Alternatively, if the user has questions, they click a 'Questions about membership?' link.
5. The click handler navigates to contact.html with query parameter topic set to 'membership'.

Workflow 5: Restore page state from URL
1. A user shares a URL including parameters such as highlightPlan and benefit requirements (e.g., membership.html?highlightPlan=professional&requireWebinars=true).
2. Another user opens this URL.
3. On initialization, the page reads all relevant query parameters (highlightPlan and any benefit requirement parameters).
4. The page calls getMembershipPlans.
5. After data is loaded, the highlight controller highlights the plan whose planType matches highlightPlan.
6. The benefit requirement form pre-selects options corresponding to the benefit requirement parameters from the URL.
7. The recommendation logic runs immediately against the loaded plans using these requirements, and highlights the corresponding recommended plan or shows a 'no matching plan' message as needed.
interactions: Click on plan toggle: Expands or collapses the benefits/details section for a specific MembershipPlan within the comparison table.

Click on column header (if sorting is provided): Changes the sort order of the plans list (e.g., sort by price ascending/descending). Sorting state is reflected in URL parameters to maintain shareable state.

Change benefit requirement option (e.g., checkboxes for member-only webinars / discounted conference fees): Updates filter state and triggers recalculation of the recommended plan; also updates corresponding URL parameters.
Click 'Apply' or 'Join' button on a plan: Navigates to membership_apply.html with planId set to the MembershipPlan.id of the selected plan.
Click 'Support us without becoming a member' link: Navigates to donate.html with no additional parameters.
Click 'Questions about membership?' support link: Navigates to contact.html with topic=membership in the URL.

Page load (onInit): Reads URL parameters (highlightPlan and any benefit requirement params) and triggers getMembershipPlans; after data is loaded, applies plan highlighting and recommendation logic based on these parameters.
Plan recommendation click (if present): Clicking on a 'View recommended plan' anchor or button scrolls the comparison table to the recommended plan and focuses it for accessibility.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is controlled via URL query parameters and in-memory component state. On every page load, the script parses the current window.location.search and derives:
- highlightPlan: If present, its string value is compared to MembershipPlan.planType values. After getMembershipPlans resolves, any plans whose planType match highlightPlan are marked as highlighted in the UI. If multiple plans share the same planType, the first in the sorted list is highlighted.
- requireWebinars (optional, boolean): If present, and parsed as true, the recommendation logic and filter consider only plans where includesMemberWebinars is true. The benefit requirement component initializes its control for this benefit as selected.
- requireDiscountedConferenceFees (optional, boolean): If present and true, the recommendation logic and filter consider only plans where includesDiscountedConferenceFees is true. The benefit requirement component initializes its control for this benefit as selected.
- sortBy and sortDirection (optional, strings): If provided, these control the initial ordering of plans (for example, sortBy=price, sortDirection=asc). The comparison table reads these parameters after getMembershipPlans completes, applies sorting to the plans array, and updates the table accordingly.

When the user interacts with the page:
- Changing benefit requirements updates the in-memory filter state and also updates the URL query string using history.replaceState (or similar), changing requireWebinars and/or requireDiscountedConferenceFees while preserving any existing highlightPlan parameter.
- Changing sorting updates in-memory sort state and also updates sortBy and sortDirection parameters in the URL.
- Clicking on a plan to focus/highlight it may update highlightPlan to that plan’s planType to generate a shareable URL focusing on that plan.

The getMembershipPlans interface is always called without parameters, and its response is treated as the single source of truth for all MembershipPlan data. No plan data, prices, or benefits are hardcoded; all are bound directly from the returned plans array. All navigation to other pages uses URL parameters only and does not rely on localStorage or other persistent client storage.

components.functionality: Initializes the Membership Overview page by calling getMembershipPlans, storing the resulting plans and currency in shared page state, and exposing loading and error states to dependent components.
event_handlers: onInit: On page load, call getMembershipPlans with no parameters. While pending, expose a 'loading' state to hide or disable dependent components.

onLoadSuccess: When getMembershipPlans returns successfully, store response.plans as the canonical array of MembershipPlan entities and response.currency as the display currency. Notify dependent components (comparison table, recommendation logic, highlight controller) that data is available.

onLoadError: If getMembershipPlans fails, store an error state. Dependent components should render an error message (e.g., 'Unable to load membership plans') and hide comparison and recommendation elements until a reload is attempted.

components.functionality: Reads and writes URL parameters related to membership page state (highlightPlan, benefit requirements, sorting) and exposes this state to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse window.location.search into a state object containing highlightPlan (string), requireWebinars (boolean), requireDiscountedConferenceFees (boolean), sortBy (string), and sortDirection (string). Provide these values to components that rely on initial URL state.

onHighlightPlanChange: When another component (e.g., the comparison table) requests a change of highlighted plan, update the internal highlightPlan value and update the URL query string accordingly using history.replaceState or an equivalent mechanism.

onBenefitRequirementChange: When the benefit requirement form updates its selections, update requireWebinars and/or requireDiscountedConferenceFees in internal state and in the URL query string without reloading the page.
onSortingChange: When the comparison table changes sorting (e.g., price ascending/descending), update sortBy and sortDirection in internal state and in the URL query string.

onExternalNavigation: This component does not handle external navigation itself but ensures that internal state is always reflected in the URL before any navigation occurs, so that the current configuration can be bookmarked or shared.

components.functionality: Displays all active membership plans in a structured, comparable format, allowing users to see names, descriptions, prices, and key benefits (including member-only webinars and discounted conference fees) and to initiate applications.
event_handlers: onPlansUpdated: When membership-plans-data-loader finishes loading plans, render each MembershipPlan as a row or card in the table, including name, description, annualPriceAmount, annualPriceCurrency, and benefits.
onApplyClick(planId): When a user clicks the 'Apply' or 'Join' action for a plan, construct a URL membership_apply.html?planId=<planId> and navigate the browser to it.

onSortChange(column, direction): When a user interacts with sortable headers (e.g., price), update the internal sort state and reorder the displayed plans accordingly; notify membership-url-state-controller to update sortBy and sortDirection in the URL.

onPlanFocus(plan): When a user clicks on a plan header, or when other components request focus on a particular plan (e.g., recommended plan), mark that plan as focused/highlighted and notify membership-url-state-controller to potentially update highlightPlan based on plan.planType.

onHighlightPlanStateChange(highlightPlanValue): When membership-url-state-controller provides or updates highlightPlan, find the first plan whose planType equals highlightPlanValue. Mark this plan as highlighted (e.g., expand details by default, visually emphasize) and scroll into view if appropriate.
onTogglePlanDetails(planId): Expand or collapse the detailed benefits list for the selected plan without altering other plan states, using the benefits array directly from the MembershipPlan entity.

components.functionality: Allows users to specify which key benefits are required in their membership plan (e.g., access to member-only webinars and/or discounted conference fees) and propagates those requirements to the recommendation logic and URL state.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrlState(requireWebinars, requireDiscountedConferenceFees): On page load, initialize the form controls (e.g., toggles or checkboxes) based on values provided by membership-url-state-controller from the URL.

onRequireWebinarsChange(isRequired): When the user toggles the requirement for access to member-only webinars, update internal state and notify both membership-url-state-controller and membership-plan-recommendation-engine of the new requirement.

onRequireDiscountedConferenceFeesChange(isRequired): When the user toggles the requirement for discounted conference fees, update internal state and notify both membership-url-state-controller and membership-plan-recommendation-engine of the new requirement.

onResetFilters: Provide an optional action to clear all benefit requirements; when triggered, set both requireWebinars and requireDiscountedConferenceFees to false, notify membership-url-state-controller, and trigger recalculation of the recommendation with no benefit constraints.

components.functionality: Computes and exposes the most economical membership plan that satisfies the user’s selected benefit requirements, based solely on MembershipPlan data returned by getMembershipPlans.
event_handlers: onPlansLoaded(plans): Store the full list of plans received from membership-plans-data-loader and, if benefit requirements are already known from URL state, immediately compute the recommended plan.

onBenefitRequirementsUpdated(requireWebinars, requireDiscountedConferenceFees): Filter the stored plans so that only plans with includesMemberWebinars=true are kept when requireWebinars is true, and only plans with includesDiscountedConferenceFees=true are kept when requireDiscountedConferenceFees is true. If either requirement is false, do not filter on that criterion.

onRecomputeRecommendation: After filtering, sort the remaining plans by annualPriceAmount ascending within the single currency provided by getMembershipPlans. The first plan in this sorted list becomes the recommended plan. If the filtered list is empty, clear any existing recommendation and expose a 'no matching plan' state to the UI.

onRecommendationAvailable(plan): When a recommended plan is determined, notify membership-plan-comparison-table to highlight this plan, and notify membership-recommendation-summary to display it. Also optionally notify membership-url-state-controller to consider aligning highlightPlan with plan.planType.

onReset: When benefit requirements are cleared (e.g., via membership-benefit-requirement-form), recompute the recommendation from all available plans with no constraints, or choose to provide no recommendation depending on product rules.

components.functionality: Displays a concise summary of the currently recommended plan (if any), including price, key benefits indicators, and a direct 'Apply' or 'Join' action that uses the same navigation rules as the comparison table.
event_handlers: onRecommendationUpdated(plan): When membership-plan-recommendation-engine provides a recommended plan, render its key fields (name, annualPriceAmount, annualPriceCurrency, and key benefit indicators derived from includesMemberWebinars and includesDiscountedConferenceFees). If no recommendation is available, either hide this panel or show a message explaining that no plan meets current requirements.

onViewPlanClick(planId): When the user clicks a 'View details' or equivalent action in the recommendation summary, notify membership-plan-comparison-table to focus and highlight the corresponding plan and scroll it into view.
onApplyClick(planId): When the user clicks an 'Apply' or 'Join' button in this panel, navigate to membership_apply.html?planId=<planId>, just as in the comparison table.

components.functionality: Provides alternative paths for users who either want to support the organization without membership or need help with membership-related questions.
event_handlers: onClickDonate: When the 'Support us without becoming a member' link is activated, navigate directly to donate.html without additional query parameters.
onClickMembershipQuestions: When the 'Questions about membership?' link is activated, navigate to contact.html?topic=membership.
onRender: Ensure these links are always available regardless of the loading state of membership plans, so users can seek support even if data loading fails.

components.functionality: Displays global informational, loading, empty-state, and error messages related to membership plan data loading and recommendation results.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange(isLoading): When membership-plans-data-loader sets loading true, display a non-blocking loading indicator for the plans area.
onLoadError(error): When membership-plans-data-loader encounters an error, show an error message and optionally a 'Retry' action that triggers the data loader to call getMembershipPlans again.

onEmptyPlans: If getMembershipPlans returns an empty plans array, show an informational message indicating that no membership plans are currently available and suggest contacting support via the membership-support-links component.

onNoRecommendation: When membership-plan-recommendation-engine reports that no plan matches the current benefit requirements, display a targeted message encouraging the user to adjust benefit requirements to see available options.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing site header (phone bar + navigation), the Membership Overview page follows a strict, linear, top-to-bottom, single-column arrangement. All visible components are stacked vertically within a narrow, centered content column. The sequence is: page title and introductory text (page chrome, not listed), membership-page-messages, membership-recommendation-summary, membership-benefit-requirement-form, membership-plan-comparison-table, membership-support-links. Data/logic components (data-loader, URL state controller, recommendation engine) do not visibly break this flow; they operate behind the scenes. No sidebars, no multi-column splits; every interactive element sits within this main central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing site header (phone bar + navigation), the Membership Overview page follows a strict, linear, top-to-bottom, single-column arrangement. All visible components are stacked vertically within a narrow, centered content column. The sequence is: page title and introductory text (page chrome, not listed), membership-page-messages, membership-recommendation-summary, membership-benefit-requirement-form, membership-plan-comparison-table, membership-support-links. Data/logic components (data-loader, URL state controller, recommendation engine) do not visibly break this flow; they operate behind the scenes. No sidebars, no multi-column splits; every interactive element sits within this main central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (already defined by the site) occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Below it, a generous vertical gap (around 10–15% of the viewport height) leads into the centered content block. The main content column is relatively narrow (about 60–70% of the page width on desktop, with a max width around 720–900px) and is centered with wide left and right margins. Within this column, the comparison table occupies the most vertical height, the recommendation summary and filter form occupy modest vertical slices, and support links consume a thin strip near the bottom. Ample vertical spacing between each component maintains a sense of a large white canvas with a compact center cluster of text and data.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low, even though this page is more information-heavy than the original 'Jobs' example. Each section is separated by generous vertical whitespace (1.5–2x the body text line height). The comparison table is the densest element but still uses comfortable row heights and breathing room above and below. Text is center-aligned for headings and summaries, and left-aligned within the table for readability. Inline elements (form fields, links) are not crammed together; instead they are spaced so that each block feels visually distinct and easy to scan.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the dark header band draws initial attention, the eye is pulled down to the center of the white content area where the 'Membership Overview' heading sits. Directly beneath it, any page messages provide immediate feedback. From there, the eye moves vertically down to the membership-recommendation-summary (which offers a concise, central takeaway), then to the requirement/benefit filter form where users can refine their view, and finally to the membership-plan-comparison-table, which functions as the main decision surface. The page ends on the membership-support-links, which, like the red email addresses in the mockup, serve as a clear set of final call-to-action options, lightly emphasized by spacing and positioning rather than heavy visual styling.

layout.overall_description: The Membership Overview page sits beneath the existing black header and contact bar, opening onto a large, calm white canvas. In the center of this canvas, a relatively narrow content column is vertically offset from the header by a generous gap, so the first thing a user sees is the centered 'Membership Overview' title with perhaps a single succinct intro line below it. Immediately under this title, any feedback messages appear as a slim, full-width (within the content column) text band, lightly colored but still understated. A bit further down, still centered, a short recommendation summary paragraph presents the system’s suggested membership option, echoing the simple, text-first style of the original mockup. Below this, aligned with the same column and still surrounded by ample whitespace, a compact requirement/benefit filter form allows users to adjust criteria; its labels and inputs are arranged in a simple vertical stack, not an intricate UI. Under another generous vertical gap, the main comparison table spans most of the content column, clearly dominating the middle of the page’s vertical real estate while still breathing inside wide side margins. The rows are comfortably spaced, and the headings are text-heavy rather than graphically styled. Finally, after the table, the white canvas continues with a noticeable vertical break before a center-aligned set of support links — short lines of text that function much like the red email addresses in the original design, anchoring the bottom of the content as the user’s final point of action. Throughout, there are no sidebars or visual clutter: just a vertical stack of well-spaced, text-centric components floating in a broad field of white.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no persistent visual representation. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the content column’s DOM structure, immediately below the page title area but above any user-visible membership content. When active (e.g., during initial data fetch), it may manifest as a very subtle, single-line loading indicator or short status text centered within the content column, spanning about 40–50% of the column width. This indicator appears with generous white space above and below (roughly equal to a paragraph’s line height) so it does not feel cramped or like a permanent UI element. Once loading is complete, it collapses entirely, returning that space to the surrounding whitespace, preserving the clean, low-density layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-sticky, appears inline at the top of the content stack when needed and collapses to zero height when not in use; no fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is purely behavioral with no visible UI. It lives behind the scenes within the page structure and does not occupy any spatial footprint. It should not introduce gaps, frames, or any visible controls; all of its effects are reflected in the state of visible components such as the filter form and comparison table.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-layout-affecting; operates entirely in the background without shifting or reserving space in the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison table is the primary, central element in the content column. It is positioned below the filter form with a generous vertical margin (approximately 40–60px on desktop) separating it from the elements above. Horizontally, it is centered within the page and spans about 80–90% of the narrow content column, leaving a modest inner left and right margin to avoid touching the column edges. Vertically, it occupies the largest portion of the page’s content area; on first view, it will typically take up the middle third to half of the viewport height, and may extend further as rows increase. Column headers align at the top of the table, with text left-aligned inside cells but the entire table block visually centered in the white space. Generous row height and slightly increased line spacing maintain the low-density feel. Above the table, a simple, centered subheading (e.g., 'Compare membership plans') can sit as part of this component, separated from the table grid by a small vertical gap, reinforcing its role without adding visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls naturally with the page. No sticky headers or floating columns by default, preserving the conservative, text-centric feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter form sits above the comparison table as a compact, centered control strip in the main content column. It is positioned roughly in the upper-middle portion of the white content area, directly below the recommendation summary and separated from it by generous vertical spacing (around 32–48px). The form’s width matches the general content column width (about 60–70% of the page width, max 720–900px), but its interactive elements are organized in a simple vertical stack: label on one line, then its dropdown/input on the next, with each field group separated by small vertical gaps. If there are multiple fields, they align to the left within the column, but the entire form block is visually centered. The overall height of the form is modest — perhaps one quarter to one third of the viewport height at most — and there is ample white space above and below so it feels like a lightly emphasized control section rather than a dense filter panel. Any submit or 'Update comparison' control should be a simple text button or lightly styled button, right- or center-aligned at the bottom of the form block, with clear spacing from the last field.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline component that scrolls with the page; it does not stick to the top as users scroll through the table, aligning with the conservative, non-dynamic page behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component provides logic for recommendations but has no direct, visible UI. It conceptually sits between the data-loader and the recommendation summary in the page’s functional hierarchy but does not occupy any space on screen. All of its influence is expressed through the content of the membership-recommendation-summary and possibly default selections in the filter form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible processing layer with no spatial footprint; it does not affect layout spacing or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommendation summary is placed relatively high in the content column, immediately below the page title and any page messages, and above the filter form. It appears as a short, centered text block — one to three lines — describing the recommended membership option. Horizontally, it spans about 60% of the content column, centered so that generous left and right margins frame the text. To maintain the calm, academic feel, it may be slightly distinguished via typographic hierarchy (e.g., slightly larger font or semi-bold text) rather than heavy background colors or borders. There is ample white space above (roughly 32–48px below the page title/messages) and below (another 32–48px before the filter form), so it feels like a distinct, easily scannable guidance element in the middle of a large white field. The text is center-aligned, echoing the central, numbered content from the original mockup while remaining concise.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline component that scrolls naturally with the page; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Support links are positioned at the bottom of the main content stack, directly after the comparison table and any concluding explanatory text. A generous vertical gap (about 48–64px) separates them from the table, allowing the table to visually conclude before the user’s eye is drawn to support options. The links themselves appear as a short list of center-aligned text items (for example, two to four lines), each on its own line with moderate vertical spacing (16–24px) between them. The block spans roughly 40–60% of the content column width and is centered, mirroring the style of the red email addresses in the original mockup: there is a sense that these items are the final, quiet calls to action. Links may use a slightly stronger color (e.g., the site’s accent color) to stand out but without bold shapes or large buttons. Below the last link, additional white space (at least 64px) gives a soft visual endpoint before the page footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline, sitting at the natural end of the scrollable content; no special sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Page messages (errors, confirmations, or informative notices) appear near the top of the content column, directly beneath the 'Membership Overview' heading and any short introductory sentence. They are laid out as a single, horizontally centered band of text that spans the full width of the content column (about 60–70% of the page width, centered) but remains relatively low in height — typically one or two lines, with comfortable padding above and below the text. A light background tint (e.g., pale green for success, pale red for error) can be used to subtly differentiate messages, but the overall effect remains understated and text-first. There is a modest vertical gap (16–24px) between the heading and the message, and another similar gap before the recommendation summary below, so the message integrates smoothly into the vertical flow without breaking the sense of spaciousness. When no messages are present, this component collapses entirely, freeing the space so that the recommendation summary moves up closer to the heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and non-sticky; appears and disappears without altering overall layout structure beyond vertical spacing adjustments.

name: Membership Application
title: Membership Application
description: Start a membership application for a selected plan by reviewing plan details and submitting initial applicant information before payment.

core_features: Read the selected membership plan ID from the URL and load active membership plans via getMembershipPlans.
Resolve and display the details of the selected membership plan, including name, plan type, description, pricing information, and benefits.
Indicate if the plan specified by the URL parameter cannot be found and offer navigation back to the membership plan overview page.
Provide a membership application form that collects full name, email, country, and role/status relevant to the chosen plan.
Validate required applicant fields and perform plan-dependent validation rules (e.g., requiring role/status for certain plan types) before submission.
Call startMembershipApplication with the selected planId and form data to create an in-progress membership application.
Display application creation status and server-returned messages without collecting or processing payment details on this page.
Offer a clear way to change the chosen plan, navigating back to the membership overview (membership.html).
user_workflows:
1. Arrive on page with selected plan:
   - User is redirected from membership.html to membership_apply.html with a planId URL parameter.
   - On load, the page reads ?planId=... from the URL.
   - The page calls getMembershipPlans.
   - When the plans load, the page finds the plan whose id matches planId.
   - If matched, the plan summary component shows plan details; the application form is enabled for that plan.
   - If no plan matches, an error state is shown with a link back to membership.html.

2. Complete application for valid plan:
   - User reviews plan summary.
   - User fills in the application form fields: full name, email, country, role/status.
   - On clicking the "Submit application" (or equivalent) button, client-side validation runs:
     - Checks required fields (fullName, email, country; roleOrStatus when plan rules require it).
     - Validates email format.
   - If client validation fails, errors are shown and the API is not called.
   - If validation passes, the page calls startMembershipApplication with { planId from URL, fullName, email, country, roleOrStatus }.
   - While the API call is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a pending state is shown.
   - On success, the page receives a MembershipApplication object and message; the form is locked to prevent duplicate submissions, and the application status panel displays the message and key application state.
   - The page exposes a "Continue" action (e.g., button) that can move the user to the next step of the membership process (handled by higher-level navigation logic).

3. Handle invalid or missing planId:
   - User arrives at membership_apply.html without a planId or with a planId that does not match any active plan from getMembershipPlans.
   - After getMembershipPlans resolves, the page detects no matching plan.
   - The plan summary component shows a functional error message indicating the plan is not available for application.
   - The application form remains disabled or hidden.
   - A "Back to membership plans" link navigates to membership.html so the user can choose another plan.

4. Change plan from within the application page:
   - User reviews the selected plan but decides to change it.
   - User clicks the "Change plan" control in the plan summary panel.
   - The page navigates to membership.html (no additional parameters required by this page definition).
   - From membership.html, the user selects a new plan, which redirects them again to membership_apply.html?planId=... with the new plan.

5. Error handling during application start:
   - User fills the form and submits; startMembershipApplication is called.
   - If the API returns an error (e.g., server-side validation, temporarily unavailable), the error is captured.
   - The application status / error component shows the error message without losing user-entered form data.
   - The submit button is re-enabled so the user can correct input and resubmit if appropriate.
interactions:
On page load:
       - Event: onLoad (page-init-loader component).
       - Action: Read planId from URL query string.
       - Action: Call getMembershipPlans with no parameters.
       - Action: Set loading state until API response is received.
       - On success: store returned plans and currency; find selectedPlan where plan.id === planId; update components.
       - On error: store error state and show a generic loading error, keeping the application form disabled.

Plan summary display:
       - Data binding: selectedPlan (resolved from getMembershipPlans) and currency.
       - Interaction: If selectedPlan exists, show its name, planType, description, annualPriceAmount/annualPriceCurrency, and benefits list.
       - Interaction: If selectedPlan is null, show a message that the plan cannot be found and hide/disable the application form.

"Change plan" navigation:
       - Event: onClick on "Change plan" link/button.
       - Action: Navigate to membership.html via standard link (no additional state storage).
       - Effect: Current page is unloaded; any partially entered form data is discarded unless the browser restores it.

Form input interactions:
       - Event: onFieldChange for each form control (fullName, email, country, roleOrStatus).
       - Action: Update internal form state and clear field-level error messages when a user modifies the corresponding field.
       - Optional: Trigger real-time validation for fields (e.g., email format) on blur.

Form submission:
       - Event: onSubmit on the membership-application-form.
       - Action: Run client-side validation rules:
            * Check required fields: fullName, email, country are non-empty.
            * If selectedPlan.planType is a value that requires explicit role/status (e.g., a non-"other" type), ensure roleOrStatus is non-empty.
            * Validate email string against a basic email pattern.
       - If validation fails: populate validation-error-list component with the issues; block API call.
       - If validation passes:
            * Disable submit button; set submitting state.
            * Call startMembershipApplication with current planId and form values.

Handling startMembershipApplication response:
       - On success:
            * Event: onApplicationStartSuccess.
            * Action: Store returned MembershipApplication object (including id, planId, status, createdAt) and response.message in application-status-panel.
            * Action: Lock or visually mark the form as completed to avoid duplicate submissions.
            * Action: Expose a "Continue" button that triggers an onContinue event for routing to the next step of the membership process (actual navigation target handled outside this page specification).
       - On error:
            * Event: onApplicationStartError.
            * Action: Show an error message in application-status-panel or validation-error-list.
            * Action: Re-enable the submit button and keep user-entered data intact.

Application continuation:
       - Event: onClick on "Continue" after a successful application start.
       - Action: Emit an applicationStarted event containing the MembershipApplication.id and planId for higher-level navigation logic; the page itself does not define the next URL.

Page reload / URL changes:
       - If user or external navigation changes the URL planId (e.g., browser navigation), the page-init-loader component reads the new planId and refilters the already-loaded plans (if still in memory) or re-calls getMembershipPlans as configured, then updates selectedPlan and resets form state accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the planId URL parameter. On every page load, the page reads planId from the query string (e.g., membership_apply.html?planId=PLAN_ID). The page then calls getMembershipPlans and, once the plans are loaded, resolves the selected plan as the plan whose id equals planId. If planId is missing or no plan matches, the page enters a 'no valid plan' state: the membership application form is disabled/hidden and a message with a link back to membership.html is shown. The internal page state includes: (1) loading state for getMembershipPlans, (2) selectedPlan (object or null), (3) form fields (fullName, email, country, roleOrStatus), (4) field-level and form-level validation errors, (5) submission state for startMembershipApplication (idle, submitting, success, error), and (6) the created MembershipApplication object after a successful submission. This internal state is held in memory for the lifetime of the page and is not persisted via localStorage or other client storage. Any navigation away from the page (e.g., via 'Change plan' to membership.html or browser navigation) clears this state; when the page is re-entered, state is fully reconstructed from the current URL parameters and API responses.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the planId from the URL, loading active membership plans via getMembershipPlans, and resolving the selected plan for the application.

Displays details of the selected membership plan, including its name, planType, description, pricing information, and benefits. If no valid plan is found for the planId, it shows an appropriate message and prevents application actions.
Shows plan-specific notes that may include eligibility guidance, deriving content from fields such as the plan description and benefits. Hidden when no plan is selected.
Captures applicant information (fullName, email, country, roleOrStatus) for the selected membership plan and coordinates validation and submission via startMembershipApplication.
Displays validation errors for the application form, including both client-side validation messages and any field-specific errors returned from startMembershipApplication.

Shows the status of the membership application creation process. After a successful call to startMembershipApplication, it presents the returned message, key information from the MembershipApplication (such as id and status), and exposes a control to continue to the next step of the membership process.
Provides a direct way for users to abandon the current plan selection and navigate back to membership.html to choose a different membership plan.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit in a single, narrow, vertically stacked content column centered beneath the existing full-width header. The order from top to bottom (within the main content area) is: page title (ambient), plan-summary-panel with the change-plan-link visually attached to it, plan-eligibility-info, application-status-panel, validation-error-list, and finally the membership-application-form. The page-init-loader floats above this entire stack when active. The arrangement preserves the mockup’s simple top-to-bottom reading flow with no sidebars or multi-column splits.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit in a single, narrow, vertically stacked content column centered beneath the existing full-width header. The order from top to bottom (within the main content area) is: page title (ambient), plan-summary-panel with the change-plan-link visually attached to it, plan-eligibility-info, application-status-panel, validation-error-list, and finally the membership-application-form. The page-init-loader floats above this entire stack when active. The arrangement preserves the mockup’s simple top-to-bottom reading flow with no sidebars or multi-column splits.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header band occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. Below it, a wide expanse of white space leads into a centered content column that spans about 50–60% of the viewport width (max ~800px), leaving generous left and right margins. Within this column, each component is given ample vertical breathing room: roughly 48–64px between major blocks (plan summary, eligibility info, status, form). The form itself occupies the largest vertical portion of the column, but overall the page still feels spacious because of the narrow width and consistent padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low by using a narrow column, comfortable line lengths, and substantial vertical spacing between sections and between field groups within the form. Text within panels is primarily left-aligned, with minimal ornamentation, light borders at most, and no dense clusters of controls. Even though the membership-application-form may be the longest element, fields are stacked in a simple single column with generous spacing so the layout feels calm and academic rather than busy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the dark header down into the white canvas, where it first encounters the centered page title, then immediately the plan-summary-panel. From there the reader flows downward through plan-eligibility-info, then any application-status-panel, and then the validation-error-list when present. The flow naturally ends on the membership-application-form fields, where interaction happens. The change-plan-link is visually tied to the plan-summary-panel (typically at its top-right) so that it is discovered as part of understanding the selected plan, without disrupting the main vertical reading path. The page-init-loader, when shown, temporarily captures attention by dimming the content and centering a small loading indicator in the viewport.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width black header, the page opens into a large, quiet white field. Centered within this is a relatively narrow content column—about half to a bit more than half of the page width—with wide white margins on both sides. After a comfortable vertical gap from the header, the user meets the page title and, immediately beneath it, a concise summary of the chosen membership plan. A small, understated change-plan link sits visually attached to this summary, making it easy to switch plans without introducing visual noise. Dropping down the column, the user next reads a plain, text-heavy eligibility information panel with generous spacing, followed by a compact status message area that indicates where they are in the application process. Any validation errors are then listed clearly above the form itself, using restrained red text similar to how email addresses were highlighted in the mockup. The application form occupies the lower portion of the column, but remains calm and readable thanks to its single-column field layout and ample vertical spacing between groups. If initial data is loading, a subtle overlay sits over this entire central column, dimming it and centering a loader, while the rest of the page stays visually quiet. The overall impression matches the mockup: a conservative, text-centric, corporate-academic page with a strong top header, a centered island of content, and plenty of white space allowing each piece of information to stand on its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the page-init-loader appears as a semi-opaque veil covering the entire main content area below the header, extending from the bottom edge of the header to the bottom of the viewport (and continuing down if the page scrolls). It sits centered horizontally and vertically in the viewport, with a small, simple loading indicator or message occupying roughly 10–15% of the viewport width. The underlying centered content column (plan summary, eligibility, form, etc.) remains visible but subtly dimmed, reinforcing that loading is in progress while preserving the overall page structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay within the viewport when active, positioned at the top of the stacking order above all other components; does not scroll with the underlying content while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-summary-panel is the first substantive block within the centered content column, placed directly below the page title with approximately 48–64px of white space separating it from the title. It spans about 60% of the viewport width (max ~800px), aligned to the center of the page, with equal white margins on left and right. The panel itself is a simple, text-forward block: a subtle bordered or lightly shaded rectangle with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Plan name and key details are left-aligned inside this block, but the block as a whole is visually centered. There is generous space (24px+) above and below the main lines of text to keep the area feeling light and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the main centered column; scrolls with the page and maintains its relative position beneath the header and page title.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-eligibility-info component sits directly below the plan-summary-panel, separated by roughly 40–56px of vertical white space to preserve the mockup’s airy feel. It aligns with the same centered content column and matches the width of the plan summary (about 60% of the viewport, max ~800px). Visually it appears as a plain text panel, slightly less visually heavy than the plan summary: it may have a very subtle border or simply rely on spacing and typography. Text is left-aligned and arranged in short paragraphs or a simple list, with comfortable line spacing and ample space between items to keep the density low. The bottom of this panel also leaves 40–56px of whitespace before the next component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the main centered column; scrolls naturally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The membership-application-form occupies the lower portion of the main content column and is placed after a small information stack: it sits below the plan-eligibility-info, the application-status-panel, and the validation-error-list. The top of the form begins approximately 32–48px below any preceding message or status panel, preserving the sense of separation between information and interaction. The form shares the same centered width as the other components (around 60% of the viewport, max ~800px), with left-aligned labels and inputs arranged in a single vertical column. Each field group (label + input, or a small related set) is separated by generous vertical spacing—about 24–32px—to avoid a dense, cramped appearance. Overall form height may be substantial, but the narrow column, ample line height, and consistent vertical rhythm keep it light and easy to scan, in line with the mockup’s text-centric design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in the main centered column; scrolls with the page so that longer forms extend downward, maintaining alignment with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation-error-list appears directly above the membership-application-form within the same centered column, separated from the form by about 16–24px and from the component above it (typically the application-status-panel or eligibility info) by about 24–32px. It matches the same width as the form and other panels (roughly 60% of viewport, max ~800px) and is left-aligned inside that column. Visually it is a text-focused block with a light, clearly delineated boundary or subtle background, and error messages are stacked in a simple vertical list. Red is used sparingly for text or bullet markers to highlight issues, echoing the mockup’s use of red for email addresses while keeping the overall look conservative. The block itself is compact in height, with enough space between each error line to maintain readability without feeling crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element; appears and disappears inline based on validation state but otherwise scrolls with the form and surrounding content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application-status-panel is positioned between the plan-eligibility-info and the validation-error-list, centered within the same content column. There is about 32–40px of white space above it (from the eligibility panel) and 24–32px below it (before the next component). It spans the full width of the main column (again, about 60% of viewport, max ~800px) and reads as a slim, text-centric status strip—perhaps a single sentence or short paragraph. Styling is understated: a light border on the top and bottom or a very soft background tint to distinguish it from standard body text, without competing visually with the plan summary or the form. The content is left-aligned but the block itself is horizontally centered, and its vertical height is relatively small compared to other panels, reinforcing its role as a contextual status note.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element inline with the content column; appears only when applicable and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The change-plan-link is visually coupled with the plan-summary-panel near the top of the content column. Within the same centered width as the plan summary, the link is positioned at the top-right corner of the plan-summary-panel’s interior, on the same baseline or just above the primary plan title. The link is relatively small and text-only, using a subtle accent color or underline to indicate interactivity while remaining consistent with the conservative, academic aesthetic. If space or responsiveness requires a simpler layout, the link may instead sit directly below the plan summary content, aligned to the right edge of the column, with about 8–12px of vertical separation from the summary text. In both cases, it remains close enough to the plan summary that users perceive it as an option related to that block, without interrupting the vertical flow of information down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline text link within or immediately adjacent to the plan-summary-panel; it moves with that panel and maintains its relative alignment on scroll.

name: My Account & Saved Items
title: My Account & Saved Items
description: Page where signed-in users manage their personal conference schedule, bookmarked conferences, saved publications, and followed researchers, with tab-based navigation and deep links into detail pages.

core_features: Load a unified view of the user’s data (schedule, bookmarked conferences, reading list, followed researchers) using getMyAccountDetails and present it in separate sections.
Provide tab-based navigation between 'My Schedule', 'Bookmarked Conferences', 'My Reading List', and 'Followed Researchers', controlled by the section URL parameter.

Display a structured list of sessions in 'My Schedule', including conference and track context, with the ability to open session details in the conference program and remove sessions using getPersonalSchedule and removeSessionFromPersonalSchedule.
Display a list of bookmarked conferences with quick access to each conference detail page and the ability to unbookmark conferences using getBookmarkedConferences and unbookmarkConference.

Display a 'My Reading List' of saved publications, including journal context where available, with navigation to publication detail pages and the ability to remove items using getReadingList and removePublicationFromReadingList.
Display all followed researchers with basic profile information, navigation to researcher profile pages, and the ability to unfollow using getFollowedResearchers and unfollowResearcher.
Provide context-aware empty states and calls-to-action so that users can jump to conferences.html, publications.html, or researchers.html when a section has no items.

Offer refresh actions per section so users can re-fetch updated data using the corresponding getPersonalSchedule, getBookmarkedConferences, getReadingList, or getFollowedResearchers interfaces after changes.
user_workflows: Open My Account from the header: The user clicks the 'My Account' navigation link (without a section parameter). The page initializes, defaults the active tab to a pre-defined section (for example, 'my_schedule'), calls getMyAccountDetails once, and populates the four sections. The tab corresponding to the default section is marked active.

Deep-link to a specific section: From other pages or the header, the user clicks 'My Schedule' or 'My Reading List'. The browser opens my_account.html with ?section=my_schedule or ?section=reading_list. On load, the page reads the section URL parameter, sets that tab as active, calls getMyAccountDetails, and scrolls or focuses the relevant section so the user immediately sees the requested content.

Review and manage the personal schedule: In the 'My Schedule' tab, the user sees a list of personal schedule items derived from the schedule part of getMyAccountDetails or from getPersonalSchedule. Each item shows session, conference, and (if available) track information. The user can click a session title, which navigates to conference_detail.html with id set to the related conference.id and sessionId set to the session.id to view it in context. If the user clicks a 'Remove from schedule' action on an item, the page calls removeSessionFromPersonalSchedule with the relevant sessionId, and on success updates the schedule view either by removing the item locally or by re-calling getPersonalSchedule.

View and manage bookmarked conferences: In the 'Bookmarked Conferences' tab, the user sees a list of bookmarked conferences retrieved either from getMyAccountDetails.bookmarkedConferences or via getBookmarkedConferences. Clicking a conference title navigates to conference_detail.html with the conference.id in the id URL parameter. Clicking an 'Unbookmark' action calls unbookmarkConference with the conferenceId; on success, the item is removed from the list or the list is refreshed by calling getBookmarkedConferences. If there are no bookmarks, an empty-state component offers a link to conferences.html ('Find more conferences').

View and manage the reading list: In the 'My Reading List' tab, the user sees saved publications (with journal details when available) using getMyAccountDetails.readingList or getReadingList. Clicking a publication title navigates to publication_detail.html with the publication.id in the id URL parameter. Clicking 'Remove from reading list' on an item calls removePublicationFromReadingList with the publicationId, and on success the UI removes or refreshes the item list by re-calling getReadingList. If the list is empty, the page shows an empty state with a link to publications.html ('Discover more publications').

View and manage followed researchers: In the 'Followed Researchers' tab, the user sees researchers they follow, derived from getMyAccountDetails.followedResearchers or getFollowedResearchers. Clicking a researcher name navigates to researcher_profile.html with the researcher.id in the id URL parameter. Clicking 'Unfollow' calls unfollowResearcher with the researcherId, and on success the researcher is removed from the list or the list is refreshed via getFollowedResearchers. If there are no followed researchers, an empty state offers a link to researchers.html ('Explore more researchers').

Switch tabs within My Account: While on my_account.html, the user clicks another tab (for example, from 'My Schedule' to 'My Reading List'). The tab navigation component updates the section URL parameter (for example, to section=reading_list) using client-side navigation. The active tab changes without requiring a full page reload; if data for that tab has not yet been loaded or has been invalidated, the page triggers the appropriate fetch (getReadingList or a full getMyAccountDetails) before rendering.

Refresh a specific section after changes elsewhere: After the user adds a session to their personal schedule from conference_detail.html, a success message or link leads them back to my_account.html?section=my_schedule. When the page loads (or when the user manually clicks a 'Refresh' control within the schedule section), getPersonalSchedule is invoked to ensure the latest data is displayed. Similar refresh workflows apply for bookmarks, reading list, and followed researchers using their respective get* interfaces.
interactions: Tab click interaction: When the user clicks a tab header, the onClickTab handler updates the section URL parameter to the corresponding value and sets the internal activeSection state so the correct content panel is visible.

Session link interaction: When the user clicks a session title in 'My Schedule', the onClickSessionLink handler reads the associated conference.id and session.id and navigates the browser to conference_detail.html with id and sessionId URL parameters set from these values.

Remove session interaction: When the user clicks 'Remove from schedule' on a session, the onClickRemoveSession handler calls removeSessionFromPersonalSchedule with the sessionId from the bound PersonalScheduleItem. Based on the returned success flag, it either removes the item from the list and shows a confirmation message or displays an error message without altering the list.

Conference link interaction: When the user clicks a bookmarked conference title, the onClickConferenceLink handler navigates to conference_detail.html with the id parameter set to the bound Conference.id.

Unbookmark interaction: When the user clicks 'Unbookmark' on a conference, the onClickUnbookmark handler calls unbookmarkConference with the relevant conferenceId and, when successful, updates the bookmarks list (either by removing the item locally or by calling getBookmarkedConferences again).

Publication link interaction: When the user clicks a publication title in 'My Reading List', the onClickPublicationLink handler navigates to publication_detail.html with the id parameter set to the Publication.id for that row.

Remove publication interaction: When the user clicks 'Remove from reading list', the onClickRemovePublication handler calls removePublicationFromReadingList with the publicationId and, on success, refreshes the list or removes that row and shows a confirmation message.

Researcher profile link interaction: When the user clicks a followed researcher’s name, the onClickResearcherLink handler navigates to researcher_profile.html with the researcher.id passed as the id URL parameter.

Unfollow interaction: When the user clicks 'Unfollow' next to a researcher, the onClickUnfollowResearcher handler calls unfollowResearcher with the researcherId and, on success, removes the researcher from the view or reloads the list from getFollowedResearchers.

Empty-state CTA interactions: When a section has no items, clicking the primary CTA in that section triggers navigation to the relevant listing page (conferences.html, publications.html, or researchers.html) without additional parameters.

Section refresh interactions: In each section, an optional 'Refresh' control’s onClickRefresh handler re-fetches the corresponding dataset using getPersonalSchedule, getBookmarkedConferences, getReadingList, or getFollowedResearchers to ensure the view reflects the latest saved items.

Query-based load interaction: On page load or when the URL changes (for example, via browser navigation between history entries), the onSectionParamChange handler parses the section query parameter, validates its value, and updates the active tab and visible section accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary client-visible state for this page is the active section, controlled by the section URL parameter. Supported values include 'my_schedule', 'bookmarks', 'reading_list', and 'followed_researchers'. On initial load, the page reads window.location.search, extracts section, and checks if it matches one of the supported keys. If it matches, that section is set as active; if not provided or invalid, the page falls back to a predefined default section. When the user switches tabs, the tab navigation component updates the browser URL (for example, my_account.html?section=reading_list) via client-side navigation, ensuring that the current section is encoded in the URL for deep-linking and bookmarking. The internal activeSection state is always derived from the current URL so that using the browser back/forward buttons correctly updates the visible tab. Data loading is coordinated with this state: on page load, getMyAccountDetails is called once to retrieve schedule, bookmarkedConferences, readingList, and followedResearchers. Each section binds to its relevant subset of this response. When section-specific refresh actions or filters are used, the page may call getPersonalSchedule, getBookmarkedConferences, getReadingList, or getFollowedResearchers to refresh just that section without changing the URL. No persistent client state is stored in localStorage or similar; all restorable state comes from the URL and the latest interface responses.

components.functionality: Initializes the My Account & Saved Items page, parses the section URL parameter to determine the active tab, triggers the initial getMyAccountDetails call, and distributes the resulting data to the schedule, bookmarks, reading list, and followed researchers sections. Manages global loading and error states for the page.

Renders the main navigation tabs for the My Account page (My Schedule, Bookmarked Conferences, My Reading List, Followed Researchers). Reads the current section value from the URL, highlights the active tab, and updates the URL and internal state when the user switches tabs.

Displays the user’s personal schedule items including session title, time, conference information, and optional track information. Uses data from the schedule portion of getMyAccountDetails or from getPersonalSchedule. Provides actions to navigate to a session within its conference program and to remove sessions using removeSessionFromPersonalSchedule. Optionally exposes filter controls that, when changed, call getPersonalSchedule with a filters object to refine the list.

Shows all conferences bookmarked by the user, using the bookmarkedConferences data from getMyAccountDetails or the bookmarks from getBookmarkedConferences. Each row displays core conference information and provides a link to the conference detail page as well as an 'Unbookmark' action powered by unbookmarkConference. If the list is empty, it renders a contextual empty state with a link to conferences.html.

Lists all publications saved in the user’s reading list using the readingList data from getMyAccountDetails or the items from getReadingList. For each item, displays publication metadata and journal information when available, provides a link to the publication detail page, and allows removal from the reading list via removePublicationFromReadingList. When no items are present, shows an empty state with navigation to publications.html.

Displays researchers the user follows using followedResearchers from getMyAccountDetails or items from getFollowedResearchers. Each row shows key researcher details and offers a link to their profile page as well as an 'Unfollow' action backed by unfollowResearcher. If there are no followed researchers, shows an empty state with a link to researchers.html.

Reusable component that detects when the bound collection for a section has no items and displays a context-appropriate message and primary call-to-action. Depending on the section it is used in, the CTA navigates to conferences.html, publications.html, or researchers.html using standard browser navigation.

Shows inline success or error messages in response to mutation actions (removing sessions, unbookmarking conferences, removing publications, unfollowing researchers). Interprets the success and message fields from removeSessionFromPersonalSchedule, unbookmarkConference, removePublicationFromReadingList, and unfollowResearcher responses and renders them within the relevant section.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: All account content sits in a single, centered column beneath the existing global header bands (info bar + main nav). At the top of this column, a page title and brief descriptor are followed immediately by the tab navigation. Only one content section (schedule, bookmarks, reading list, or followed researchers) is visible at a time, directly under the tabs, keeping the layout strictly linear and top-to-bottom with no sidebars or multi-column body content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All account content sits in a single, centered column beneath the existing global header bands (info bar + main nav). At the top of this column, a page title and brief descriptor are followed immediately by the tab navigation. Only one content section (schedule, bookmarks, reading list, or followed researchers) is visible at a time, directly under the tabs, keeping the layout strictly linear and top-to-bottom with no sidebars or multi-column body content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header continues to occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The My Account page shell occupies the remaining vertical space as a white canvas. The main content column is relatively narrow (about 50–60% of the viewport width on desktop) and centered, leaving wide left/right margins. Within this column, each major element (page title, feedback messages, tabs, section content, empty states) is separated by generous vertical whitespace—roughly 2–3 times the body text line height—so that each block feels isolated and calm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low: lists are simple vertical stacks, each item spanning the full width of the content column but separated by ample vertical gaps. There is no visual clutter such as dense tables or multi-column grids. Tabs use concise labels, and each visible section focuses on a small number of key actions (view, manage, remove) instead of exposing lots of controls at once. Empty states are minimalist, with a short message and a single call-to-action, preserving the spacious, text-centric feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the user’s eye is drawn to the dark site header, it moves down to the centered page title in the white content area, then immediately to the horizontal tab navigation as the primary interaction point. From the active tab, the eye follows a straightforward vertical reading flow through any feedback messages, the section heading (if used), and then down the list of items. When lists are short or empty, the layout emphasizes a central focal point—the empty state message and CTA—anchored in the middle of the content column. The final visual anchors in most sections are right-aligned or inline action links (e.g., 'Remove', 'View details'), which act as subtle calls-to-action without breaking the text-heavy aesthetic.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s dark header bands, the My Account page appears as a broad white field with a single, centered column of content. This column is relatively narrow and sits comfortably inside wide side margins, giving the page a quiet, academic feel. At the top of the column, a modest 'My Account & Saved Items' title is center-aligned or lightly left-aligned within the column, with ample white space above and below. Any feedback messages form a slim band just under the title, spanning the column width. Directly beneath, a horizontally arranged tab navigation bar runs across the width of the column, its labels evenly spaced and text-focused. The active tab’s content occupies the space below, with either a list of schedule entries, bookmarked conferences, saved publications, or followed researchers. Each list is a simple vertical stack of text blocks with generous gaps between items and minimal chrome, reinforcing the low-density, text-centric design DNA. When a section has no content, the list area collapses into a centered empty-state message with a brief explanation and a single, clearly visible link or button, surrounded by plenty of whitespace. The overall impression is of a calm, ordered page where content is easily scannable, and the user’s focus moves naturally from the header to the tabs and then down through the selected section’s items or messages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell occupies the full width of the browser window beneath the existing site header bands, extending from just below the dark navigation bar to the bottom of the viewport. Within this full-width area, a centered content column defines the actual My Account area: on desktop, this column spans approximately 50–60% of the viewport width, with equal left and right margins of 20–25% each. The column starts with a top padding band that provides about 5–8% of the viewport height of empty space between the global header and the first line of content. Within the shell, the content is stacked vertically in this order: page title and optional subtitle at the top, then feedback messages (if present), then the tab navigation bar, and then the currently active content section. The bottom of the column leaves generous whitespace after the last visible content—roughly one full viewport’s worth of scrollable space at minimum when content is short—mirroring the mockup’s impression of a compact center cluster of text floating in a large white canvas.
