component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that scrolls with the page; the centered column width is fixed at a max-width (e.g., 960–1040px) beyond which it does not grow, maintaining wide side margins on large screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tab navigation sits within the centered content column, directly below the page title/intro block and any feedback messages. It spans nearly the full width of the column (around 90–100% of the column width), aligned horizontally. Tabs are arranged as a single row of text labels, evenly spaced from left to right. Each tab’s label is short and set in a conservative, readable typeface; the active tab is indicated by a subtle visual cue (such as a bottom border line or a slightly bolder text weight) rather than a heavy background color. Vertical spacing above and below the tab row is generous: approximately one line of body text above (to separate it from the title/feedback) and 1.5–2 lines below (before the section content begins), maintaining the calm vertical rhythm. The tabs are horizontally centered within the content column but may be aligned so that the left-most tab starts near the left edge of the column, consistent with a traditional academic navigation feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal bar anchored in the flow; it scrolls off-screen with the rest of the content and does not stick to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The My Schedule section appears directly beneath the tab navigation when its corresponding tab is active. It occupies the full width of the centered content column and extends downward as needed. A subtle section heading (e.g., 'My Conference Schedule') may be placed at the top of this block, left-aligned within the column, with a moderate space (approximately a single line height) below the tabs and another line height below the heading before the first item. Schedule entries are listed as a vertical stack of text blocks, each item occupying the entire column width with no side-by-side content. Within each item, key details (conference name, session title, date/time, location) are stacked with small internal spacing, while a primary action (e.g., 'View details' or 'Remove from schedule') is positioned either at the right end of the last line or as a simple text link directly below the metadata, right-aligned. Items are separated by generous vertical gaps (around 1.5–2 times the body text line height), and there is substantial blank space at the bottom of the section after the last item, echoing the mockup’s low density and airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Only one main section (including this one) is visible at a time under the tabs; it expands vertically according to the number of items and scrolls within the page shell.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Bookmarked Conferences section occupies the same spatial footprint as the My Schedule section and is swapped into view when its tab is active. It starts immediately below the tab navigation (or section heading if used) and spans the full width of the centered content column. A modest section header such as 'Bookmarked Conferences' can be left-aligned at the top, with a comfortable gap below the tabs. Below the heading, bookmarked conferences are rendered as a vertical list of text entries. Each conference item uses the full column width and stacks its content: conference name (prominent), dates, location, and possibly a small line indicating the number of related sessions saved. A simple text link such as 'View conference' and a secondary 'Remove bookmark' action are placed at the bottom of each item, typically right-aligned or set in a small horizontal line aligned with the right edge of the column. Vertical spacing between items is consistent with other sections—ample gaps of around 1.5–2 line heights—so that even a long list feels uncluttered. The bottom of the section fades into whitespace, matching the restrained, text-first aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Exclusive with other sections under the tabs; it appears and disappears based on the active tab, while maintaining the same centered, scrollable area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Reading List section replaces other sections when its tab is active, occupying the same centered column area. It begins directly below the tab navigation, possibly with a left-aligned heading like 'Saved Publications.' Below this, saved publications are displayed as a vertical sequence of text blocks spanning the column width. Each item includes the paper title on its own line (most prominent), followed by authors, publication venue, and year on subsequent lines. An action row—containing minimal text links such as 'View publication' and 'Remove from list'—appears at the bottom of the item, aligned to the right edge of the column or slightly indented but still right-biased. Vertical space between one publication item and the next is slightly larger than the internal spacing within an item, giving each entry a clear, separate presence. The entire section is kept visually light: no item borders or heavy backgrounds, relying on whitespace and consistent alignment to structure the content. Adequate blank space remains at the bottom of the column once the list ends, preserving the mockup’s impression of a compact center of text within a large white area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Visible only when its tab is active, occupying the same scrollable region below the tabs as the other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Followed Researchers section shares the same structural area beneath the tab navigation and appears when its tab is selected. Starting just below the tabs (and an optional left-aligned header like 'Followed Researchers'), it spans the full width of the centered column. Researchers are displayed as a single-column vertical list. Each researcher item is a text-focused block: the researcher’s name appears first, slightly more prominent, followed by one or two lines of secondary details (affiliation, main field, or a brief tag line). A small set of text actions—such as 'View profile' and 'Unfollow'—is positioned at the bottom of each item, aligned flush right, respecting the overall minimalist link style. Items are separated by generous vertical spacing similar to other lists, avoiding any dense, directory-like feel. There is no avatar grid or card chrome; everything is arranged as simple text blocks with ample breathing room above and below, reinforcing the academic and conservative tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Swaps in as the only visible section under its tab; its height is determined by the number of followed researchers and scrolls naturally in the page shell.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state component appears inside whichever section is active when that section has no items. Instead of a list, the section’s content area collapses into a single, centrally positioned message block within the centered column. This block is vertically offset from the tabs by more whitespace than a normal list—approximately 2–3 line heights—to give a sense of a deliberate, focal message. The empty-state text (e.g., 'You have no saved publications yet.') is center-aligned within the column, with a maximum width slightly narrower than the main column (about 70–80% of the column width) to keep the text compact. Below the message, a single, simple call-to-action link or button is placed, also center-aligned, with moderate spacing (around one line height) between the message and the CTA. The surrounding area remains blank, extending both above and below the empty-state block, exaggerating the feeling of a small, central cluster of text in a large white space, very much echoing the mockup’s Jobs page impression.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the list content inside any given section; it remains centered within the content column and scrolls with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Feedback messages appear as a slim horizontal band of text within the centered content column, positioned between the page title/intro and the tab navigation. They span nearly the full width of the column (around 90–100%), with left-aligned or center-aligned text depending on severity and style. The height of this band is minimal—roughly one or two lines of text—surrounded by generous vertical padding (about half a line height above and below) so it feels distinct but not heavy. Color is used sparingly: for example, a subtle tinted background or a thin colored left border might indicate success or error states, but the overall look remains restrained to match the conservative academic style. Multiple feedback messages would stack vertically with small gaps, still confined to this narrow zone above the tabs. Once the user scrolls, the messages move away with the content; they do not remain fixed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline block in the flow of the page; appears only when there is a message to show and does not overlay other components.

name: About the Organization
title: About the Organization
description: Page that loads and presents dynamic information about the organization’s mission, history, focus areas, programs, and leadership using getAboutOrganizationContent, and routes users to membership, donation, conferences, and contact pages.

core_features: Load organization information (mission, history, focus areas, program descriptions, leadership) via getAboutOrganizationContent on page initialization.
Display separate structured sections for mission, history, focus areas, programs, and leadership, driven entirely by the data returned from getAboutOrganizationContent.
Provide internal section navigation that uses URL parameters to deep-link to specific sections (e.g., mission, history, programs, leadership).
Highlight or focus a specific program in the programs section based on a URL parameter (e.g., programCode) without hardcoding program content.
Offer a ‘Become a member’ link that navigates to membership.html from the membership/community section of the page.
Offer a ‘Support Early Career Research Grants’ call-to-action that navigates to donate.html with the required programId URL parameter.
Offer a ‘View our conferences’ link that navigates to conferences.html from the programs / conferences context.
Offer a ‘Contact us’ link for general inquiries that navigates to contact.html.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: General visitor reads about the organization
1. User opens about.html (possibly from the footer or an external link, without URL parameters).
2. On page initialization, the page calls getAboutOrganizationContent.
3. Once data is returned, the mission, history, focus areas, programs, and leadership sections are rendered.
4. User scrolls through the sections or uses the internal section navigation to jump to areas of interest.
5. If the user wants to inquire further, they click the ‘Contact us’ link, which navigates to contact.html.

Workflow 2: Visitor interested in membership
1. User opens about.html (with or without section parameters).
2. Page loads data via getAboutOrganizationContent and renders the sections.
3. User navigates to or scrolls to the community/membership-related part of the programs or a dedicated membership context in the content.
4. User clicks ‘Become a member’.
5. The page navigates to membership.html (optionally preserving current section in a URL parameter on the target page if desired by the overall site design, but this page’s responsibility is to trigger navigation to membership.html).

Workflow 3: Visitor comes to support early career research
1. User opens about.html, possibly with a query like ?section=programs&programCode=early_career_research_grants from an external link.
2. On initialization, about.html calls getAboutOrganizationContent and waits for the programDescriptions array.
3. After load, the programs section locates the program whose programCode matches the programCode URL parameter and highlights or brings it into view (if present).
4. Within that program’s card, the ‘Support Early Career Research Grants’ call-to-action is displayed if the program’s programCode matches the expected donation program code.
5. User clicks the call-to-action.
6. Browser navigates to donate.html with the URL parameter programId=early_career_research_grants.
7. The donation page uses that programId according to its own logic (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow 4: Visitor wants to see conferences organized by the organization
1. User opens about.html.
2. Page loads content via getAboutOrganizationContent and renders the programs section, which includes information about conferences (if provided in programDescriptions or other program-related text).
3. User locates the part of the programs section that discusses conferences.
4. User clicks ‘View our conferences’.
5. The page navigates to conferences.html, where conference-specific data is handled by that page.

Workflow 5: User uses section deep-links
1. User clicks a section link in the internal navigation (e.g., ‘Mission’, ‘History’, ‘Programs’, ‘Leadership’).
2. The click handler updates the URL’s query parameter section (for example, about.html?section=history) using pushState or by reloading with the new URL.
3. The page scrolls or focuses to the corresponding section and visually indicates that it is active.
4. If the user copies and shares the URL, another user opening that link will see the same section selected and scrolled into view, because the page reads the section parameter on load.

Workflow 6: User deep-links to a specific program
1. An external site links to about.html with a programCode query parameter (e.g., about.html?section=programs&programCode=<some-code>).
2. On page load, the about-content-loader component calls getAboutOrganizationContent.
3. When programDescriptions have been loaded, the programs section checks for a program whose programCode matches the URL’s programCode parameter.
4. If a match exists, the corresponding program item is visually emphasized and scrolled into view; if there is no match, the programs section displays normally without a specific item highlighted.
interactions: Click internal navigation item: Updates the URL’s section query parameter and scrolls to the selected section (mission, history, focus_areas, programs, leadership).
Click ‘Become a member’: Navigates to membership.html using a standard link or programmatic navigation.
Click ‘Support Early Career Research Grants’ call-to-action: Navigates to donate.html with the URL parameter programId=early_career_research_grants.
Click ‘View our conferences’: Navigates to conferences.html.
Click ‘Contact us’: Navigates to contact.html.

Initial page load: Triggers getAboutOrganizationContent; while loading, the page may display a generic loading state; on error, the page shows a non-static error message and may offer a reload action.

URL change (manual or via browser navigation): When section or programCode query parameters change (e.g., via back/forward navigation), the page re-applies section selection and program highlighting without recalling getAboutOrganizationContent unless a full reload occurs.
Optional reload interaction: A user-initiated reload or retry action (e.g., clicking a ‘Retry’ control in the loader) re-triggers getAboutOrganizationContent.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL query parameters to manage state. Supported parameters include: (1) section: Defines which main section is considered active when the page loads or when the URL changes. Expected values map to section identifiers such as mission, history, focus_areas, programs, leadership. When section is present, the page scrolls to the corresponding section and marks it active in the internal navigation. If section is missing or does not match any known section key, the page uses a default section (e.g., mission) or shows all sections without a specific active state. (2) programCode: Used by the programs section to identify a specific program item within the programDescriptions array returned by getAboutOrganizationContent. On load, after data is available, the programs component searches for a program with programCode equal to the programCode query parameter. If found, it highlights or auto-expands this program’s item and may scroll it into view. If no matching program exists, the programs list remains unfiltered. (3) Additional parameters not used by this page are ignored. The page does not use localStorage or other client-side storage; all state that needs to be shareable or deep-linkable is derived from the current URL and the single API response from getAboutOrganizationContent. Any in-page interactions that change section or targeted program update the URL’s query parameters accordingly (via history.pushState or equivalent), ensuring that navigation and deep-link behavior remain consistent.

components.functionality: Initialize the page by calling getAboutOrganizationContent, manage loading and error states, and provide the returned mission, history, focusAreas, programDescriptions, and leadership data to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getAboutOrganizationContent with no parameters when about.html is loaded.
onLoadSuccess: Store the returned data in memory for the lifetime of the page and notify dependent components that content is available.
onLoadError: Set an error state that can be rendered by the loader (e.g., display a generic error and a retry control).
onRetryClick: Re-invoke getAboutOrganizationContent to attempt another load when the user activates a retry control.

components.functionality: Render navigation links to the main sections of the About page (mission, history, focus areas, programs, leadership) and keep the active section in sync with the URL’s section parameter.
event_handlers: onClickNavItem: When a navigation item (e.g., mission, history, programs) is clicked, update the section query parameter in the URL (e.g., about.html?section=mission) and scroll/focus to the corresponding section component.
onUrlChange: When the browser’s URL changes due to back/forward navigation or manual edits, re-evaluate URLSearchParams.section and update which navigation item is marked active.

onContentLoaded: Once getAboutOrganizationContent data is available, ensure that section navigation links are only rendered for sections where content is present (e.g., only show a focus areas item if focusAreas exists and is not empty).

components.functionality: Display the organization’s mission text as returned by getAboutOrganizationContent and respond to section selection and deep-linking.
event_handlers: onSectionActivated: When URLSearchParams.section equals the mission section key, scroll this section into view and mark it as active for accessibility (e.g., adjust heading focus or ARIA attributes).

components.functionality: Display the organization’s history narrative as returned by getAboutOrganizationContent and participate in section deep-linking via the section URL parameter.
event_handlers: onSectionActivated: When URLSearchParams.section equals the history section key, scroll this section into view and mark it as active.

components.functionality: List the organization’s focus areas using the focusAreas array and optionally allow per-item focusing based on user interaction or future URL parameters (though only section-level parameter is required here).
event_handlers: onSectionActivated: When URLSearchParams.section equals the focus areas key, scroll this section into view and mark it as active.

onToggleItemDetails: If individual focus areas support collapsible detail rendering, toggle their expanded/collapsed state in response to user clicks without persisting state outside the current session.

components.functionality: Display a structured list of major programs using programDescriptions. Enable highlighting of a specific program based on URLSearchParams.programCode and provide navigation to related pages, including a donation call-to-action when the relevant program is present.
event_handlers: onSectionActivated: When URLSearchParams.section equals the programs key, scroll this section into view and mark it as active.
onContentLoaded: After programDescriptions are available, find any program whose programCode matches URLSearchParams.programCode and mark that program item as focused or expanded.

onClickProgramAnchor: When a user selects a program item for emphasis, update the programCode query parameter in the URL to that program’s programCode (e.g., about.html?section=programs&programCode=<code>) and adjust the highlighted item accordingly.

onClickDonateEarlyCareer: If a program in programDescriptions has programCode equal to the donation program code used for early career research grants, render a ‘Support Early Career Research Grants’ control associated with that program. When clicked, navigate to donate.html?programId=early_career_research_grants.
onClickViewConferences: When a user clicks a control intended to lead them to the list of conferences described in this section, navigate to conferences.html.

components.functionality: Present a structured list of leadership entities returned by getAboutOrganizationContent.leadership, including names, roles, and affiliations, without hardcoding specific individuals.
event_handlers: onSectionActivated: When URLSearchParams.section equals the leadership key, scroll this section into view and mark it as active.

components.functionality: Expose membership/community-related actions in the context of the About page content and route users to the membership page.
event_handlers: onClickBecomeMember: When the user clicks the ‘Become a member’ control, navigate to membership.html. This navigation may include optional context parameters if defined at a higher level of the application, but this component’s primary responsibility is to target membership.html as specified.

components.functionality: Provide quick access to related pages from the About context: conferences listing, contact information, and potentially other related navigational links specified by the site architecture.
event_handlers: onClickViewConferences: Navigate to conferences.html when the user activates the conferences link in this panel (this may duplicate the link in the programs section but provides additional entry points).
onClickContactUs: Navigate to contact.html for general inquiries.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly linear, top-to-bottom single-column layout beneath the existing full-width black header (info bar + navbar). All About-page components sit inside one centered content column with no sidebars. The sequence within that column is: data loader (appears only while loading) at the very top of the white area, then the page title (implicit, before the internal navigation), then the about-section-navigation as a slim, centered horizontal text menu, followed by stacked sections in this order: mission-section, history-section, focus-areas-section, programs-overview-section, leadership-section. At the end of the content column, two stacked link panels appear: community-membership-panel first, then contact-and-related-links-panel as the final call-to-action, mirroring the mockup’s end emphasis on email/links.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly linear, top-to-bottom single-column layout beneath the existing full-width black header (info bar + navbar). All About-page components sit inside one centered content column with no sidebars. The sequence within that column is: data loader (appears only while loading) at the very top of the white area, then the page title (implicit, before the internal navigation), then the about-section-navigation as a slim, centered horizontal text menu, followed by stacked sections in this order: mission-section, history-section, focus-areas-section, programs-overview-section, leadership-section. At the end of the content column, two stacked link panels appear: community-membership-panel first, then contact-and-related-links-panel as the final call-to-action, mirroring the mockup’s end emphasis on email/links.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (thin info bar + thick navbar) continues to occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height as a full-width dark band. Below it, the About content sits in a centered column occupying about 50–60% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width around 720–800px), leaving generous left and right white margins. Vertically, only about the middle third of the content area is actively used by text at any given view, with generous spacing between sections. Each section block (mission, history, etc.) gets its own vertical slice with substantial top and bottom margins, so the page feels like a sequence of distinct yet lightly separated text islands floating in a large white canvas.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains intentionally low. Paragraphs are moderately short with comfortable line spacing (roughly 1.5–1.7 line-height) and limited line length. Lists and section headings have generous vertical spacing above and below, echoing the mockup’s numbered list with ample breathing room. There are no multi-column text layouts, no dense tables, and minimal visual decoration. Lists (focus areas, leadership, programs) are vertical and well-spaced, not compacted. Link panels are text-centric, with only one or two key links each, avoiding clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s Z-pattern and then vertical scroll. Users’ eyes first land on the dark global header and logo, scan along the main navigation and social/search icons, then drop sharply into the bright white content region. There, the eye is drawn first to the centered page-level title (About the Organization, implied) and immediately below it the centered about-section-navigation. From that horizontal menu, the flow becomes a simple downward reading path through each section in order: mission, history, focus areas, programs, leadership. The last two panels (membership and contact/related links) form the closing call-to-action area, with contact details and key links visually emphasized (e.g., using a red or accent color for email text), providing a clear terminal point similar to the mockup’s red email address.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: Below the site’s familiar dark full-width header band, the About page opens onto a broad, quiet white background. In the center of this white field sits a relatively narrow, fixed-width content column—about half the viewport width—anchoring all text content. At the very top of this column, where the black header meets the white canvas, a slim, understated loading message appears only while content is being fetched, then collapses away. A centered, typographically strong page title (handled by the surrounding layout) introduces the page, and directly beneath it a minimalist horizontal text menu lets visitors jump to Mission, History, Focus Areas, Programs, or Leadership. This internal navigation is centered, with simple text links spaced evenly across one line, maintaining the academic, text-first character. As users scroll, each section appears as its own quiet block: a centered section heading, a comfortable gap, then left-aligned paragraphs or vertical lists, all set in the same narrow column with generous white space above and below, so that only one or two sections dominate the viewport at a time. The mission and history read as narrative text segments; focus areas, programs, and leadership lists appear as simple, well-spaced vertical lists—no cards, no images—maintaining the conservative aesthetic. Near the bottom of the page, the flow subtly shifts into action: a centered membership panel appears as a lightly framed or shaded rectangle within the column, inviting readers to explore membership or support, followed by a final compact contact-and-related-links panel. This last block emphasizes email and key navigation links, with contact email addresses possibly highlighted in red, echoing the mockup’s treatment of final CTAs. Throughout, the surrounding white margins and lack of visual noise make the page feel calm, deliberate, and academically serious, while still providing clear pointers to related areas of the site.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader occupies the very top of the white content area, directly below the dark global navigation band and aligned with the centered content column. It spans the full width of that column (about 50–60% of the viewport, capped at roughly 720–800px) but is visually modest: typically a single-line status message or a thin loading bar. When active, it sits with a small vertical margin below the header (roughly 24–32px) and a similar margin above the page title area, so it appears as a narrow strip of text in the middle of a large white band. When loading is complete, this component collapses to zero height, allowing the page title and subsequent content to shift upward, preserving the largely empty white space above the content block, as seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline at the top of the content column and collapses completely when not needed. It scrolls normally with the page and does not overlay or fix itself to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-navigation sits directly beneath the page’s main heading within the central content column. It is a single horizontal row of text links (e.g., Mission, History, Focus Areas, Programs, Leadership) centered within the column. The navigation bar itself occupies about 70–80% of the column width, leaving small left and right padding within the column, and appears roughly one and a half line-heights below the title, creating a clear but not crowded relationship. Each link is spaced evenly along the row, with generous internal spacing so that the row feels airy and not menu-dense. A subtle horizontal rule or a faint bottom border running the width of the navigation row may appear below it, further separating this navigation band from the main body content while still staying visually light. Vertically, there is a generous gap (around 40–56px) between this navigation row and the first section (mission-section), reinforcing the mockup-inspired feeling of a large white canvas surrounding a compact central text cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the rest of the content as part of the natural document flow. Each link jumps to the corresponding section via in-page anchors; no sticky behavior is applied to keep the layout simple and consistent with the conservative, non-dynamic mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission-section is the first substantial text block in the content column. It sits centered beneath the about-section-navigation, separated by a generous vertical gap (about 48–64px). The section’s heading is horizontally centered within the column, with a font size slightly smaller than the page title but clearly prominent. Below the centered heading, mission text appears left-aligned for legibility, maintaining a narrow text measure within the column. The body text spans approximately 90–95% of the column width, with small padding from the column edges. Paragraphs are separated by comfortable vertical space, reflecting the low content density of the mockup. The mission-section occupies roughly a quarter to a third of the visible content height when in view, surrounded above and below by ample white space so it feels like a discrete, self-contained block on the otherwise open white page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal block within the single-column flow, stretching in height according to content but always constrained to the centered column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The history-section appears directly below the mission-section in the same centered column, separated by a large vertical gap (around 56–72px) to keep each section visually distinct. Its heading is centered, mirroring the mission-section’s heading style, positioned near the top of the block. Below the heading, history content—often multiple paragraphs or a simple timeline presented as paragraphs or a lightly formatted list—is left-aligned and confined to the same narrow column width. If a timeline format is used, each time period is separated by generous vertical spacing rather than packed together. Overall, the history-section visually reads as another calm text island in the vertical sequence, occupying roughly a similar vertical amount of space as the mission-section but able to extend slightly taller if content demands, always maintaining a clear band of white above and below the block before the next section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level behavior within the single column; no special positioning, expanding vertically as needed with content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The focus-areas-section is placed immediately below the history-section, again separated by a generous top margin (roughly 56–72px). A centered section heading sits at the top of the block, consistent with earlier sections. The list of focus areas itself is laid out as a simple vertical list inside the same narrow column, each item left-aligned and set on its own line or short cluster of lines. List items are spaced with noticeably larger line gaps than standard list defaults, ensuring the list feels airy and easy to scan rather than dense. Bullet or numbered markers, if used, are unobtrusive and aligned flush with the left text edge within the column. The entire section spans the full width of the content column but maintains the same visual rhythm: centered heading, then left-aligned, well-spaced text, surrounded above and below by ample white space before the next section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a normal vertical list within the fixed-width central column, expanding in height with the number of focus areas.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The programs-overview-section follows the focus-areas-section with a similar vertical gap (around 56–72px). It mirrors the overall structure of previous sections: a centered section heading at the top, followed by left-aligned content within the same narrow column. Each program in the list is presented as a vertically stacked item, possibly with the program name in a slightly stronger text style (e.g., bold) followed by a short descriptive sentence or two beneath it. Items are separated by generous vertical spacing (comparable to double paragraph spacing), and if a divider is used, it is a light, thin horizontal rule spanning only 70–80% of the column width and centered, maintaining the subtle, text-centric aesthetic. The section occupies as much vertical space as needed, but due to the comfortable spacing and narrow column, it still presents as a calm sequence of program entries rather than a crowded directory.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the stacked flow; no fixed elements or side-by-side layouts, honoring the single-column, text-first design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The leadership-section appears below the programs-overview-section, separated by a similar large vertical margin (around 56–72px), and serves as the final major content section before calls-to-action. Its heading is centered, consistent with all other sections. The leadership list is left-aligned within the central column and presented as a vertical list of individuals. Each entry may include a name on the first line (set in a slightly stronger style), with role/affiliation on the line below, followed by a modest vertical gap before the next person. The spacing between leaders is intentionally generous, ensuring that the list maintains the low-density, academic feel rather than appearing as a tightly packed directory. The section likely occupies a taller vertical slice due to multiple entries, but still sits within the same narrow column, framed by white space above (separating it from programs) and below (separating it from membership and contact panels).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally as a vertical list within the central column. Height adapts to the number of leadership entries without changing its centered, single-column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The community-membership-panel is the first of two closing panels and is positioned beneath the leadership-section with a slightly more pronounced vertical gap (around 64–80px) to signal a transition from descriptive content to action-oriented content. It appears as a centered, self-contained block within the content column, spanning roughly 80–90% of the column width to create a subtle inset effect. A simple border, faint background tint, or top rule can distinguish this panel from the plain white background, but decoration remains minimal. Inside, text is center-aligned: a brief heading or label such as ‘Membership & Community’ at the top, a short explanatory sentence beneath, and one or two key links (e.g., ‘Learn about membership’, ‘Make a donation’) presented as inline text links or conservative button-like elements. Vertical spacing within the panel is generous, and the panel as a whole occupies only a modest vertical slice, with white space above and below preserving the calm overall feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level panel within the column. It scrolls normally and does not stick to any viewport edge. The panel’s borders or background, if used, span only within the central column, not full-bleed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-and-related-links-panel sits immediately beneath the community-membership-panel with a smaller but still comfortable vertical gap (around 40–56px), forming the final cluster at the bottom of the content column and acting as the ultimate call-to-action, analogous to the red email addresses in the mockup. It is similar in width to the membership panel—about 80–90% of the central column—and may share the same subtle framing (border or light background) for consistency. Inside, content is center-aligned: a concise heading such as ‘Contact & Related Links’ at the top, followed by a short line or two indicating how to reach the organization, with the main email addresses or contact link visually emphasized using the site’s accent color (potentially red, echoing the mockup). Below or alongside the contact info, a small set of links to related pages (Conferences, Donate, Contact page, etc.) appears as a compact vertical or horizontal list with generous spacing. This panel sits with a clear margin above the bottom of the viewport and page footer, leaving a band of empty white space beneath it so that it feels like a deliberate endpoint, not a crowded footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky panel at the bottom of the content flow. It remains aligned to the central column and scrolls out of view normally, providing the last focal point before the footer.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for accessing the organization’s contact details and submitting topic-specific inquiries via a contact form.

core_features: Load and display contact channels (email addresses, phone numbers, mailing addresses, inquiry topics, and response time info) using the getContactInfo interface.
Provide a contact form that binds to the available inquiry topics from getContactInfo and supports submission via submitContactForm.
Preselect the inquiry topic in the form based on the optional URL parameter ?topic=, when it matches a topic code returned by getContactInfo.
Clarify expected response times or support hours by displaying the responseTimeInfo returned by getContactInfo near the form.
Offer alternative contact options by rendering clickable email links, phone numbers, and mailing addresses sourced from getContactInfo.
Guide users to related pages (such as conferences.html, events.html, donate.html, membership.html, index.html) based on their chosen inquiry topic, without hardcoding topic-specific content.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: General inquiry from footer link
1. User clicks the "Contact" link in the site footer and lands on contact.html with no topic URL parameter.
2. On page load, the page parses the URL, detects no ?topic= parameter, and sets the selected topic state to undefined.
3. The page calls getContactInfo.
4. Once getContactInfo resolves, the page populates:
   - A list of contact emails, phone numbers, and mailing addresses.
   - The inquiry topic dropdown options using inquiryTopics.
   - The response time text using responseTimeInfo.
5. The user fills out the contact form fields (name, email, topic, message), selecting a topic from the dropdown.
6. The user submits the form.
7. The page validates that required fields are non-empty and that email is in a valid format.
8. If validation passes, the page calls submitContactForm with the current form values.
9. If submitContactForm returns success=true, the page displays the success message from the API and reveals a "Return to homepage" link to index.html (and enables the logo to navigate there after submission).
10. If submitContactForm returns success=false, the page displays the returned message as an error and keeps the form fields intact for editing.

Workflow 2: Topic-prefilled inquiry (e.g., from conferences.html)
1. User is browsing another page (e.g., conferences.html) and clicks a contextual help link that navigates to contact.html with a topic URL parameter, such as contact.html?topic={topicCodeFromSourcePage}.
2. On page load, contact.html parses the ?topic= parameter and stores it as initialSelectedTopicCode.
3. The page calls getContactInfo.
4. After getContactInfo resolves, the page searches inquiryTopics for an item whose code matches initialSelectedTopicCode.
5. If a matching topic is found, the topic dropdown in the contact form preselects that topic; if not found, the dropdown remains unselected.
6. The user can keep the preselected topic or change it to another topic from the dropdown.
7. The user completes the form and submits it.
8. The page calls submitContactForm with the current topic value (which may match or differ from the URL parameter) alongside name, email, and message.
9. The page displays success or error feedback based on the submitContactForm response, as in Workflow 1.

Workflow 3: Using direct contact details (email/phone/address) instead of the form
1. User lands on contact.html (with or without a topic parameter).
2. On page load, the page calls getContactInfo and renders lists of emails, phone numbers, and mailing addresses.
3. The user scans the list and clicks on a specific email address.
4. The click triggers the default mail application using a mailto link constructed from the selected email address.
5. Alternatively, the user clicks on a phone number to initiate a call via tel: on supported devices, or copies the number/address for external use.
6. The contact form remains available if the user decides to send a structured inquiry instead.

Workflow 4: Handling submission errors and retry
1. User fills in the contact form and submits it.
2. The page enters a submitting state, disables the submit button, and calls submitContactForm.
3. submitContactForm returns success=false with an explanatory message.
4. The page:
   - Displays the returned message in an error panel.
   - Re-enables the submit button.
   - Keeps all current form values so the user does not lose their message.
5. The user edits the form (for example, correcting the email address or changing the topic) and submits again.
6. The page repeats the submission process until submitContactForm returns success=true, after which the success state and navigation options are shown.
interactions:
On page load:
- Parse the current URL for the topic parameter.
- Store topic (if present) as initialSelectedTopicCode.
- Call getContactInfo.
- While waiting for getContactInfo, keep the contact components in a loading state (e.g., disable form and hide topic options).
- Once getContactInfo resolves, populate the contact details and topic options, and attempt to preselect the topic using initialSelectedTopicCode.

Contact topic selection:
- When the user opens the topic dropdown, the options are populated from getContactInfo.inquiryTopics.
- When the user selects a topic option, update the internal selectedTopic state used by the contact form.
- Optionally, update the page URL using history.pushState/replaceState to reflect the new ?topic= value without reloading the page, ensuring that the topic selection is shareable via URL.

Contact form input:
- name field: onChange updates formState.name.
- email field: onChange updates formState.email.
- topic field: onChange updates formState.topic to the selected topic code from inquiryTopics.
- message field: onChange updates formState.message.
- Real-time validation can update a validationState object (e.g., missing fields, invalid email) to enable/disable the submit button.

Contact form submission:
- onSubmit event handler prevents default form submission.
- Validate formState (required fields, basic email pattern).
- If validation fails, prevent the API call and display validation errors.
- If validation passes, set submissionState to "pending" and disable the submit button.
- Call submitContactForm with { name: formState.name, email: formState.email, topic: formState.topic, message: formState.message }.
- When submitContactForm resolves:
  - If success=true, set submissionState to "success", store the returned message as successMessage, and optionally clear form fields.
  - If success=false, set submissionState to "error" and display the returned message as errorMessage while keeping the current form values.

Response time / support-hours display:
- After getContactInfo resolves, display responseTimeInfo in a dedicated informational area near the form.
- If responseTimeInfo is empty or missing, keep the area hidden to avoid implying incorrect or static response times.

Navigation interactions:
- "Return to homepage" link: when clicked (typically after a successful submission), navigate to index.html without additional parameters.
- Site logo: when clicked after successful submission, also navigate to index.html as per outgoing_connections.
- Topic-related guidance links: basic anchor links from the Contact page to related pages such as conferences.html, events.html, donate.html, membership.html, or index.html. These links may include a topic parameter to preserve context (e.g., contact.html?topic=<code>) when returning to Contact in future flows.

Clicking contact details:
- Clicking an email entry triggers a mailto: link constructed from the email address.
- Clicking a phone number triggers a tel: link constructed from the phone number.
- Clicking a mailing address could copy it to clipboard or open a map in a new tab (if configured), but the address content itself is always sourced from getContactInfo.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is derived entirely from URL parameters, in-memory page state, and the results of getContactInfo and submitContactForm.

URL parameter handling:
- The page supports a single URL parameter: topic (string).
- On initial load, the page reads window.location.search and extracts topic.
- initialSelectedTopicCode is set to the extracted topic value or left undefined if the parameter is missing.

Topic resolution logic:
- After getContactInfo resolves, the page searches getContactInfo.inquiryTopics for an item whose code matches initialSelectedTopicCode.
- If a match is found, selectedTopic is initialized to that code and the contact form topic dropdown reflects that selection.
- If no match is found (e.g., unknown topic code), selectedTopic remains undefined and the topic dropdown has no preselected value.
- When the user manually changes the topic, selectedTopic is updated accordingly; the URL may optionally be updated using the History API (e.g., replaceState) to set topic to the new selectedTopic, ensuring shareable state without a full reload.

Loading and error states:
- contactInfoState:
  - status: 'idle' | 'loading' | 'loaded' | 'error'.
  - data: the full object returned by getContactInfo when status='loaded'.
  - errorMessage: populated only when status='error'.
- On page load, contactInfoState.status is set to 'loading' and getContactInfo is called.
- On successful response, contactInfoState.status becomes 'loaded' and data is set to the API result.
- On failure, contactInfoState.status becomes 'error', and the UI may show a generic error message and disable the contact form until retried.

Form and submission state:
- formState: { name, email, topic, message } is initialized to empty strings, except topic which is initialized from selectedTopic once contactInfoState is 'loaded'.
- validationState: tracks field-level validation results and whether the submit button should be enabled.
- submissionState:
  - status: 'idle' | 'pending' | 'success' | 'error'.
  - successMessage: set from submitContactForm.message when status='success'.
  - errorMessage: set from submitContactForm.message when status='error'.
- On form submit, submissionState.status becomes 'pending'; based on submitContactForm's response, it transitions to 'success' or 'error'.

Navigation state:
- After submissionState.status becomes 'success', navigation elements ("Return to homepage" button and logo) are allowed to navigate to index.html, as defined in outgoing_connections.
- No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client-side storage; all state is ephemeral and derived from the current page load, URL parameters, and API responses.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getContactInfo to retrieve emails, phone numbers, mailing addresses, inquiry topics, and response time information, and exposes this data and loading/error states to other components.

Parses the topic URL parameter on load, initializes the selected topic based on inquiryTopics from getContactInfo, and optionally syncs changes in the selected topic back to the URL using the History API without reloading the page.
Displays the list of contact emails, phone numbers, and mailing addresses returned by getContactInfo and provides interactions such as mailto and tel links.
Shows the responseTimeInfo string from getContactInfo to clarify expected response times or support hours, hiding itself if the data is unavailable.
Provides fields for name, email, topic selection, and message; binds topic options to inquiryTopics from getContactInfo; handles validation and triggers submitContactForm on submission.
Validates the contact form input and, if valid, calls submitContactForm with the current form values; updates submissionState based on the API response.
Displays success or error messages after form submission based on submissionState and submitContactForm.message, and controls visibility of post-submission navigation options.

Provides contextual navigation links from the Contact page to related pages (such as conferences.html, events.html, donate.html, membership.html, index.html). Links can be filtered or annotated based on the currently selected topic but do not hardcode topic-specific text.
After a successful contact form submission, enables navigation options such as a "Return to homepage" button and makes the site logo navigate to index.html as specified in outgoing_connections.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing full-width header (thin info bar + thicker navbar), the Contact page uses a single, centered content column with a strictly top-to-bottom flow. The sequence is: page heading (Contact), a concise response-time banner, the contact details list, topic-specific guidance links, and then the main contact form with its feedback panel. Non-visual controllers and loaders are conceptually attached to these visible blocks but do not alter the linear visual arrangement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing full-width header (thin info bar + thicker navbar), the Contact page uses a single, centered content column with a strictly top-to-bottom flow. The sequence is: page heading (Contact), a concise response-time banner, the contact details list, topic-specific guidance links, and then the main contact form with its feedback panel. Non-visual controllers and loaders are conceptually attached to these visible blocks but do not alter the linear visual arrangement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header (info bar + navigation) occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. The remaining 80–85% is a broad white canvas. Within this, a narrow content column (about 50–60% of the page width, max ~720px) is centered horizontally. Each content block (banner, details list, guidance links, form) occupies only a modest vertical slice, with generous vertical spacing between them so the overall impression remains of a large, open white area with a compact cluster of centered information.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains very low: short text snippets and lists with generous line-height, 1.5–2× standard, and large vertical gaps (roughly 2–4 line-heights) between blocks. Lists and paragraphs are not crowded; each section has breathing room above and below. The form fields are stacked in a simple vertical order with plenty of space between inputs, avoiding any multi-column or dense form layouts.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first engages with the dark header band and logo/nav, then drops sharply to the high-contrast white content area where the centered 'Contact' title sits. From there, the flow is a straightforward vertical scan: the response-time banner just below the title, then the contact details list, then the topic guidance links, and finally the form. Email addresses within the contact details are emphasized in red as a clear call-to-action, acting as an end point for users who prefer email and a prelude for those continuing downward to the form.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page sits beneath the site’s existing black header band (info bar plus navigation), which spans the full width of the viewport. Below this, the rest of the screen opens into a bright, unbroken white field. Centered within this is a narrow, fixed-width content column, leaving wide left and right margins that underline the page’s calm, conservative character. At the top of this column, the word “Contact” appears as a centered heading, with ample white space above and below it. Just under the heading, a single line or short sentence describes typical response times, also centered and occupying only about half the page width. Further down, a vertically spaced list of contact details (offices, departments, and email addresses in red) forms the first main content block. Beneath that, still centered, a small cluster of topic guidance links helps users choose the right channel, followed by a simple, vertically stacked contact form. The form fields and submit button sit in the same narrow column with generous spacing, and feedback messages appear directly underneath the button in-line, without disrupting the column. Throughout, empty white space surrounds and separates each block so that the content feels like a compact, orderly island of text in the middle of a large, quiet canvas, faithfully echoing the original mockup’s restrained academic tone.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual background component with no direct presence in the layout. It initializes together with the main content column, conceptually attached to the top of the centered Contact section. It influences what appears in the contact-details-list but does not render any visible element, does not occupy width or height, and does not shift any surrounding components. Any perceived loading state is reflected inside the contact-details-list area, not as a separate UI block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; runs on page load and does not participate in the document flow or consume any layout space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state controller is invisible and has no physical footprint in the main content column. It sits conceptually behind the topic-guidance-links and contact-form, updating their state based on URL parameters. It does not create new lines, spacing, or change the grid; any effect is only in which options are highlighted or preselected within those visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; operates in the background, without occupying space or altering the layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details-list appears in the upper half of the white content area, directly beneath the centered page heading and the response-time banner. It is placed about 20–25% of the viewport height below the bottom of the black header, continuing the design’s generous top spacing. The list is contained within the central content column, spanning approximately 50–60% of the page width (max ~720px) and centered horizontally. The block itself is center-aligned as a whole, with each detail item stacked vertically with ample space—roughly 24–32px—between rows. Labels (e.g., ‘General inquiries’, ‘Conference office’) can be left-aligned within the column, while the email addresses sit on the same line or directly beneath and are rendered in red to mirror the mockup’s highlighted addresses. The entire list occupies perhaps 15–25% of the vertical content region, with generous white space above it (separating it from the heading/banner) and a similar margin below before the next section, making it feel like a calm, clearly defined block of information in the center of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document flow inside the centered column; scrolls naturally with the page and does not fix or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-response-time-banner is a short, text-centric strip positioned immediately below the main 'Contact' heading within the central column. It is center-aligned and spans slightly less than the text column—around 80–90% of the column width—so it feels like a concise secondary line under the title, not as wide or heavy as the main content blocks. Vertically, it sits with modest spacing: roughly one line-height (12–20px) below the heading and 32–40px above the start of the contact-details-list. The banner may use a slightly different text weight or a subtle horizontal rule above or below to distinguish it as an informational note, but it remains understated and text-first, occupying only about one or two lines of text height. It reads as a small, calm cue about response expectations before the user dives into contact options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the normal content flow; scrolls with the page and remains centered with the main column, without sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form is placed below the contact-details-list and the topic-guidance-links, in the same centered content column. There is a substantial vertical gap—roughly 48–72px—between the bottom of the contact-details-list and the form, maintaining the low-density, spacious feel. The form’s width matches the column width (about 50–60% of the page, max ~720px), centered horizontally with wide margins on both sides. All fields are arranged in a single vertical stack: name, email, topic or category selector (which may reflect the current URL-synced topic), message textarea, and finally the submit button. Each field has generous vertical spacing between them (24–32px), and labels sit directly above their inputs to keep the layout linear and text-centric. The submit button is centered within the column and occupies a modest width (e.g., 25–35% of the column) so it remains visually prominent but restrained. Overall, the form occupies roughly the lower third of the main white content area on a typical desktop viewport, surrounded by open space above and below, aligning with the mockup’s compact center cluster of text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Placed in the normal document flow, scrolling with the page. It does not stick or float; its boundaries align with the centered content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a logic/action component with no visible UI. It is conceptually attached to the contact-form’s submit action and operates entirely within that footprint. It does not introduce any additional margins, lines, or elements around the form; any user feedback is expressed through the contact-feedback-panel within the same column, not via a separate region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; occupies no layout space and does not affect the document flow apart from triggering state changes in visual components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-feedback-panel appears directly beneath the contact-form’s submit button within the same centered column, acting as an in-line status area. Initially, it may be hidden with only a small reserved gap (around 8–12px) below the button to prevent layout jumps; when active, it expands into one or two lines of text, still within the column width (50–60% page width, max ~720px). The panel is center-aligned relative to the form content; its text may be left-aligned within the block but visually anchored under the button. Success messages can use a subtle colored accent (e.g., a light green or a simple check icon) and error messages a restrained red, but the panel remains visually light and text-forward, not a heavy banner. Vertically, it sits roughly 16–24px above any additional space at the bottom of the page, so after submission, the eye naturally lands on it as the last element in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands and collapses within the normal flow directly under the form; does not overlay other elements or float, and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-topic-guidance-links block is positioned between the contact-details-list and the contact-form, acting as a bridge that helps users decide whether to email or use the form. It sits in the same centered column with a vertical gap of roughly 32–40px above (from the bottom of the contact-details-list) and 32–48px below (before the contact-form). The links are arranged either as a short horizontal row or a tightly stacked vertical list, but in both cases the entire group is center-aligned within the column. Individual links (e.g., ‘General inquiries’, ‘Sponsorship’, ‘Paper submissions’) are separated by generous horizontal or vertical spacing (16–24px) to maintain the low-density feel. The block itself occupies a shallow vertical band—perhaps only 2–4 line-heights—so that it reads as a concise navigational hint rather than a dense menu, and it does not visually compete with the form or the contact-details-list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Renders inline within the central column, following the normal document flow and scrolling with the rest of the page; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This navigation controller is non-visual and operates after a successful form submission. It may scroll the user back to the top of the form or adjust the URL for bookmarking, but it does not render any visible element or occupy any dedicated space on the page. Any perceived movement in the layout (such as a scroll to the feedback area) occurs by repositioning the viewport over existing components, not by adding new visual blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; does not take up any measurable space. Its only spatial effect is programmatic scrolling or focus changes within the existing layout, without changing the structure or flow.

name: Policies & Terms
title: Policies & Terms
description: Page for viewing all website policies and terms, including usage, privacy, cookies, and program-specific conditions, with deep-linking to individual documents.

core_features: Load all available policy documents using getPolicyDocuments and expose them to other components.
Support deep-linking to a specific policy document via the `doc` URL parameter and automatically focus/scroll to it after data is loaded.
Provide an in-page navigation list of all policy documents so users can quickly jump between them.
Display the full content of a selected policy document with clear headings and internal anchor targets.
Enable users to contact the organization about a specific policy via a 'Contact us about this policy' action that navigates to contact.html with the prescribed parameters.
Allow users to filter or search within the set of policy documents (e.g., by title or content text) on the client side, without additional API calls.
user_workflows:
View all policies (default visit):
1. User opens policies.html without URL parameters (e.g., from footer navigation).
2. On page initialization, the page calls getPolicyDocuments.
3. When documents are returned, the page populates the policy navigation list and the main content area.
4. The first document in the returned array (or another implementation-defined default) is selected and displayed in the viewer.
5. User can scroll through the content or use the navigation list to switch to other documents.

Deep-link to a specific policy (from other pages):
1. User clicks a policies link on another page (e.g., donate.html, membership.html) that navigates to policies.html with a `doc` parameter, such as policies.html?doc=donation_policy.
2. On page initialization, the page reads the `doc` URL parameter and stores it as the target policy code.
3. The page calls getPolicyDocuments.
4. After the documents are loaded, the page looks for a document whose `code` matches the `doc` parameter.
5. If a match is found, that document becomes the selected document: the navigation item is highlighted, the main viewer scrolls to its anchor, and focus is set appropriately (for accessibility).
6. If no matching `code` is found, the page falls back to a default document (e.g., the first in the list) and does not treat the `doc` parameter as active.

Switch between policy documents using in-page navigation:
1. User is on policies.html and sees a list of policy document titles in a navigation component.
2. User clicks on a title in the list.
3. The click event updates the currently selected policy code in page state.
4. The URL query string is updated (e.g., to policies.html?doc=<clicked-code>) using history.replaceState or similar, without reloading the page.
5. The main viewer scrolls to or directly renders the clicked document and updates heading focus.
6. If the user refreshes the browser or shares the URL, the same document will be selected on load via the `doc` parameter.

Search/filter within policies:
1. User types a query term into a search input on the Policies & Terms page.
2. On each change or on submit, the page filters the in-memory list of documents returned by getPolicyDocuments based on the query (e.g., checking title and/or content fields).
3. The navigation list updates to show only documents that match the filter, or highlights matching documents.
4. If the currently selected document is filtered out, the page can either keep it selected (but visually indicate no other matches) or switch selection to the first matching document.
5. Clearing the search restores the full list of documents.
6. (Optional) The search term may remain local to the page session and is not required to be reflected in the URL.

Contact the organization about a policy:
1. User scrolls to the bottom (or designated area) of a policy document where a 'Contact us about this policy' action is available.
2. User clicks this action for the currently viewed document.
3. The page navigates to contact.html with the query parameter topic=other (e.g., contact.html?topic=other).
4. The contact page handles all further interaction; this page does not send any additional data about the policy beyond this navigation.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Event: onInit (page load).
- Behavior: Read the `doc` URL parameter (if present), store it as targetPolicyCode, then call getPolicyDocuments.
- After getPolicyDocuments resolves, populate state with the returned `documents` array, determine the initial selected document based on targetPolicyCode or default logic, and trigger rendering of navigation and viewer components.

Policy navigation click:
- Component: policy-navigation-list.
- Event: onClick on a navigation item representing a single document.
- Behavior: Set currentSelectedPolicyCode to the clicked item’s `code`; update the query string `doc` parameter using history.replaceState; scroll the main viewer to the corresponding document section or re-render the viewer with that document at the top; move keyboard focus to the document header for accessibility.

Search input change/submit:
- Component: policy-search-form.
- Event: onChange (live filtering) or onSubmit (form submission).
- Behavior: Read the current search query string from the input and filter the in-memory `documents` list based on title and/or content; update the navigation list to show only matching documents or visually mark matches; optionally select the first matching document if none is currently selected or if the selected one is no longer in the filtered set.

Deep-link handling after load:
- Component: policy-documents-loader + central page controller.
- Event: onDocumentsLoaded (custom internal event after getPolicyDocuments succeeds).
- Behavior: If a targetPolicyCode from the URL `doc` parameter exists and matches at least one document’s `code`, set that document as selected and scroll/focus accordingly; otherwise, select a default document without changing the URL.

Contact link click within a policy:
- Component: policy-document-viewer.
- Event: onClick on 'Contact us about this policy'.
- Behavior: Navigate the browser to contact.html?topic=other, using a full-page navigation; no extra data or API calls are made from this page.

Anchor/section navigation inside a long policy:
- Component: policy-document-viewer (optional internal table-of-contents per document if structured content is available inside `content`).
- Event: onClick on internal section links within a document.
- Behavior: Scroll the document viewer to the appropriate heading or section within the same document; does not change the `doc` parameter because the policy document itself has not changed.

Error state handling for document load:
- Component: policy-documents-loader.
- Event: onError from getPolicyDocuments.
- Behavior: Display an inline functional error state (e.g., 'Unable to load policies at the moment') with a retry control; on retry click, call getPolicyDocuments again. This state is transient and not encoded in the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the `doc` URL query parameter, which determines which policy document is selected and focused. On every page load, the page controller reads `doc` from the URL before calling getPolicyDocuments. Once documents are loaded, the page tries to match `doc` to a document’s `code` property. If a match is found, that document is selected and shown; if not, a default document (e.g., the first in the loaded array) is selected. When a user manually selects another policy via in-page navigation, the application updates the URL to include the new `doc` value using history.replaceState (e.g., changing policies.html?doc=privacy_policy to policies.html?doc=terms_of_use) without reloading the page. This allows bookmarking and sharing links to specific documents. No long-term state (such as selected document or search query) is persisted via localStorage or similar; the selected document is reconstructed on load solely from the URL `doc` parameter, and search/filter state can remain ephemeral in memory or optionally be cleared on refresh. The page does not modify or introduce any URL parameters other than `doc` for its own state management, and interactions like contacting support navigate away to contact.html with only the prescribed parameter topic=other.

components.functionality: Fetches all available policy documents from the getPolicyDocuments interface on page initialization and exposes the resulting `documents` array to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read the `doc` parameter from the current URL and store it as targetPolicyCode in page-level state; call getPolicyDocuments with no parameters.

onDocumentsLoaded: When getPolicyDocuments resolves successfully, store the returned `documents` array in shared state; emit an internal event or callback so that the navigation and viewer components can render.
onError: If getPolicyDocuments fails, set an error flag and error message in state so that an error/retry UI can be shown; no documents are exposed until a successful retry.
onRetryClick: Re-invoke getPolicyDocuments, clearing any previous error state before the new call.

components.functionality: Displays a navigable list of policy documents and allows users to switch the currently selected policy. Supports filtering based on the search component and deep-link selection based on the URL `doc` parameter.
event_handlers: onDocumentsChanged: When the `documents` array is updated by the loader, rebuild the navigation list items using each document’s `title` and `code`; apply any active filters.

onSelectionFromURL: After initial load, use targetPolicyCode (from the `doc` URL parameter) to mark one navigation item as selected; if no match, select a default document and update pageState.currentSelectedPolicyCode.

onNavItemClick: When a user clicks a navigation item, update pageState.currentSelectedPolicyCode to the clicked document’s `code`; call a page-level function to update the URL `doc` parameter via history.replaceState; signal the viewer component to display the newly selected document and scroll/focus to it.

onFilterUpdate: When the search component updates the filter, recompute which documents are visible in the navigation and update the list; if the currently selected document is not in the filtered set, optionally set the selection to the first visible item.

components.functionality: Provides a text input for users to search across policy documents by title and/or content, and updates the navigation list and selection based on the search query.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize pageState.searchQuery to an empty string; no URL parameter is required for this state.

onChange: Update pageState.searchQuery as the user types; compute a filtered set of document `code`s whose `title` or `content` contain the query text; update pageState.filteredDocumentCodes and notify the policy-navigation-list component.
onSubmit: Prevent default form submission and reuse the same logic as onChange to apply the current search query; keep the user on the same page without reloading.

onClearClick: When a clear action is triggered, reset pageState.searchQuery to empty, restore pageState.filteredDocumentCodes to include all documents, and notify the navigation to show the full list.

components.functionality: Displays the full text of the currently selected policy document and exposes a contextual 'Contact us about this policy' action to navigate to the contact page.
event_handlers: onSelectionChange: When pageState.currentSelectedPolicyCode changes (from navigation or URL updates), find the corresponding document in `documents` by matching the `code` property; render its `title` and `content` in the main content area; scroll to the document’s root and move keyboard focus to the title element for accessibility.

onDocumentsChanged: If the set of `documents` changes (e.g., after a retry), re-evaluate the current selection; if the previously selected code no longer exists, select a default document and update the URL accordingly.

onInternalAnchorClick: When the user clicks an anchor link within the policy content (if present in the `content` string), scroll to the relevant section inside the viewer; do not modify the `doc` URL parameter because the selected document has not changed.

onContactClick: When the user clicks 'Contact us about this policy', navigate to contact.html?topic=other using a standard page navigation; no additional parameters are added and no extra API calls are made.

components.functionality: Manages synchronization between page state (selected policy) and the `doc` URL parameter to support deep-linking and bookmarking.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse the current URL query string to extract `doc`; store the value in pageState.targetPolicyCode for use by the loader and navigation components.

onSelectedPolicyChange: Whenever pageState.currentSelectedPolicyCode changes (due to user navigation or default selection), update the browser’s URL query string to set `doc` to this value using history.replaceState; ensure other existing query parameters, if any, are preserved.

onPopState: If the user uses the browser’s back/forward buttons and the `doc` parameter changes, update pageState.currentSelectedPolicyCode to match the new `doc` value; trigger the navigation and viewer to adjust selection and content to align with the updated URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a fixed-width centered content column; header uses a full-width horizontal grid subdivided into logo, navigation menu, and social/search areas.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header (phone/info bar + dark nav bar). Within the white content area, stack elements in a simple top-to-bottom order: page heading at the top center, a short descriptive intro line, then the policy search form centered, followed by a vertically stacked list of policy navigation links, and finally the policy document viewer occupying the lower portion of the column. Infrastructure components (loader and URL state manager) operate without visible UI, conceptually tied to the navigation list and document viewer.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header (phone/info bar + dark nav bar). Within the white content area, stack elements in a simple top-to-bottom order: page heading at the top center, a short descriptive intro line, then the policy search form centered, followed by a vertically stacked list of policy navigation links, and finally the policy document viewer occupying the lower portion of the column. Infrastructure components (loader and URL state manager) operate without visible UI, conceptually tied to the navigation list and document viewer.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Retain the mockup’s proportions: the combined header area occupies about 15–20% of the viewport height. The main content column is relatively narrow, about 50–65% of the viewport width, centered with wide left/right margins.
